confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py fix_cover() deletes the user's cover with no recovery when no portrait exists anywhere
confirmed.evidence:
CONFIRMED. steam-fetch-metadata.py:143-146 permanently deletes the user's cover with a bare unlink() and no recovery, violating the project hard rule "NEVER rm user data — deletions must MOVE to a recoverable _TMP + RECOVERY.txt".

Code path (steam-fetch-metadata.py):
- fix_cover() L119: existing = covers.glob(stem+".*"); L120-123 returns "ok" ONLY if dims() says portrait/square (w<=h). Otherwise falls through.
- L129-141 tries Steam CDN library_600x900 (2 URLs) then SGDB; on a successful portrait it deletes existing (L137-138, also bare unlink) and atomically replaces (L139 tmp.replace — that part IS atomic).
- L143-146: if no portrait downloaded anywhere AND existing AND not dry -> "for e in existing: e.unlink()" -> "removed". No shutil.move to _TMP, no .bak (the only backup in the script is the gamelist at L217, NOT the covers). grep confirms the only unlink/move calls: L138, L141, L145; no _TMP/RECOVERY/shutil.move/os.rename anywhere.

Reachable on a normal run: fix_cover is called unconditionally for every Steam game (L240); not gated by --no-screenshots/--no-metadata. MEDIA (L38) is the LIVE user-data dir /run/media/deck/1tbDeck/downloaded_media/steam — verified it holds the user's real cover art (e.g. "80s OVERDRIVE.jpg" 93KB, "Bastion.jpg" 51KB, dated Jul 2025+).

Reproduced in /tmp/_madrev sandbox by importing the REAL module, pointing MEDIA at the sandbox, and stubbing download->False (simulating Steam CDN 404 + no SGDB key):
- Landscape cover (900x600): RESULT "removed", AFTER [] — file gone, find shows NO _TMP/.bak/recovery copy.
- Zero-byte/unprobeable cover where dims() returns None (ValueError on "".split("x")): also RESULT "removed", file deleted — so a valid-but-unprobeable cover (e.g. ffprobe 15s timeout at L94, or truncated download) is destroyed too, not just landscape banners.
- Control: a true portrait (600x900) returns "ok" and is KEPT; a 0x0 corrupt file is parsed as square and KEPT — so the destructive path is specifically landscape-or-unprobeable + no replacement.

False-positive checks all fail: it is the live user-data file; path is reachable without flags; the replace at L139 is atomic but the bare unlinks at L138/L145 are not the atomicity issue — the issue is irrecoverable deletion. ffprobe is present (/usr/bin/ffprobe, ffmpeg 2:7.1-5, base-image dep of freerdp/kfilemetadata/qt6-webengine), so "ffprobe missing after SteamOS update" is NOT a realistic amplifier; the dims()->None edge is limited to corrupt/truncated/timeout.

Severity: original "medium" -> upgraded to HIGH. It is confirmed unrecoverable loss of user media on a path hit in normal use, and a direct violation of the project's explicit hard rule. Tempered (not critical): the deleted item is usually a "bad" landscape banner, covers are re-scrapeable, the gamelist itself is backed up, and it is not a crash/security hole. Fix: move to a same-filesystem _TMP with RECOVERY.txt instead of unlink at L138 and L145.

confirmed.title: steam-collection-gen.py rewrites the live steam gamelist.xml with NO ES-DE-running guard
confirmed.description: main() at line 127 calls GAMELIST.write_text(...) at line 171 to fully rewrite ~/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml (GAMELIST defined line 21). A full grep of the file (grep -niE 'pgrep|pidof|running|close|emulationstation') returns ZERO hits — there is no ES-DE-running guard anywhere in the script. This is the direct counterpart of the known steam-fetch-metadata.py / dedup-disc-gamelists.py / skyscraper-apply.py / openbor-gen-gamelist.py findings, but steam-collection-gen.py is NOT on the known list. Aborting.'`), and the gen script's docstring (line 15) tells the user to run it directly, so the missing guard is reachable on the normal path. confirmed.impact: If the user runs steam-collection-gen.py while ES-DE is open (the docstring explicitly tells them to run it), ES-DE rewrites gamelist.xml on its own exit and silently clobbers the freshly generated steam collection — and a crash mid-write leaves a truncated/empty steam gamelist. confirmed.fix: Add the same pgrep('-f','ES-DE|emulationstation') abort guard steam-collection-sync.py:83 already uses at the top of main(), and write atomically (write to GAMELIST.with_suffix('.tmp') then os.replace) to avoid truncation.
confirmed.evidence: steam-collection-gen.py:171 does `GAMELIST.write_text("\n".join(lines)+"\n", ...)` where GAMELIST=line 21 `HOME/"ES-DE"/"gamelists"/"steam"/"gamelist.xml"` — the LIVE ES-DE user-data file (`ls` shows real 48456-byte file, and `pgrep -f 'ES-DE|emulationstation'` returned RUNNING during this review, proving the concurrency window is real). No guard exists: `grep -niE 'pgrep|pidof|running|emulationstation|ES-DE'` over the whole 186-line gen script returns only docstring text + the GAMELIST path constant — zero guard. The sibling steam-collection-sync.py DOES guard (lines 83-86: `if not CHECK and subprocess.run(["pgrep","-f","ES-DE|emulationstation"]...).returncode==0: print("ES-DE is running — close it first (it rewrites the gamelist on exit). Aborting.")`), so the asymmetry is real. The write is non-atomic: a single write_text with no temp+rename, so a crash mid-write truncates the live gamelist in place. Reachable on the documented normal path: gen is a direct CLI entry (lines 184-185) and the sync docstring line 15 explicitly says "To ADD newly-installed curated games, run steam-collection-gen.py". confirmed.title: Quitting MAD from the camera-tuning page orphans the ffmpeg preview and leaves the Sinden lightgun dead
confirmed.evidence: router-config-gui.py:1870-1875 quit() = _gp_cleanup(); _cam_restore_driver(); self.root.destroy() — it NEVER calls _cam_kill_ffmpeg() and NEVER calls _clear(). _cam_kill_ffmpeg() is invoked only inside _clear() (line 1374-1375: `if self._cam_proc or self._cam_driver_paused: self._cam_kill_ffmpeg(); self._cam_restore_driver()`) and in the camera page's own stop/preview/save handlers — none of which run on the quit path. grep for `<Destroy>`/WM_DELETE/killpg/atexit shows no other ffmpeg teardown (atexit at line 83 only writes a log line). ffmpeg is started detached: router-config-gui.py:2455-2460 subprocess.Popen([...ffmpeg... v4l2 -i sinden_cfg.CAM[player] ...], start_new_session=True), and CAM = {1:/dev/video0, 2:/dev/video2} (lib/sinden_cfg.py:21). I empirically confirmed the orphan mechanism in /tmp/_madrev: a child started with start_new_session=True survived its parent's exit (reparented to PPID 1) — `python3 orphan_test.py` then `ps -C sleep` → `2273149  1  2273149 sleep 30`. So quitting (hold Start+Select 650ms → GamepadNav fires on_quit=self.quit at line 632/865) while a preview is LIVE leaves ffmpeg running and holding the camera open. quit() DOES call _cam_restore_driver() (line 1874) which restarts the Sinden driver if it was running (_cam_restore_driver lines 1930-1942 → _sinden_restart → detached `sinden-stop.sh; sleep 1; sinden-start.sh`), so the claim "leaves the lightgun dead" is partially overstated — MAD tries to revive it. BUT the orphaned ffmpeg still pins /dev/videoN; sinden-start.sh/sinden-stop.sh contain no ffmpeg/video reference (grep returned nothing), so the restart does not clear the orphan, and a UVC camera held open by ffmpeg will block LightgunMono.exe from acquiring it → gun non-functional until the orphan is killed. Reachability is specific: the common exits (B/back at line 1854, section switch at line 1273) route through _clear() which DOES kill ffmpeg; only the quit-while-previewing path leaks. Recoverable (kill ffmpeg / reboot). Confirmed real but edge-case + recoverable → downgrade high→medium.

refuter.evidence:
I tried hard to refute this and could not. The gap is real and reachable.

quit() at router-config-gui.py:1870-1875 only calls _gp_cleanup() and _cam_restore_driver(), then self.root.destroy(). It NEVER calls _clear() and NEVER calls _cam_kill_ffmpeg(). Compare _clear() at lines 1374-1376 which is the cleanup path every OTHER leave (back/goto/switch_section/_replace) goes through: `if _cam_proc or _cam_driver_paused: self._cam_kill_ffmpeg(); self._cam_restore_driver()`. So pressing B to leave the page tears down ffmpeg; holding Start+Select to quit MAD does not.

The ffmpeg preview is spawned at _cam_preview (line 2455-2460) with start_new_session=True. I confirmed by execution that start_new_session=True puts the child in its own session/pgid, so when the GUI process exits the child is NOT killed and is reparented to init: ran a surrogate Popen(["sleep",...], start_new_session=True) → child pgid != parent pgid (detached=True), and `ps` showed it `Ss` (session leader) still alive after the parent would exit. ffmpeg (n7.1, /usr/bin/ffmpeg present) is `-f v4l2 ... -update 1` reading a live camera, so it runs indefinitely until killed → orphan holds /dev/video0 or /dev/video2 forever.

quit() reachable from the camera page: the hold-Start+Select quit is a global timer in GamepadNav (lines 627-632, self._quit_after = root.after(QUIT_HOLD_MS, self.on_quit); on_quit = App.quit, wired at line 865). It is not page-scoped, so it fires on the camera-tuning page just like anywhere else.

The "gun left dead" half: quit() does call _cam_restore_driver() (line 1874) which, if the driver was running before preview, fires _sinden_restart() = detached `sinden-stop.sh; sleep 1; sinden-start.sh`. But the camera is an explicit single-opener — comment at line 2330 "Driver holds the camera (UVC single opener), so previewing PAUSES the driver"; CAM = {1:/dev/video0, 2:/dev/video2} (lib/sinden_cfg.py:21) is the SAME device ffmpeg opened. The orphaned ffmpeg is never killed, so when the ~1s-later restart launches LightgunMono.exe it cannot acquire the busy camera. sinden-start.sh only pgreps that the mono PROCESS exists (lines ~82-90), which does not prove it opened the camera — so the "LightgunMono up" check does not save it. Net: orphan ffmpeg + dead lightgun after returning to ES-DE, recoverable for a non-technical user only by pkill/reboot.

No mitigation exists: no <Destroy> binding, no WM_DELETE_WINDOW protocol, no finally around root.mainloop() (main() at 4601-4606 is bare), and the atexit handler (line 83) only writes the fault log — none kill child processes.

Honest caveat keeping this static-confirmed rather than reproduced-by-execution: I confirmed the orphan-survival half by running a detached-Popen surrogate, but the final outcome (restarted driver fails to open the busy camera) is inferred from the code's own single-opener design + standard V4L2/UVC exclusivity; no camera is attached in this review environment to reproduce that last step on-device. Severity stays high (real bug on a plausible normal path: finish tuning, quit MAD; leaves a core feature — the lightgun — dead plus a runaway ffmpeg).

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py rewrites steam/gamelist.xml with NO ES-DE-running guard (its sibling has one)
confirmed.evidence:
CLAIM IS REAL (not a false positive), but severity downgraded high->medium.

Guard truly missing in target: steam-fetch-metadata.py writes the live ES-DE user-data gamelist GL = ~/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml (defined line 37; confirmed live file exists: `ls -la /home/deck/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml` -> 48456 bytes). The write is a non-atomic in-place truncate at line 252: `GL.write_text(new, encoding="utf-8")`. main() (lines 195-255) contains NO pgrep/pidof/ES-DE-running check — confirmed by `grep -rn -iE 'pgrep|pidof|running|/proc' steam-fetch-metadata.py` (no match).

Sibling DOES have the guard (claim verified): `grep` found steam-collection-sync.py:83-86 — `if not CHECK and subprocess.run(["pgrep","-f","ES-DE|emulationstation"], capture_output=True).returncode == 0: print("ES-DE is running — close it first (it rewrites the gamelist on exit). Aborting."); return 1`. That sibling writes the SAME file (steam-collection-sync.py:25 GL = ~/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml). So a direct sibling guards the identical file and the target does not — a real, confirmed inconsistency violating project rule #3.
(Note: steam-fetch-media.py is NOT a relevant sibling — `grep -n gamelist steam-fetch-media.py` returns nothing; it only writes media, no gamelist, so it correctly needs no guard. steam-collection-gen.py:171 also writes the gamelist with no guard, but it fully regenerates from scratch so a clobber is just a re-run.)

WHY DOWNGRADED TO MEDIUM (not high): (1) A timestamped backup is ALWAYS written before any modification on non-dry runs — line 217: `GL.with_suffix(f".xml.bak-{time.strftime('%Y%m%d-%H%M%S')}").write_text(txt)`. So the worst-case ES-DE-exit clobber loses the freshly-fetched metadata (must re-run), but the user's prior gamelist is fully recoverable from the .bak — NO permanent data loss. (2) Reachability is manual-only: `grep -rn steam-fetch-metadata .` and `grep -rn fetch-metadata lib/ mad-backend.py router-config-gui.py` return ZERO callers — it is a hand-run CLI maintenance tool (docstring flags --dry-run/--only), not wired to GUI/backend/daemon, normally run with ES-DE closed. The clobber requires the unusual case of running it while ES-DE is open. Genuine missing-guard defect, but recoverable + manual + edge-triggered = medium, not high. py_compile OK.

confirmed.title: skyscraper-apply.py rewrites per-system gamelist.xml with no ES-DE-running guard
confirmed.evidence: skyscraper-apply.py:3,15 target the LIVE ES-DE user-data file: GLD=~/ES-DE/gamelists, real=os.path.join(GLD,sysn,"gamelist.xml"). Confirmed live: `ls -la /home/deck/ES-DE/gamelists/nes/gamelist.xml` -> 674317 bytes, mtime Jun 9; doc cache deck-docs line 362 states "gamelists/<system>/gamelist.xml ... - "Clobber not guaranteed" — refuted by source + the user's actual setting. es_settings.xml:151 `SaveGamelistsMode value="always"`. SystemData.cpp:2064-2070 `onMetaDataSavePoint()` calls `writeMetaData()` whenever mode=="always"; FileData.cpp:198 calls `onMetaDataSavePoint()` on game launch (lastplayed/playcount). So launching ANY game in that system during the session rewrites the whole gamelist.xml from ES-DE's authoritative in-memory copy (loaded before/independent of the script's disk edit), wiping the script's changes. Destructor path SystemData.cpp:593-595 also writes on exit. GamelistFileParser.cpp:~390 saves via pugixml `doc.save_file(xmlWritePath)` (overwrite, not the script's edits).

Secondary issue (not the basis for severity): the script's own write is non-atomic (open('w') truncate-in-place, no temp+rename), so a crash/power loss mid-write also corrupts the file — though the .bak allows recovery.

Severity high (not critical): the clobber causes silent loss of the just-applied metadata in normal use, which is exactly the high-severity project rule ("never edit gamelist.xml while ES-DE running"); the pre-edit .bak prevents permanent destruction of the pre-existing data, so it is not critical. Reproducing the live clobber needs ES-DE running on-device (GUI) — every link in the mechanism is statically confirmed in the patched fork source.

confirmed.title: quit-combo-watcher self-SIGTERMs and breaks the policy's fast KILL escalation (Eden/multi-app quit_cmd)
confirmed.evidence:
CONFIRMED by execution. Reachability: the watcher is launched by ES-DE game-start hook /home/deck/ES-DE/scripts/game-start/quit-combo-watcher.sh:22-23 as `python3 quit-combo-watcher.py --system "$SYSTEM" --quit-cmd "$QUIT"`, so the watcher's own /proc cmdline literally contains the quit pattern. For the switch/Eden backend the pattern is `Eden|Yuzu|Suyu|Ryujinx` (controller-policy.toml:415: `pkill -TERM -f 'Eden|Yuzu|Suyu|Ryujinx'; sleep 2; pkill -KILL -f 'Eden|Yuzu|Suyu|Ryujinx'`). `grep -E "Eden|Yuzu|Suyu|Ryujinx"` against the watcher's argv string => MATCH, so `pkill -TERM -f` hits the watcher itself.

quit-combo-watcher.py:120 runs `subprocess.run(quit_cmd, shell=True)` SYNCHRONOUSLY in the watcher's own process, with no SIGTERM handler in main(). So the first `pkill -TERM` inside quit_cmd SIGTERMs the watcher mid-run; it never reaches line 121, and the quit_cmd's own `sleep 2; pkill -KILL` fast escalation never executes. Faithful simulation (/tmp/_madrev): watcher.log shows "armed SIGKILL backstop (+6s)" + "about to run quit_cmd synchronously (line 120)" then STOPS — "REACHED line 121" and "after _quit returned" never logged => watcher self-killed. End-to-end (e2e.sh) with a SIGTERM-ignoring fake emulator: emulator survives the TERM and is only killed at t=6s by the detached setsid backstop (KILL_AFTER, line 50/113), NOT by the policy's intended 2s escalation. This is exactly the outcome the policy author tried to avoid — controller-policy.toml:411-414 explicitly says the 2s escalation exists "instead of waiting on the watcher's 6 s global backstop — ES-DE returns promptly." That intent is defeated.

So the title is accurate: watcher self-SIGTERMs and the policy's FAST KILL escalation is broken. NOT a false positive — no guard prevents it.

Impact is BOUNDED (why medium, not high): the detached setsid backstop still SIGKILLs the hung emulator, just ~4s later (6s vs the intended 2s). No data loss, no permanent hang. Also only affects emulators that IGNORE SIGTERM (Eden v0.0.3 per the comment); Yuzu/Suyu/Ryujinx handle TERM and quit on the first pkill regardless. The Hypseus backend is NOT affected: its `[h]ypseus.real` bracket trick (controller-policy.toml:471) means the watcher's literal `[h]ypseus.real` argv does NOT contain `hypseus.real`, so `pkill -f '[h]ypseus.real'` does not match the watcher (verified: grep -E NO MATCH). Original medium rating is well-calibrated; kept at medium.

confirmed.title: Base controller-policy.toml parse is unguarded; a hand-edit typo aborts EVERY game launch
confirmed.evidence: lib/routing.py:48-50 parses the BASE controller-policy.toml UNGUARDED (tomllib.load with no try/except), while lib/routing.py:51-56 wraps ONLY the LOCAL file parse in try/except(TOMLDecodeError,OSError) with the comment "a broken local file must never break routing" — so the human-edited base file (per the lines 26-27 comment, "the (commented, human-edited) defaults") is the unprotected one. Reproduced with the REAL load_policy(): copied the live policy to /tmp, appended a TOML syntax error, monkeypatched routing.POLICY_FILE to the copy, and load_policy() raised `tomllib.TOMLDecodeError: Invalid initial character for a key part (at line 493, column 3)` at lib/routing.py:50, process EXIT CODE 1 (uncaught). Reachability via launch: load_policy() is called by _setup() at controller-router.py:158 with NO try/except up through main()/sys.exit (controller-router.py:578-586). The setup is invoked two ways: (1) controller-router-wrap.sh — the <command> for 112 entries in the live /home/deck/ES-DE/custom_systems/es_systems.xml (verified: `grep -rc controller-router-wrap.sh` = 112) — uses `set -euo pipefail` (line 14) and runs `"$ROUTER" setup ...` (line 31) BEFORE `exec "$@"` (line 34); I reproduced that a non-zero "router" stand-in makes set -e abort before the exec (wrapper exit 1), and the wrapper header (lines 11-13) states ES-DE treats non-zero as a launch failure → these games do NOT launch. (2) the ES-DE game-start HOOK install.sh:101-103 runs the router with `|| echo "...launch continues"` then `exit 0`, so it TOLERATES the crash — unwrapped RetroArch systems are NOT aborted. Therefore the finding is REAL but its title "EVERY game launch" OVERSTATES scope: it aborts the ~112 wrapped commands, not all launches. Severity downgraded-consideration: confirmed medium (not high) — real crash on a broad path, but the trigger requires the user to hand-edit the base TOML and introduce a syntax error (not a normal operation; the GUI writes only to .local.toml precisely to avoid touching the base), it is not data loss, and the author already mitigated the analogous local-file case.

confirmed.title: enumerate_devices() mutates the module-global _ENUM_CACHE with no lock; unsafe under mad-backend's concurrent threads
confirmed.evidence:
CLAIM IS TECHNICALLY TRUE AND REACHABLE — kept at medium (genuine but edge-case + self-healing).

(1) No lock guards _ENUM_CACHE. devices.py:134 declares it module-global; it is read (.get L163), written (_ENUM_CACHE[path]=... L193), popped (L171), and SWEPT by iterating the live dict in a comprehension then deleting (L216-217: `for p in [p for p in _ENUM_CACHE if p not in alive]: del _ENUM_CACHE[p]`). `grep -n Lock lib/devices.py` shows only _SDL_LOCK (L394, guards SDL only) — nothing protects _ENUM_CACHE.

(2) Concurrency is real, not hypothetical. rpc.py:24 `_POOL = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)`; dispatch (rpc.py:82-83) runs every `slow=True` method on the pool. enumerate_devices() is called by multiple slow methods: device_cmds.py:76 devices.sdl(slow), preview_cmds.py:142 preview.route(slow), :156 preview.all(slow), device_cmds.py:159 gamepads.list(slow). It is ALSO called from independent daemon threads: _WatchStream.run() (device_cmds.py:134, 2s poll) -> _scan() -> enumerate_devices(), and the capture Stream's _identify() (capture_cmds.py:157) — both on their own threads (rpc.py:107). So >=2 threads can be inside enumerate_devices() simultaneously.

(3) Proven by execution. /tmp/_madrev/race_threshold.py reproducing the exact get/insert/sweep pattern across 4 threads: tight loop -> 31 RuntimeError('dictionary changed size during iteration') over ~211k walks/3s (~1/6800); realistic cadence (small sleeps, ~33 nodes/walk) -> 0 errors over ~20k walks/3s. So the race fires only under heavy overlap AND when the sweep actually deletes (a device just (un)plugged).

WHY NOT HIGHER: enumerate_devices() writes NO files / no user data (grep shows only /sys,/dev READS) — zero data-loss risk. The RuntimeError is caught everywhere: _run's blanket `except Exception` (rpc.py:64-67) -> one-off EINTERNAL response, NOT a daemon crash; _WatchStream `except Exception: pass` (device_cmds.py:142); capture _identify `except Exception: return None` (capture_cmds.py:159). Worst case = a single failed RPC reply or skipped hotplug poll; self-healing next call. WHY NOT REFUTED/LOWER: it IS a real unlocked-shared-state bug on a reachable multi-thread path, mechanism proven; medium (robustness/edge-case) is the correct rating.

confirmed.title: systems.set_ra_option runs on the worker pool and does an unlocked read-modify-write of shared .cfg files
confirmed.evidence:
CONFIRMED, severity stays medium (calibrated — timing-dependent, single-user, non-destructive).

Root facts:
- lib/madsrv/systems_cmds.py:242 `@method("systems.set_ra_option", slow=True)` → dispatched on the worker pool. rpc.py:24 `_POOL = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)`; rpc.py:82-83 slow methods run via `_POOL.submit`, so up to 4 run concurrently. Comment rpc.py:10-12 confirms slow=True ⇒ worker pool.
- No lock anywhere: `grep -n "Lock\|lock\|threading" lib/retroarch_cfg.py` finds none around set_system_option (only the word in comments); systems_cmds.py imports no threading lock for this method.
- set_system_option (lib/retroarch_cfg.py:317-350) is a read-modify-write: line 325 `existing = target.read_text(...)`, modify in memory, line 346 `_atomic_write(target, new_text)`. _atomic_write (213-217) uses a FIXED temp name `target.with_suffix(suffix + ".router-tmp")` then `tmp.replace(target)` — atomic for ONE writer, NOT concurrency-safe (shared tmp name).
- Client does not serialize: ES-DE fork MadBackend.cpp:387-405 `request()` uses `mNextId++` + `mPending[id]` map (multiple in-flight). GuiMadPageSystems.cpp:253 each RA-option SwitchComponent callback fires an independent `setRaOption`→`systems.set_ra_option` (313-325) with no in-flight guard. _retroarch_running() (systems_cmds.py:53-60) spawns `pgrep` inside each call, widening the window.

Reproduced by execution (copied retroarch_cfg.py to /tmp/_madrev, monkeypatched core_dirs_for_system to one fake dir, two threads hammering two distinct keys on the same n64.cfg):
  EXCEPTIONS: [('video_fullscreen','FileNotFoundError',"... 'n64.cfg.router-tmp' -> 'n64.cfg'")]
  FINAL: has video_fullscreen: False ; has audio_sync: True ; preserved unrelated video_smooth: True
Two real failure modes: (a) FileNotFoundError on tmp.replace from the SHARED fixed temp filename (surfaces as EINTERNAL — set_ra_option has no try/except around the write); (b) lost update — one just-requested toggle silently dropped.

Why medium (not higher): single-user GUI; needs two RA-option toggles for the SAME system within one round-trip (different systems = different files, no race); worst case is a dropped toggle or a transient error flash the user retries — NOT corruption of user-authored content (unrelated keys are preserved, verified). Why not refuted/downgraded: the race is genuinely unguarded, reachable via the unserialized C++ client + independent switch callbacks, and concretely reproduced.

confirmed.title: Standalone backends (cemu/pcsx2/eden/rpcs3/xemu) overwrite live config non-atomically
confirmed.evidence:
All five standalone backends write the live user config non-atomically (truncate-in-place). Confirmed by reading the code: pcsx2_cfg.py:195 `ini.write_text(text, encoding="utf-8")`; eden_cfg.py:187 `ini.write_text(...)`; xemu_cfg.py:110 `path.write_text(...)`; cemu_cfg.py:132 and :203 `_port_path(...).write_text(...)`; rpcs3_cfg.py:183-185 `with ymlp.open("w") ... yaml.safe_dump(data, f, ...)` (worst case: a safe_dump exception mid-stream leaves a half-written yml). Python `Path.write_text` opens in 'w' (truncate) mode then writes — verified via `inspect.getsource(Path.write_text)`: "Open the file in text mode, write to it, and close the file" — so a crash/power-loss between truncate and completed write leaves the file truncated/corrupt.

This is NOT a false positive and it is reachable: controller-router.py:350-368 calls these assign() functions at every ES-DE standalone-game launch (cemu/pcsx2/xemu/eden/rpcs3 backends). The write target IS the live user data file (e.g. ~/.config/PCSX2/inis/PCSX2.ini, rpcs3 Default.yml) — confirmed by the cfg.get("config_file"/"config_dir") defaults.

The defect is real because the SAME codebase establishes an explicit atomic-write norm everywhere else (tmp + os.replace): retroarch_cfg.py:213 `_atomic_write` (docstring line 16 "Writes are atomic (tmp + rename...)"), sinden_cfg.py:128-129, hypinput.py:264-273, es_systems_wrap.py:98-109, localpolicy.py:92. These five backends are the outliers.

Severity stays MEDIUM (not higher, not lower) for honest calibration: (1) the failure is an edge case — a crash/power-loss during a sub-ms write of a small text file, not any normal launch; (2) crucially, ALL five backends take a one-time `.router-backup` BEFORE the first write (pcsx2:184-187, eden:181-184, xemu:104-107, rpcs3:178-181, cemu `_backup_once`), so even a torn write does not destroy the original user config — it is recoverable. So it is a genuine robustness gap inconsistent with the project's own standard, but not data loss on a common path. Fix: route these through a temp+os.replace helper like the other backends already do.

confirmed.title: esde_paths never finds the extracted ES-DE AppDir the wrapper actually runs — silently reads the stale legacy ~/AppDir bundled system set
confirmed.evidence:
The finding's mechanism is REAL and reachable. The live launcher /home/deck/Applications/ES-DE.AppImage is a bash script (file => "Bourne-Again shell script") whose own header says it runs the build "from a PERMANENTLY EXTRACTED AppDir instead of FUSE-mounting the AppImage ... Running the extracted AppRun creates NO FUSE mount." It ends with `APPDIR="$HOME/Applications/ES-DE-MAD.AppDir"` then `exec "$APPDIR/AppRun" "$@"`. So no /tmp/.mount_ES-DE* ever exists during normal use — confirmed: `glob.glob('/tmp/.mount_ES-DE*/usr/share/es-de/resources') => []`.

Executing the resolver live: `esde_resources() => /home/deck/AppDir/usr/share/es-de/resources` and `bundled_es_systems() => /home/deck/AppDir/usr/share/es-de/resources/systems/linux/es_systems.xml` (esde_paths.py:23-36). That legacy ~/AppDir is dated 2026-04-05; the ACTUAL running resources at /home/deck/Applications/ES-DE-MAD.AppDir/usr/share/es-de/resources/systems are dated 2026-06-13 and are NEVER in the resolver's candidate list (lines 29-36 only know /tmp mount, ~/AppDir, /usr/share). grep confirms nothing syncs or writes home-root ~/AppDir except esde_paths reading it; deck-post-update.sh:94 extracts only to ES-DE-MAD.AppDir. So esde_paths reads a frozen snapshot of a different/older build — exactly as the title states.

Severity stays MEDIUM (not higher): the two es_systems.xml are currently byte-identical (diff => IDENTICAL; 195 systems each), so no harm today. Consumers (es_systems_wrap.wrap_system reading bundled <command>/<name>; es_systems.py quit-combo set; router-config-gui.py:1509-1512 cache mtime) read only very stable fields. Divergence requires a future ES-DE update that changes a stock system's command AND the user wrapping that system; even then it only emits a slightly-stale wrapped <command> into the user's CUSTOM file via an atomic write that first takes a .bak (es_systems_wrap.py:98-110,134) — a recoverable correctness glitch, not data loss or a crash. The .is_file() guards make the fallback graceful. Genuine latent bug, bounded impact => medium confirmed.

confirmed.title: supermodel-sinden-smart.py rewrites Supermodel.ini non-atomically with no backup — interrupted launch corrupts the user's config
confirmed.evidence:
supermodel-sinden-smart.py:178-193 reads the whole INI then reopens the SAME path with open(INI,'w') (truncate-in-place) and writelines() — classic non-atomic rewrite. No temp+rename/backup/fsync: grep for tempfile|os.replace|os.rename|.bak|backup|fsync|mkstemp returned "(no matches)".

Reachability confirmed: supermodel-sinden.sh:14 unconditionally invokes the launcher on every game start, and main() (line 250) always calls patch_ini() before os.execvpe (no --info in the launch path). The target is live user data: /home/deck/Emulation/emulators/supermodel-linux-sinden/Config/Supermodel.ini (real file, 8739 bytes).

Reproduced by execution: loaded the real module, redirected its INI constant to a /tmp/_madrev copy of the live file, and ran patch_ini(5,6,[4],[2]). Result: "inode before: 34386 after: 34386 -> SAME inode means truncate-in-place (NOT temp+rename)", size 8739 -> 8687, target lines rewritten in place. Same-inode proves the file is truncated at open('w') before writelines completes, so a kill/power-loss/OOM during that window leaves a truncated/partial config and there is no .bak to restore non-input user settings (resolution, etc.).

Severity kept at MEDIUM (not raised): the corruption window is a narrow millisecond truncate->writelines gap that requires an external interrupt (power loss/OOM/pkill on a battery handheld), not a common-path crash, and the file is largely regenerable. The project rule "config writes should be atomic (temp+rename)" applies and validates it as a legitimate robustness/edge-case finding. Refutation attempts failed: no upstream guard, path is reachable, file is genuinely live user data, and the write is demonstrably non-atomic.

confirmed.title: sinden-update-retroarch-mouseindex.py overwrites global retroarch.cfg non-atomically and with no backup, from a game-start path
confirmed.evidence:
CONFIRMED at medium. The write is non-atomic and backup-less, on a real game-start path, against the codebase's own atomic-write convention.

(1) Non-atomic write — sinden-update-retroarch-mouseindex.py:43 `CFG.write_text(new)`. Python's Path.write_text opens the target in mode 'w', which TRUNCATES the file to 0 bytes BEFORE writing. Proven by execution in /tmp/_madrev: after `open(p,"w")` and before any write, `p.read_text()` returned '' (empty) — the original content was already destroyed at open() time. So a kill / power-loss / disk-full between truncate and full write leaves the live 114KB global retroarch.cfg empty or partial.

(2) No backup taken by the script — `grep -n 'bak|backup|copy2|shutil|os.replace|tempfile|.tmp'` on the script => "NO backup/atomic logic in the script". It writes in place without first copying a .bak.

(3) It IS the live user-data file — CFG = ~/.var/app/org.libretro.RetroArch/config/retroarch/retroarch.cfg; `ls` shows it present at 114565 bytes (the real global RetroArch config governing all settings).

(4) Reachable from a game-start path — ES-DE hook /home/deck/ES-DE/scripts/game-start/sinden.sh:13 launches sinden-start.sh, which at line 98-99 runs this script (`[[ -x $MIDX ]] && "$MIDX"`) on every lightgun-collection ROM launch.

(5) Bypasses the project's own standard — lib/hypinput.py:264, lib/retroarch_cfg.py:213, lib/es_systems_wrap.py:98, lib/localpolicy.py:89, lib/sinden_cfg.py all use atomic tmp+os.replace (hypinput also writes a one-time .bak per CLAUDE.md rule #5). There is even a dedicated lib/retroarch_cfg.py:_atomic_write for RetroArch configs. This script ignores all of them.

WHY MEDIUM, NOT HIGH: two real mitigations keep it off the common-failure path. (a) The `if new != text` guard (line 42) means the truncating write only fires when the mouse index actually CHANGED (device topology shift) — verified: re-applying the existing P1=4/P2=5 values yields `new != text == False`, so no write. (b) The re.sub is surgical and does NOT drop other user keys — a difflib of the transform shows only the two intended lines change (orig 3379 lines / 114565 B -> new 3379 lines / 114566 B). So the only failure mode is corruption during the brief truncate->write window on an interruption (crash/power-loss/ENOSPC), not silent key loss and not deterministic corruption on the happy path. That is a robustness/edge-case data-integrity bug = medium, consistent with the original rating.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py rewrites gamelist.xml with no ES-DE-running guard
confirmed.evidence: steam-fetch-metadata.py:37 GL = HOME/"ES-DE"/"gamelists"/"steam"/"gamelist.xml" resolves to the LIVE user-data file (confirmed: /home/deck/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml exists, 48456 bytes of real data). Line 252 `GL.write_text(new, encoding="utf-8")` rewrites it on the DEFAULT path: `dry` is False unless --dry-run (line 196) and `do_meta` True unless --no-metadata (line 198), so plain `python3 steam-fetch-metadata.py` reaches the write. grep for any guard in the file found NONE: `grep -niE 'pgrep|pidof|...running' steam-fetch-metadata.py` returned only doc/comment hits (lines 3,12,37), no pgrep/pidof. Aborting." — proving the author knows the rule and simply omitted it here. confirmed.title: openbor-gen-gamelist.py overwrites gamelist.xml with no ES-DE-running guard and no backup

confirmed.title: dedup-disc-gamelists.py edits gamelist.xml with no ES-DE-running guard

Write is NON-atomic truncate-in-place: line 41 `open(real,'w',encoding='utf-8').write(new)` (grep for os.replace/os.rename/tempfile/.tmp -> none found); a kill/disk-full mid-write leaves a truncated file. Tool is actively used and durable, not dead code (memory media-metadata-overhaul-2026-06-02.md: "Durable scripts: ~/Emulation/tools/launchers/skyscraper-apply.py"; "PENDING: fba full-scrape apply"). py_compile -> OK (valid, runs). MITIGATIONS that hold severity at medium (not higher): line 24 `shutil.copy(real, real+f".bak-metaall-{TS}")` takes a timestamped backup BEFORE writing, and line 42-43 validates the result as XML and `shutil.copy` restores the .bak on ParseError — so true irreversible data loss is unlikely (a recoverable .bak always exists). The actual harm is the rule-#3 hazard (silent loss of apply work via ES-DE's save-on-exit) plus a non-atomic write window — a real robustness/correctness gap, but cushioned by the backup. It is a manually-invoked CLI tool, not an automatic path. Medium is correctly calibrated; the finding's stated fact (no ES-DE-running guard) is accurate.

confirmed.title: Standalone emulator config writers (cemu/xemu/pcsx2/rpcs3/eden) overwrite the live profile non-atomically
confirmed.evidence: The non-atomic write is real and verified by execution. grep over lib/*cfg*.py shows cemu_cfg.py:132 `_port_path(...).write_text(text,...)` and :203-204 `_port_path(cfg_dir, first).write_text(tpath.read_text(...),...)` are bare truncate-in-place writes; pcsx2_cfg.py:195 `ini.write_text(text,...)`, xemu_cfg.py:110 `path.write_text(text,...)`, eden_cfg.py:187 `ini.write_text(text,...)`, and rpcs3_cfg.py:183 `with ymlp.open("w",...) as f: yaml.safe_dump(data, f, ...)` are all the same pattern (rpcs3 dumps straight into the truncated handle — worst case). By contrast the SAME codebase has an established atomic convention these 5 ignore: sinden_cfg.py:127-129 (tmp.write_text + os.replace), retroarch_cfg.py:213 `_atomic_write`, hypinput.py:264 `_atomic_write` (with backup), es_systems_wrap.py:98 `_atomic_write`. So the writers are the clear outliers vs the project rule "config writes should be atomic (temp+rename)". CONFIRMED but bounded: (1) Reachability is normal — every Wii U/PS2/Xbox/PS3/eden launch hits this — but the corruption window is a single syscall on a tiny file. (2) Execution context refutes the concurrency angle: controller-router.py:294 + _standalone() invoke these as a one-shot hook at ES-DE game-start with the emulator CLOSED — no live emulator and no second writer can race, so corruption needs a power-loss/SIGKILL precisely mid-write. (3) The "drop existing user keys" sub-claim is REFUTED: these are full read-modify-write (xemu_cfg.py:99-102 explicitly keeps non-port keys; pcsx2 _set_section / inifile.set_section / rpcs3 yaml round-trip preserve the rest), so user keys are NOT silently dropped. (4) Data loss is mitigated: every writer takes a one-time `.router-backup` (cemu _backup_once L61-70; pcsx2 L184-187; xemu L104-107; eden L181-184; rpcs3 L178-181) before the first write, and the router regenerates the managed files from templates/policy on every launch, so a truncated managed file self-heals next launch. Net: a genuine atomicity robustness gap across 5 writers (matches the stated project requirement), but not a common-path crash, no key loss, low probability, recoverable. Medium is the correct severity (not high/critical; arguably low given self-healing, but breadth + explicit project rule keep it at medium).

confirmed.title: mad_backup.do_restore copies emulator configs back with no emulator/ES-DE running guard (text says 'close emulators' but does not enforce)
confirmed.evidence: lib/mad_backup.py:102-130 do_restore() copies each snapshot file back onto the live target with shutil.copy2(f, p) (line 113) / shutil.copytree (115) / shutil.copy2(lp, LOCAL) (121), with NO check that the owning emulator is closed. The "Close emulators first if any were open." string is only in the AFTER-the-fact success message (line 130), not an enforced precondition. Targets come from backup_targets() (lib/mad_config.py:181-191) = backends.*.config_dir/config_file + backups.extra_configs; per controller-policy.toml:343-491 these are emulator INPUT configs (Cemu controllerProfiles, PCSX2.ini, eden/yuzu/suyu qt-config.ini, rpcs3 Default.yml, Ryujinx Config.json, etc.) plus the LOCAL policy file — NOT gamelist.xml/es_settings.xml, so the HIGH-severity ES-DE rule does not apply. The codebase itself establishes the expected guard pattern: lib/madsrv/systems_cmds.py:253-254 raises RpcError("EBUSY","Close RetroArch first — it overwrites these on exit.") via _retroarch_running() pgrep (line 53-57), and sinden_cmds.py uses _driver_running(); restore is the outlier with no such guard. Reachable via GUI Restore button (router-config-gui.py:4498-4499) and backup.restore RPC (lib/madsrv/backup_cmds.py:199-201). Write is non-atomic (in-place truncate), confirmed by execution: `python3 -c "shutil.copy2(src,dst); inode unchanged → True; dst content: NEW"` — so a running emulator can clobber the restore on exit or read a partial file. Severity capped at medium: trigger (clicking Restore while an emulator is running) is uncommon, and the outcome is a clobbered/partial restore that is still RECOVERABLE (the snapshot in SNAP_DIR is untouched) — not data loss on a common path.

confirmed.title: Device name interpolated unescaped into a regex replacement template corrupts Cemu controller XML
confirmed.evidence: lib/cemu_cfg.py:131 — `text = _DISPLAY_RE.sub(rf"\g<1>{dev.name}\g<3>", text, count=1)` interpolates dev.name (the raw evdev kernel device name, devices.py:48/181) into a re.sub REPLACEMENT template, where backslashes are special. Executed proof: name=r"x\" -> '<display_name>x\g<3>' (closing </display_name> tag LOST -> corrupt XML); name=r"num\1ref" -> '<display_name>num<display_name>ref</display_name>' (group ref injected); name=r"name\g" -> re.PatternError (uncaught). Reachable on the normal Cemu launch path: controller-router.py:350 cemu_assign -> _write_port_from_template line 131, with an external pad present; line 132 then truncate-writes the live controllerN.xml. Confirmed inconsistency with every sibling writer (sinden_cfg.py:96,125,145; pcsx2_cfg.py:84,92; retroarch_cfg.py:204,299,331,364) which all wrap dynamic values with re.escape(); only this one is unprotected. The companion line 130 (new_uuid = int_'_'+SDL-hex-GUID) is backslash-free, so only line 131 is affected. Mitigations that cap severity (not refute): (1) trigger requires a backslash or \g<N>/\N in a controller NAME — real pad names (DualSense, Wii U Pro, Xbox, 8BitDo) never contain these, only an exotic/user-renamed BT name would, so this is an edge case not a common path; (2) a one-time backup of managed controllerN.xml is taken (cemu_cfg.py:61-70 _backup_once) before the first write, so even a corrupted write is recoverable; (3) the PatternError variant does not crash the launch — the install.sh:112-113 hook runs the router with `|| echo "...launch continues"`, swallowing non-zero/traceback exits. Real bug, correctly rated medium (edge-case robustness, recoverable).

confirmed.title: Cover/media files deleted outright instead of moved to recoverable _TMP
confirmed.evidence:
steam-fetch-metadata.py:118 covers = MEDIA/"covers" where MEDIA=/run/media/deck/1tbDeck/downloaded_media/steam (line 38) — confirmed real user-data dir (ls shows ~real .jpg covers e.g. "Blasphemous 2.jpg"). fix_cover() deletes covers via e.unlink() with NO move-to-_TMP, violating CLAUDE.md rule #5.

Line 143-145 (cv=="removed"): `if existing and not dry: for e in existing: e.unlink(); return "removed"` — deletes the user's existing landscape cover and replaces it with NOTHING. True uncompensated deletion of user-layout data.

confirmed.title: Base controller-policy.toml parsed with no error handling — a typo aborts every game launch
confirmed.evidence:
CONFIRMED but the title overstates it ("every game launch" is wrong). lib/routing.py:46-57 load_policy(): the BASE file read (lines 48-50 `with POLICY_FILE.open("rb") as f: base = tomllib.load(f)`) has NO try/except, while the immediately following LOCAL file branch (lines 51-56) IS wrapped in `try/except (tomllib.TOMLDecodeError, OSError): pass`. The asymmetry confirms it's an oversight, not intent.

Verified the exception propagates end-to-end (no live files touched): simulated in /tmp/_madrev by repointing routing.POLICY_FILE at a bad-syntax toml — `load_policy RAISED: TOMLDecodeError - Expected '=' after a key... (at line 1, column 6)`. tomllib.load against the real /home/deck/.../controller-policy.toml parses OK; a typo raises. No try/except guards load_policy() in _setup (controller-router.py:157-159) or anywhere up to main()→sys.exit(main()) (line 586) — `awk` over _setup lines 157-305 found only a `try` token inside a comment.

Reachability/blast radius: via controller-router-wrap.sh (set -euo pipefail at line 14; unguarded `"$ROUTER" setup ...` at line 31; comment lines 9-13/30 "router exits non-zero → do NOT exec the emulator → ES-DE treats it as a launch failure") a router crash ABORTS the launch. The wrap is used by 112 command entries in the live /home/deck/ES-DE/custom_systems/es_systems.xml (`grep -o controller-router-wrap.sh | wc -l` = 112) — those games would fail to start.

REFUTATION of the "every launch" claim: the unwrapped-RetroArch path /home/deck/ES-DE/scripts/game-start/04-controller-router-setup.sh:21-23 explicitly catches non-zero with `|| echo "...WARN: setup returned non-zero (launch continues)"` then `exit 0`, so those systems are NOT aborted. Also only triggers on a hand-edit typo (the base policy is human-edited config per its own header; the machine-written local file is the one already defended) — not a runtime/data-driven path. Severity stays MEDIUM: real and high-impact for wrapped systems, but requires a manual edit mistake and fails loudly (game won't launch) rather than causing silent data loss. confirmed.title: openbor-fetch-media.py crashes on a manifest missing a DIR= line (unguarded .group)
confirmed.evidence:
openbor-fetch-media.py:72 — `dirn=re.search(r'^DIR=(.*)$', man, re.M).group(1)` calls `.group(1)` directly on the re.search result with no None-guard. If a manifest lacks a `DIR=` line, re.search returns None and `.group(1)` raises AttributeError. The path IS reachable: line 72 is only skipped when the PREFIX basename `isdigit()` returns early (line 71). Many real manifests have a non-digit PREFIX (`prefix_base=prefix`), so they fall through to line 72.

Reproduced the mechanism in /tmp/_madrev: a manifest `PREFIX=/some/path/prefix` (non-digit, no DIR=) → "CRASH at line 72: AttributeError 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'group'"; a manifest with `DIR=SomeGame` → parses OK. (PREFIX search at line 68 is correctly guarded by `if m:`; only the DIR search at line 72 is unguarded.)

Caller analysis confirms blast radius: copy_art() (line 86) calls appid_for() with no try/except, and main() (line 183) loops copy_art with no try/except — so the AttributeError aborts the ENTIRE run, not just the one game.

NOT a false positive, but bounded reachability + low impact keep it at medium, near the medium/low line:
- All 30 current `.openbor` manifests in /run/media/deck/1tbDeck/ROMs/openbor/ contain a `DIR=` line (verified via grep: every file shows `DIR=1`), so it does NOT crash on any live data today — trigger requires a malformed/future manifest missing DIR=.
- This is a read-only media-fetch utility: a crash merely aborts the fetch. No user-data loss, no gamelist.xml/es_settings.xml writes, no corruption. (Note: appid_for also reads `ES-DE/gamelists/openbor/gamelist.xml` in games() but only with ET.parse for reading, not writing.)

confirmed.title: Sinden lightgun is admitted to nav devices but its `_mad_sinden` guard is dead — aiming drives stray menu navigation and the documented trigger-to-quit does nothing
confirmed.description: In GamepadNav._scan a Sinden gun's MOUSE interface is admitted into self.devs: `sinden = (d.info.vendor == 0x16c0 and e.BTN_LEFT in keys and not gamepad)` (line 279), then `d._mad_sinden = sinden` (line 283). The inline comments (lines 277-278 and 621-625) promise two behaviors: (1) HOLDING the gun trigger can quit MAD, and (2) `_handle` reads ONLY its trigger 'not motion/other → no stray nav', gated by `_mad_sinden`. BOTH are unimplemented. A `grep` for `_mad_sinden` over the file returns ONLY lines 277/279/283 — the flag is never read anywhere in `_handle`. Concretely: (a) `_handle`'s EV_ABS branch (lines 665-700) has no `_mad_sinden` check, and `_scan` builds `_dir_thresh` for ABS_X/ABS_Y of EVERY admitted device including the Sinden (lines 297-303; the build only requires `ai.max > ai.min`, true for an absolute lightgun pointer 0..32767). lib/devices.py:112 confirms the Sinden mouse interface carries `BTN_LEFT` + `ABS_X`. So when a connected Sinden gun is aimed, its ABS_X/ABS_Y motion crosses the 35%/65% zone thresholds and fires `_spatial_move`, moving menu focus unexpectedly. (b) The quit logic (lines 626-639) only arms on BTN_START/BTN_SELECT; BTN_LEFT (the gun trigger, 0x110) matches no branch in `_handle` (it is not in 0x130-0x13f, not START/SELECT, not in `_dpad`, and the val==1 face-button block tests only BTN_SOUTH/EAST/TL/TR/TL2/TR2), so the trigger is a complete no-op — the documented hold-to-quit-via-trigger does not exist.
confirmed.impact: With a Sinden lightgun powered on while the MAD panel is open (a likely scenario — MAD is the lightgun control panel and the camera/calibration pages assume a live gun), pointing the gun at the screen randomly moves the focus ring, making the panel hard to drive. The documented gun-trigger quit silently does nothing (user-facing dead feature). The gun is also needlessly opened/polled by the nav loop.
confirmed.fix: Either (a) actually honor `_mad_sinden` in `_handle`: skip the EV_ABS directional/trigger branches for `getattr(dev, '_mad_sinden', False)` devices (and don't populate `_dir_thresh`/`_trig_thresh` for them in `_scan`), and add a BTN_LEFT hold-to-quit timer for sinden devices if that behavior is still wanted; or (b) drop the dead comments and stop admitting the Sinden mouse interface at all (line 279-280) since nothing reads its events. The current state contradicts its own design comments.
confirmed.evidence: Core claim CONFIRMED on live hardware. (1) `_mad_sinden` is dead: `grep -rn _mad_sinden /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/` returns ONLY router-config-gui.py:277 (comment), 279, 283 (set) — never read. (2) Live Sinden devices are connected; reading capabilities (no grab) of the "SindenLightgun Mouse" interfaces /dev/input/event10 and event20: vendor=0x16c0, BTN_LEFT present, NOT gamepad (no 0x130-0x13f) -> `_scan` admits sinden=True (line 279-283). (3) DECISIVE: those mouse interfaces report ABS_X/ABS_Y min=0 max=32767 and have NO REL axes, so `_scan`'s `ai.max > ai.min` test (line 299) is True for BOTH axes -> `_dir_thresh[path]` is populated for ABS_X/ABS_Y. (4) Reachability: `_poll` (router-config-gui.py:708) iterates self.devs (which now includes the gun mouse), reads each event via read_one() (711) and dispatches to `self._handle(x, now, d)` (729); the EV_ABS branch (683-700) processes the gun's absolute aim motion -> crosses 35%/65% zone thresholds -> calls `_spatial_move`, moving menu focus. There is NO `_mad_sinden` guard anywhere in this path. (5) Trigger no-op CONFIRMED by trace: BTN_LEFT=0x110 matches no branch in `_handle` (not 0x130-0x13f capture range, not BTN_START/SELECT=0x13b/0x13a, not the BTN_DPAD set 0x220-0x223, not the face-button block BTN_SOUTH/EAST/TL/TR/TL2/TR2) -> complete no-op. CALIBRATION: sub-claim (b) "documented hold-to-quit does not exist" overstates a contradiction — the newer in-code comment at lines 621-625 says the trigger-quit was DELIBERATELY removed ("The gun trigger no longer quits either ... it collides with testing gun buttons"), so the dead trigger is intentional and only the stale comment at 277-278 is wrong (a doc nit, not a bug). The load-bearing impact — stray menu navigation when a live Sinden gun is aimed while the MAD panel is open, a normal scenario since MAD is the lightgun control panel — is fully real and reachable. Severity medium is correct: UX degradation, not data loss/crash/security.

confirmed.title: Camera preview never polls ffmpeg: if it dies on its own the feed freezes-but-claims-live and leaves a zombie
confirmed.description: _cam_preview() launches ffmpeg into self._cam_proc and schedules _cam_tick() every 66ms. _cam_tick's only liveness guard is `if not self._cam_proc:` (line 2480) — it tests the truthiness of the retained Popen OBJECT, never calls self._cam_proc.poll(). So if ffmpeg exits on its own — which is a documented, plausible path here: the page comment at line 2330 notes the camera is a 'UVC single opener' that the driver holds, and _sinden_pause_driver only does `sinden-stop.sh; sleep 0.5` (lines 1956 / sinden-stop.sh:6) before _cam_preview opens the device, so a not-yet-released fd, a busy/unplugged camera, or any ffmpeg error makes ffmpeg fail to open /dev/video* and exit immediately — then: (1) the tick loop keeps re-arming itself forever showing the last (stale or blank) frame; (2) the status label keeps saying 'Preview Pn live'; (3) the dead child is never reaped. I confirmed empirically (/tmp test) that a Popen object stays truthy after its child exits, the child sits in state Z (zombie), and only .poll()/.wait() reaps it. Because self._cam_proc keeps a live reference, the object is NOT in subprocess._active, so CPython's opportunistic subprocess._cleanup() (which DOES reap dropped Popens) will NOT reap it — it persists as a zombie until _cam_kill_ffmpeg runs (only on stop / Save / page-leave). This is distinct from wave-1 [4.0] (quitting MAD orphans a *running* ffmpeg) and [4.2] (the stderr fd leak on line 2459): this is the *self-exited* ffmpeg case, which is both a stale/lying UI and an un-reaped zombie for the rest of the page visit.
confirmed.impact: On a common-enough failure (camera still held by the just-stopped driver, camera busy/unplugged, ffmpeg error) the tuning page shows a frozen frame while telling the user it is 'live' — they tune sliders against a dead feed and can't tell. A zombie ffmpeg lingers until they leave the page. No crash, but a confusing dead-feed that looks like a hung preview.
confirmed.fix: In _cam_tick, before re-arming, detect ffmpeg death: `if self._cam_proc.poll() is not None: self._cam_kill_ffmpeg(); self._cam_lbl.config(image='', text='( camera busy / preview ended — retry )'); self._cam_status.config(text='Preview ended (camera busy?). Press Preview to retry.'); return`. poll() also reaps the child, eliminating the zombie. (Optionally cap retries before showing the busy hint.)
confirmed.evidence: CODE: router-config-gui.py:2478-2488 _cam_tick guards only `if not self._cam_proc:` (line 2480) — truthiness of the retained Popen object; it never calls self._cam_proc.poll(). The only poll/wait in the cam path is `p.wait(timeout=2)` at _cam_kill_ffmpeg:2403, which runs only on stop/Save/page-leave (callers: _cam_preview start 2444, _cam_stop_preview 2429, _cam_save 2515, _clear 1375). `grep -n '\.poll()\|\.wait(\|_cam_proc'` shows NO poll() anywhere and wait() only at 2403/4474 — confirming the per-tick loop never detects a self-exited child. So on ffmpeg self-exit: (1) tick re-arms forever via self.root.after(66,...) at 2488 showing a stale/blank frame, (2) status stays "Preview P{player} live" (set at 2464, never updated by the tick), (3) the child is un-reaped. REACHABILITY: _sinden_pause_driver (1949-1960) runs sinden-stop.sh then _cam_preview immediately Popen-opens the UVC device; sinden-stop.sh only does `pkill LightgunMono.exe; sleep 0.5` (lines 5-6) before the fd may still be held — page comment at 2330 documents the camera as a 'UVC single opener' the driver holds — so an ffmpeg fast-exit (device busy/not-yet-released/unplugged) is a plausible, not contrived, path. EMPIRICAL (/tmp): `python3` with subprocess.Popen(["true"]) → after child exit: `bool(p) after child exited: True`, `p.returncode (no poll): None`, `child state in /proc: Z`, `poll() -> 0`. Confirms the Popen stays truthy, returncode None without poll, and the child is a zombie until poll()/wait(); since self._cam_proc holds a live ref, it is not in subprocess._active so opportunistic _cleanup() cannot reap it. NUANCE (finding is accurate, not overstated): the zombie is bounded to the page visit — _clear:1374 tests `_cam_proc` truthiness and calls _cam_kill_ffmpeg whose wait() reaps it on leave; the finding states this correctly. SEVERITY: no data loss/crash/security → not critical/high; real but failure-path-only, impact is a lying "live" UI + one transient (page-bounded) zombie → medium confirmed.

confirmed.title: supermodel-sinden.sh: launcher/emulator exit code is swallowed by the `tee` pipe — ES-DE always sees success
confirmed.description: The whole launch is `{ ...; supermodel-sinden-smart.py "$@" 2>&1; } 2>&1 | tee "$LOG"`. The script is `#!/bin/sh` with no `set -o pipefail` and does not capture `$PIPESTATUS`/`${PIPESTATUS[0]}`. The exit status of the pipeline is the exit status of the LAST command, `tee`, which is essentially always 0. I reproduced the pattern on a /tmp copy: a failing command piped to `tee` yields script exit 0. So if supermodel-sinden-smart.py fails (Supermodel.ini not found, smart launcher crash, emulator missing) the script still exits 0, and ES-DE treats the launch as successful. Contrast hypseus-pin.sh, which deliberately does `"$HB" "$@" 2>&1 | tee "$HRUNLOG"` then `exit "${PIPESTATUS[0]}"` — the correct pattern that this script omits.
confirmed.impact: A failed Supermodel (Sinden fork) launch is invisible to ES-DE; the user gets no error feedback and the game-end hooks/controller-router cleanup run as if a game had actually played. Masks real failures and makes diagnosis harder.
confirmed.fix: Switch the shebang to bash (or add `set -o pipefail` if the shell supports it) and end with `exit "${PIPESTATUS[0]}"` (or in POSIX sh, capture the inner status via a fifo / `{ cmd; echo $? >status; } | tee`), mirroring hypseus-pin.sh.
confirmed.evidence:
Mechanism confirmed in /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/supermodel-sinden.sh:11-15 — it is `#!/bin/sh` (line 1), with the entire launch as `{ echo...; supermodel-sinden-smart.py "$@" 2>&1; } 2>&1 | tee "$LOG"`. No `set -o pipefail` and no `exit "${PIPESTATUS[0]}"` after it, so the script's exit = tee's exit = 0. Reproduced on /tmp copies: inner `exit 42` -> wrapper exit 0; `os.execvpe('/tmp/_madrev/NOPE_supermodel',...)` (FileNotFoundError, simulating missing/broken supermodel binary) -> wrapper exit 0 ("WRAPPER EXIT CODE ES-DE WOULD SEE: 0"); `python3 -c "raise SystemExit(3)"` (simulating patch_ini crash) -> exit 0.

Impact confirmed against ES-DE: es-app/src/FileData.cpp:2212-2219 — after `returnValue = launchGameUnix(...)` (line 2206), it does `if (returnValue != 0) { ...; window->queueInfoPopup(_("ERROR LAUNCHING GAME '%s' (ERROR CODE %i)"), ...); }`. launchGameUnix (PlatformUtil.cpp:232-261) derives that value from `pclose(commandPipe) >> 8`, i.e. the wrapper's exit status. So a swallowed 0 suppresses the only failure popup the user would see.

Reachability confirmed: this script IS the live emulator command — /home/deck/ES-DE/custom_systems/es_systems.xml:54 launches `controller-router-wrap.sh naomi ... -- ".../supermodel-sinden.sh" ...`, and controller-router-wrap.sh:34 does `exec "$@"`, so supermodel-sinden.sh's exit code is exactly what ES-DE reads. Failure modes are real: supermodel-sinden-smart.py:256 `os.execvpe(f"{SM_DIR}/supermodel", ...)` (missing/broken binary -> raises), patch_ini crash, or supermodel itself exiting non-zero — all swallowed.

Contrast confirmed: hypseus-pin.sh:83-84 uses the correct pattern (`"$HB" "$@" 2>&1 | tee "$HRUNLOG"` then `exit "${PIPESTATUS[0]}"`, and it is `#!/usr/bin/env bash` so PIPESTATUS is available). openbor.sh propagates via `exit $?` (no tee). supermodel-sinden.sh copied the tee-to-log idea but omitted the exit-code capture. Note (not a defect in the finding): the inner command is intentionally NOT exec'd and goes through tee, which correctly keeps ES-DE's stdout pipe blocking until supermodel exits — the bug is solely the lost exit code, exactly as scoped. Severity medium upheld: masks failures / hinders diagnosis; not data loss, crash, or security.

confirmed.title: Headless X-Arcade tester escape combo (P1+P2 Start) keys off calibration-driven spot state, not raw BTN_START — breaks under user calibration and is not suppressed during calibration
confirmed.description: XArcadeTesterStream's on-cabinet escape (`both = self.spots.get("mouse1") and self.spots.get("mouse2")`, lines 826-827) is driven by the SPOT state, not the raw BTN_START key. spots['mouse1']/['mouse2'] are set by P1/P2 BTN_START ONLY through the *default* mapping in `_spot_for` (lines 673-674); but `_event` (line 722) lets `self.cal.get(f"{tag}:k{ev.code}")` override that mapping. So (a) if the user calibrates P1/P2 Start onto any other spot, pressing Start sets that other spot and the escape becomes UNREACHABLE from Start; and (b) if the user calibrates some OTHER input onto mouse1/mouse2, that input — not Start — now drives the escape. The equivalent Tk tester (lib/mad_xarcade_tester.py:729) checks the RAW key `f"{od['tag']}:k{e.BTN_START}" in self._xa_pressed`, which is immune to calibration; the headless port lost that robustness. Additionally, the Tk version suppresses the combo during calibration (`if not self._xat_cal: self._xa_quit_check()`, mad_xarcade_tester.py:718-719) but the headless loop runs the escape check unconditionally every tick — so pressing both Start buttons WHILE calibrating (e.g. to bind them) can hold-3s-end the test, and since calibration is only persisted on the explicit `save` action (tester_cmds.py:1081-1086), any binds made that session are silently lost.
confirmed.impact: On the ES-DE-native panel (which input-LOCKs nav during the test via input.lock {nav:True}, line 806, so the escape gesture is the intended way out), a user who has calibrated their X-Arcade can find the documented 'hold P1+P2 Start 3s' escape doesn't work, or fires off the wrong buttons; and a mid-calibration accidental Start+Start ends the test and discards unsaved bindings. ■ Stop with the Deck pad is still available as a fallback, so not a hard lock-out.
confirmed.fix: Track raw BTN_START per gamepad node (mirror the Tk version: maintain a set of pressed `{tag}:k{BTN_START}` keys directly from the event stream) and compute `both` from that rather than from the calibratable spot dict; and gate the escape check on `self._cal_armed is None`/not-calibrating so the combo can't end the test mid-calibration.
confirmed.evidence:
All structural claims verified against the actual code; behavior needs the X-Arcade cabinet to reproduce live (static-confirmed, not executed).

(1) Headless escape keys off CALIBRATION-driven spot state, not raw BTN_START — tester_cmds.py:826-827 `both = self.spots.get("mouse1") and self.spots.get("mouse2")`. spots is set by set_spot (line 404-407) keyed by SPOT name. In _event (line 722): `spot = self.cal.get(f"{tag}:k{ev.code}") or (... self._spot_for(tag, ev.code))`. _spot_for maps BTN_START→mouse1/mouse2 (line 673-674), but self.cal (loaded from xarcade-calib.json, line 658) short-circuits the `or`. So (a) calibrating P1/P2 Start onto another spot makes Start set that spot, never mouse1/mouse2 → escape unreachable from Start; (b) calibrating any input onto mouse1/mouse2 makes THAT input drive the escape. Both confirmed.

(2) The Tk original is calibration-immune and guards calibration — mad_xarcade_tester.py:729 `held = sum(... f"{od['tag']}:k{e.BTN_START}" in self._xa_pressed)` checks the RAW key dict, not the calibrated spot; and line 718-719 `if not getattr(self,'_xat_cal',False): self._xa_quit_check()` suppresses the escape during calibration. The headless run() loop (tester_cmds.py:810-839) runs the escape check EVERY tick with NO `_cal_armed`/calibration guard. Confirmed regression.

(3) Mid-calibration Start+Start discards unsaved binds. Native panel (ES-DE C++ fork) GuiMadPageXArcade.cpp:338-339 `toggleCalibrate()` calls startTest() — calibration runs while the cab is grabbed and nav is locked (tester_cmds.py:805-806 `event("input.lock", {"locked":True,"nav":True})`). Binds are persisted to xarcade-calib.json ONLY by the `save` action (tester_cmds.py:1081-1083), which the C++ sends solely when the user toggles calibrate OFF (GuiMadPageXArcade.cpp:321-325). On `ended=escape` the C++ only sets footer text (lines 256-263), no save. So a stray 3s P1+P2 Start during calibration ends the test (lines 833-836) and silently drops that session's binds. Confirmed.

Severity held at MEDIUM (not downgraded): both paths are real but edge — sub-claim (a) needs a deliberate non-default remap of Start; sub-claim (b) needs an unlucky mid-calibration double-Start. The escape is the documented/intended exit (module docstring line 9; footer "Hold P1+P2 Start 3s to end") and nav is locked, but the Deck-pad STOP TEST fallback always works (no hard lockout, as the finding states). Lost data is unsaved session calibration state, not an existing user file — so not critical/high. Not downgraded to low because it is a genuine correctness+robustness regression vs. the Tk original (two deliberate guards dropped) on a feature (X-Arcade calibration) that is fully wired and reachable in the live native panel (GuiMadPageXArcade.cpp:133 CALIBRATE button, :472-480 arm, :313-345 toggle).

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py crashes on any non-Steam launcher .sh (unguarded regex .group)
confirmed.evidence:
Mechanism is real but severity/framing is overstated; DOWNGRADED high→low.

steam-fetch-metadata.py:205-208:
  for sh in glob.glob(str(ROMS / "*.sh")):
      rg = int(re.search(r"rungameid/(\d+)", Path(sh).read_text()).group(1))
re.search returns None on no-match, so .group(1) raises AttributeError, uncaught (no try/except around the loop — grep -n "try:|except" shows the nearest try blocks are at lines 55-112 inside helper funcs and at 258 __main__ just calls main()). Reproduced in /tmp/_madrev: a .sh lacking rungameid/ yields "CRASH: AttributeError - 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'group'".

WHY NOT HIGH / refute the "any non-Steam launcher" framing:
- ROMS = ~/ROMs/steam (realpath /run/media/deck/1tbDeck/ROMs/steam), the ES-DE `steam` system dir. The glob only ever sees files IN this dir — never "any launcher". Non-Steam games live in their own system dirs and are never scanned here, so the title's premise (iterating non-Steam launchers) is misframed.
- Live data: all 79 .sh files contain rungameid/ — `grep -l "rungameid/" ~/ROMs/steam/*.sh | wc -l` = 79, and the "NO MATCH" loop printed nothing. Each is the standard template `exec steam steam://rungameid/<id>`. So the common path does NOT crash.
- No data-loss risk: the crash at line 206 occurs BEFORE the gamelist backup (line 217) and any write (line 247) — nothing is truncated or clobbered.

Real residual bug: a single malformed/partial/stray .sh in ~/ROMs/steam (e.g. one Steam couldn't resolve, or a sync leftover) aborts the entire run with a traceback instead of skipping that file. Genuine but edge-case robustness gap with no data risk and off the common path → low.

refuter.evidence:
The crash is real and reproducible. steam-fetch-metadata.py:206 chains `.group(1)` directly onto `re.search(r"rungameid/(\d+)", Path(sh).read_text())` with no None-guard, inside an unguarded `for sh in glob.glob(...)` loop in main() (lines 205-208). Sandbox repro (/tmp/_madrev/steam): a `.sh` lacking rungameid gives `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'group'`; the loop has no try/except (greps at 55-112 are all inside appdetails/network helpers, not the loop), and no wrapper calls this script, so the traceback aborts the tool.

KEY POINT — this is a confirmed-but-OVER-RATED finding; I downgrade high -> medium:
1) Misleading title/reachability. ROMS = ~/ROMs/steam (realpath /run/media/deck/1tbDeck/ROMs/steam), a folder generated SOLELY by steam-collection-gen.py whose SH_TEMPLATE (steam-collection-gen.py:120-124) always writes `exec steam steam://rungameid/{rgid}`; even "nonsteam" shortcuts get a numeric rungameid (nonsteam_rungameids() line 110). So in the normal documented workflow EVERY .sh has rungameid. Verified on live data: scanned all 79 .sh in ~/ROMs/steam — zero missing `rungameid/[0-9]+` (loop printed no MISSING lines). The crash only fires if a stray/malformed .sh is dropped into the generator-owned folder — an edge case, not "any non-Steam launcher" and not a common path.
2) No data loss / no corruption. The crash at line 206 happens BEFORE the .bak backup (line 217) and BEFORE the gamelist write (line 252), so the gamelist is never touched — failure is a clean abort with an opaque traceback, nothing more.
3) It IS a genuine bug (not trusted-input by design): the two sibling scripts iterating the same files guard correctly — steam-fetch-media.py:206-209 (`m = re.search(...); if not m: continue; rgid = int(m.group(1))`) and steam-collection-sync.py:91-94 (`m = re.search(...); if not m: continue`). metadata is simply missing that guard. So: confirmed real, but edge-case reachability + no data loss => medium, not high.

confirmed.title: load_merged()'s hand-rolled 2-level merge diverges from the router's recursive deep_merge at 3-level tables (templates / per-system pins)
confirmed.evidence:
The divergence is REAL but UNREACHABLE on current data/paths, so the medium rating is overstated -> downgraded to low.

CODE: lib/policy.py:24-32 load_merged() hand-rolls a 2-level merge: at level 2 it does `base[k][kk].update(vv)` (shallow), whereas lib/routing.py:35-43 deep_merge() recurses at every depth. So a level-3 base sub-dict gets REPLACED wholesale by load_merged but MERGED by deep_merge.

DEPTH PROOF (python3 over the live files): controller-policy.toml has dict-nesting depth 5; `backends` reaches depth 4 and contains exactly one depth-3 dict table reachable by a local override: backends.cemu.templates = {"057e:0330":..., "054c:0ce6":...}. All other local-writable depth-3 tables have NO base counterpart: `base systems.<s>.pins present in: []`; `slot_profiles` exists only in the local file; quit_combo per-system value `buttons` is a LIST (replaced wholesale by both). So only backends.cemu.templates can diverge.

SYNTHETIC DIVERGENCE (proves the bug is real): partially overriding templates -> deep_merge {'057e:0330':'CHANGED','054c:0ce6':'DS P1'} vs hand_merge {'057e:0330':'CHANGED'} (base key dropped). "SAME? False".

WHY UNREACHABLE: the only writers of templates (lib/madsrv/policy_cmds.py:195-205 `_set_backend_template` and router-config-gui.py:4089-4097 `_set_template`) both do `tmpl = dict(merged...get("templates",{})); tmpl[cls]=profile; data[...]["templates"]=tmpl` — i.e. they round-trip the FULL merged dict, so the local override is always a superset of base's template keys and the shallow .update() drops nothing. Verified by running the REAL writer against copies in /tmp/_madrev_10: "After real set_template writer, equal? True", "base key 057e:0330 survived?: True".

NO DATA-LOSS PATH: the live router uses deep_merge/load_policy (lib/routing.py), not load_merged; load_merged feeds only read-only GUI/backend consumers (e.g. lib/cemu_cfg.py:158 `dict(cfg.get("templates",{}))` copies and never persists). So a hypothetical divergence would at worst yield a wrong runtime value, never clobber user data.

Net: genuine latent two-implementations divergence worth fixing (call deep_merge), but produces identical results on all current reachable paths -> low, not medium.

confirmed.title: User calibration/position/P2 JSON files are written non-atomically (rule violation; truncation on crash)
confirmed.evidence:
CONFIRMED mechanically, but DOWNGRADED medium->low. tester_cmds.py:228-230 `_write_json` does `path.write_text(json.dumps(...))`. Path.write_text opens mode 'w' (O_TRUNC) and writes in place — NOT atomic. Verified via `python3 -c "import inspect; print(inspect.getsource(Path.write_text))"` -> "Open the file in text mode, write to it, and close the file" (no temp+rename). A crash/power-loss/disk-full between truncate and full write leaves the file empty/truncated. This violates the project's atomic-write rule. The codebase otherwise uses tmp+os.replace EVERYWHERE: sinden_cfg.py:127-129, es_systems_wrap.py:107-109, retroarch_cfg.py:213, localpolicy.py:89-92, hypinput.py:264-273, model2_cmds.py:154, and even THIS SAME FILE at lines 882-884 (tester slot file, comment "Atomic write") — so _write_json is the lone non-atomic outlier.

REACHABILITY (real, normal use): callers are the MAD GUI save handlers — gamepads.positions_save (294), gamepads.set_p2 (302), xarcade.positions_save (368), calibrate save (1083,1085). Targets are LIVE user-data files confirmed present: `ls /home/deck/Emulation/storage/control-panel/` shows gp-dualshock4-positions.json, gp-p2-units.json, gp-classic-positions.json, gp-*-calib.json etc. (CONTROL_PANEL = ~/Emulation/storage/control-panel, line 27).

WHY LOW not MEDIUM (skeptical calibration): (1) files are tiny (<1KB; live files 21-959 bytes) so the truncate/write window is sub-millisecond — corruption requires a crash precisely in that window. (2) Data is regenerable: user re-runs calibration, and _read_json (217-225) swallows any JSON error returning {}, so a corrupt file degrades GRACEFULLY to _baked_positions defaults (262,270) rather than crashing — no hard data loss, no crash on a common path. (3) Single-threaded JSON-RPC, one tester at a time (_active guard line 186/374) — no concurrency truncation. So it is a genuine rule violation worth fixing for consistency, but practical blast radius is an edge-case robustness issue, not a normal-use bug. No false-positive escape found (the write really is truncate-in-place and really targets live user files), hence confirmed — only the severity is overrated.

confirmed.title: reset_local() deletes the user's GUI-overrides file with unlink() — no recoverable _TMP move (project never-delete rule)
confirmed.evidence:
FACTUALLY TRUE but OVER-RATED — downgrade medium→low. lib/mad_backup.py:155-159 reset_local() does `if LOCAL.is_file(): LOCAL.unlink()` — a hard delete with no _TMP move / no RECOVERY.txt. LOCAL = controller-policy.local.toml (lib/policy.py:16), a real live user-data file (`ls`: 5784 bytes, 145 lines, modified Jun 12 22:57; contains GUI overrides incl. per-system controller-port priority orderings + esde_splash). Reachable: GUI "Reset overrides" button (router-config-gui.py:4504-4505,4595) and JSON-RPC policy.reset_local (lib/madsrv/policy_cmds.py:228-230) + backup.reset_local (lib/madsrv/backup_cmds.py:204-206). Reproduced in /tmp/_madrev: copied the live file to sample.toml, ran the exact unlink logic — file gone, nothing left behind (no _TMP/RECOVERY). So the strict never-delete rule IS technically violated.

WHY DOWNGRADE TO LOW (the medium framing as a data-loss bug does not hold up):
1. This is the USER'S EXPLICITLY REQUESTED action, not accidental clobbering. Button label "↺ Reset overrides", RPC named reset_local, docstring "Delete the GUI overrides file (revert to documented defaults)". The _TMP rule targets unexpected/collateral deletion of user data, not a deliberately-labeled Reset.
2. The file documents itself as disposable — its own header (written by lib/localpolicy.py:74-75): "# Auto-generated by router-config-gui ... Safe to delete to reset." It is an OVERRIDES LAYER on controller-policy.toml defaults; deleting cleanly reverts to a known-good documented baseline, not corruption.
3. Recovery path exists in the SAME UI panel: "💾 Backup" (do_backup snapshots LOCAL, mad_backup.py:97-98) sits beside "Reset overrides", with "Restore" (do_restore, mad_backup.py:118-123).
Residual valid nit: codebase elsewhere is careful (localpolicy.dump is atomic temp+rename, lib/localpolicy.py:88-92), so an intentional reset would be more consistent if it moved to _TMP+RECOVERY first, since overrides are real manual work. That is a low-severity convention/robustness nit, not a medium data-loss bug.

confirmed.title: wire-bezels.py writes per-game bezel configs into non-installed RetroArch cores (operator-precedence bug)
confirmed.evidence:
The operator-precedence bug at wire-bezels.py:66 is REAL. Line 66: `if c not in detected and (CFG/c).exists() or c in fallback and c not in detected:`. Python binds `and` tighter than `or`, so this parses as `(c not in detected and (CFG/c).exists()) or (c in fallback and c not in detected)`. Inside `for c in fallback` (line 65), `c in fallback` is ALWAYS True, so the second disjunct reduces to `c not in detected`, which subsumes the first — the whole condition is just `c not in detected`, and the `(CFG/c).exists()` guard is DEAD. This contradicts the stated intent in the line-64 comment ("then any fallback dirs that exist").

Reproduced by execution (python3 simulation): with detected=['Snes9x - Current'] and only that dir existing on disk, the buggy line yields cores=['Snes9x - Current','bsnes','Snes9x','Snes9x 2010'] while the intended `c not in detected and exists(c)` yields only ['Snes9x - Current']. So uninstalled fallback cores ARE appended.

Consequence is real but minor: the write loops (wire-bezels.py:97-98 and 102-103) iterate `cores` and do `(CFG/c).mkdir(parents=True,exist_ok=True)` then `write_text`, so phantom config dirs + inert bezel cfgs get created for non-installed cores. NOT data loss / not a clobber: line 89 skips any rom whose `stem.cfg` already exists in any core (preserves user per-game cfgs); line 105 `if tgt.exists(): continue` protects existing content-dir overrides. RetroArch never reads a config dir for a core it doesn't launch, so the phantom files are inert.

Reachability is limited: APPLY gate requires `--apply` (line 18); default is dry-run (line 111). `grep -rln wire-bezels` over launchers + esde-build shows the script is referenced only by itself — no systemd unit, no cron, no caller; it is a hand-run tool. On the user's actual machine `ls .../config/retroarch/config` plus a python check showed EVERY fallback core in SYS already has a config dir, so the bug currently produces NO observable difference here.

Verdict: bug confirmed, but the medium (9.0) rating overstates impact — worst case is a handful of inert/empty config files in unused core dirs, no data loss, no clobber, no crash, manual+dry-run-by-default. Downgraded to low.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py permanently deletes existing cover when no portrait can be fetched (network-outage data loss)
confirmed.evidence:
The deletion exists and is reachable, but the finding's "network-outage data loss" framing is overstated; downgrading medium->low.

CODE (steam-fetch-metadata.py:142-146): `if existing and not dry: for e in existing: e.unlink(); return "removed"`. This is a HARD delete (also at line 138), NOT a move to _TMP — `grep -n '_TMP\|RECOVERY\|trash\|shutil.move' steam-fetch-metadata.py` finds only the gamelist .bak at line 217, nothing for cover images. So rule #5 (move-to-_TMP, no rm) is technically violated. MEDIA/covers is real user data on the SD card (`ls /run/media/deck/1tbDeck/downloaded_media/steam/covers/` shows ~real .jpg files).

REFUTATION of the broad claim ("permanently deletes existing cover"): I simulated the fix_cover decision logic in /tmp/_madrev — a genuine PORTRAIT or SQUARE cover (d[0] <= d[1]) returns "ok" at line 123 BEFORE any download is attempted and is NEVER deleted. Only a LANDSCAPE cover (w>h) — or one whose `dims()` returns None (corrupt/ffprobe-fail) — falls through to the deletion. Deleting a landscape cover when Steam has no portrait is the script's DOCUMENTED, INTENDED behavior (docstring lines 10-12: "if Steam has none, delete the bad landscape cover so ES-DE doesn't render it sideways") — not a bug.

REFUTATION of "network-outage": fix_cover is the sole caller's last step (line 240) and is only reached after `appdetails()` SUCCEEDS — `process()` returns early at line 236 (`if not data: return block`) when the store API call fails. appdetails uses store.steampowered.com (line 57) while portraits use shared.akamai.steamstatic.com / steamcdn-a.akamaihd.net (lines 44-45) and SGDB uses steamgriddb.com — different hosts. So a TOTAL outage never reaches the deletion at all; it requires a PARTIAL/transient condition (store API up, image CDNs down).

NET: real but narrow. On the narrow partial-CDN-outage path, a landscape (bad) or unreadable cover is deleted with no _TMP recovery. The lost data is re-fetchable downloaded artwork (next successful run re-downloads the portrait via fix_cover), not irreplaceable user-authored data.
