Edge cases: a `dims()`-None (corrupt) cover, or a user's deliberately-placed wide custom cover, could be nuked on a transient hiccup. Genuine robustness/rule-5 gap, but not the data-loss landmine the medium rating implies → low.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py rewrites the gamelist via a single non-atomic write_text — a crash mid-write truncates the live gamelist
confirmed.evidence: steam-fetch-metadata.py:252 `GL.write_text(new, encoding="utf-8")` writes the LIVE ES-DE gamelist (GL = ~/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml, line 37) via Path.write_text, which opens mode="w" => truncates-in-place, NOT atomic (no temp+rename; grep for os.replace/os.rename/tempfile/.tmp returned nothing). So the mechanism in the finding is REAL and reachable: a crash/kill between truncate and the final buffered write would leave a truncated/corrupt gamelist. HOWEVER the finding omits the mitigation that downgrades it: lines 215-217 write a timestamped backup of the ORIGINAL before any mutation — `txt = GL.read_text(...)`; `GL.with_suffix(f".xml.bak-{time.strftime('%Y%m%d-%H%M%S')}").write_text(txt, ...)`. Verified the backup filename is a distinct sibling (with_suffix('.xml.bak-...') != GL) and that this works in practice: `ls /home/deck/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/` shows the live gamelist.xml plus ~18 preserved gamelist.xml.bak-<timestamp> files (e.g. bak-20260611-175606, 47850B, written immediately before the 48456B gamelist.xml). So the original is recoverable; this is not true data loss. The crash window is also a single ~48KB write syscall in a manually-invoked CLI tool (not the daemon, not a hot path), making the truncation window very narrow. NET: real non-atomic-write robustness gap (project rules do call for temp+rename), but the "truncates the live gamelist" data-loss framing is overstated given the proven pre-write backup => severity medium -> low. Separately, GL.write_text only runs when `not dry and do_meta` (line 251), confirming reachability on the normal (non-dry) path.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-media.py place() unlinks the existing artwork BEFORE copying the new one (non-atomic, no recovery)
confirmed.evidence:
place() at steam-fetch-media.py:93-97 does unlink-then-copy (non-atomic) — technically TRUE: `for other in (MEDIA/sub).glob(glob.escape(stem)+".*"): other.unlink()` then `shutil.copy2(src_path, dst)`. Reproduced in /tmp sandbox: old Game.jpg removed, new Game.png written, NEW content present. BUT the medium rating is overstated and I downgrade to low:

(1) No risk to user/irreplaceable data. The unlinked file lives in MEDIA=/run/media/deck/1tbDeck/downloaded_media/steam (line 43) — ES-DE scraped/downloaded ART that THIS script produces. Docstring line 17: "Overwrites art so prior mis-scrapes are corrected." deck-backup.sh:13/23 treats downloaded_media as a regenerable LARGE separate archive, not user-authored data. The project "_TMP / RECOVERY.txt" rule targets irreplaceable user data; a fetch cache regenerated by re-running `steam-fetch-media.py` does not qualify.

(2) src can NEVER be the unlinked dst — different trees. src=local_sources(rgid) resolves to ~/.steam/steam/appcache/librarycache/<appid>/ (lines 79-82) or ~/.steam/steam/userdata/*/config/grid (line 42); dst is downloaded_media/steam. Sandbox confirmed source.png intact after place(). So the unlink cannot destroy the source.

(3) The glob+unlink is intentional, not careless: old art may have a different extension than the new one (jpg→png), so it must clear all-extension variants to avoid ES-DE matching a stale Game.jpg. The same pattern is used deliberately in cdn_download (lines 112-114) and sgdb_download (lines 133-135).

Worst case: a copy2 failure mid-run loses one game's previously-fetched art for one media kind — fully recoverable by re-running the script. Real but minor robustness nit, hence low, not medium.

confirmed.title: controller-router-wrap.sh silently succeeds (launches nothing) when the emulator command after '--' is empty
confirmed.evidence: controller-router-wrap.sh:18-34 guards arg count (`$# -lt 5`, line 19) and the literal `--` (line 24), but NOT 'non-empty after --'. Invoked as `wrap.sh SYS ROM NAME FULLNAME --` (nothing after `--`): $#=5 passes line 19; $1=='--' passes line 24; after `shift` $#=0; then `exec "$@"` (line 34) with no args. Verified in bash: `set --; exec "$@"` is a no-op returning rc=0 (does NOT replace the process, does NOT error even under `set -e`) — output 'after exec rc=0 / subshell rc=0'. So the router setup runs, exec is a no-op, script exits 0, ES-DE sees a successful launch but nothing launches. Real behavior. Severity LOW: this is reachable only via a malformed es_systems.xml <command> (empty emulator) — an authoring error, not a normal-use path; the system-authored commands always carry a real emulator after `--`.

confirmed.title: quit-combo button list from policy/env is int()-converted with no guard; a bad value crashes the watcher (combo quit disabled)
confirmed.evidence: quit-combo-watcher.py:75-82. The try/except (tomllib.TOMLDecodeError, OSError) at lines 68-74 wraps ONLY the tomllib.load loop; the int() conversions at lines 79 and 82 (`set(int(b) for b in buttons)`) sit OUTSIDE any try. _read_quit_combo is called from main() at line 131 with no surrounding guard, so a non-int-coercible `buttons` value raises an uncaught ValueError → watcher process exits → combo-quit silently disabled. Verified: `int('abc')` raises ValueError. Severity LOW: the two real write paths both pre-coerce to int — policy.set_quit_combo (lib/madsrv/policy_cmds.py:141 `sorted(int(b) for b in params['buttons'])`) and the QUIT_COMBO_BUTTONS env override (line 79 `int(x)`). A bad value requires hand-editing controller-policy.toml. Edge-case robustness only.

confirmed.title: devices.watch can latch a stale token if the watch stream thread dies, preventing restart
confirmed.evidence: device_cmds.py:147-156 — _devices_watch returns {'already': True} whenever _WATCH['token'] is truthy. The stream's lifecycle teardown in rpc.py:114-126 (_guarded) runs cleanup() and pops self.token from _STREAMS but NEVER clears _WATCH['token']; only devices.unwatch (device_cmds.py:159-163) resets it. So if the watch thread terminates without an explicit unwatch, the token latches and watch can never restart. BUT reachability is near-zero: _WatchStream.run (device_cmds.py:132-144) wraps both risky ops (evdev.list_devices() and self.emit()) in try/except Exception (continue/pass); the only unguarded lines are self.stopped.wait(2.0), frozenset comparison, and an assignment — none of which raise in practice. So the latch bug is real in code but effectively unreachable in normal operation. Low is correctly calibrated.

confirmed.title: Required request fields are accessed via params[...] producing EINTERNAL instead of a clean EINVAL
confirmed.evidence: backends_cmds.py:93 (params['backend']), :208-209 (params['backend'], int(params['slot'])), :281 (params['name']) use direct subscription. A missing key raises KeyError, which rpc.py:64-67 catches in the generic 'except Exception' and returns {'code':'EINTERNAL','message':"KeyError: 'backend'"} instead of the clean EINVAL pattern used elsewhere in the SAME functions (e.g. backends_cmds.py:97, :211, :280 raise RpcError('EINVAL', ...)). Real inconsistency, but these are internal JSON-RPC methods invoked only by the trusted MAD GUI which always supplies the required params; a missing param is a GUI bug and the only consequence is an uglier error code/message in the response. No data loss, no crash (the daemon survives via _run's catch-all). Low is generous; info is also defensible.

confirmed.title: P2 auto-detection matches a bare "2" token in any controller name
confirmed.evidence: tester_cmds.py:276-278 — tokens=(name).lower().replace('#',' ').split(); auto=any(t in ('p2','ii','2','player2') for t in tokens). Reproduced in python: names with a STANDALONE '2' model token false-positive as Player 2: '8BitDo Pro 2'->True, '8BitDo SN30 Pro 2'->True, 'PlayStation 2 Controller'->True, while substring cases like 'ps2' do not (it splits to tokens). Genuine but purely cosmetic default: line 279 is result['p2']=bool(overrides.get(uniq, auto)), so the per-unit override in gp-p2-units.json ALWAYS wins and the user can correct it in the GUI. The 'auto' is only the initial default. Low is well-calibrated.

confirmed.title: Stickless pads (FC30) emit phantom lstick/rstick tokens in the snapshot
confirmed.evidence: tester_cmds.py:468-477 — _update_sticks unconditionally iterates ('lstick',ABS_X,ABS_Y),('rstick',ABS_RX,ABS_RY) and calls self.set_stick(stick,...) for ALL pads. For a stickless pad (FC30) ABS_X/ABS_Y ARE the d-pad (line 478-479 even comments 'stickless pads (FC30) ride ABS_X/Y instead'), and _event (line 528-530) routes those axes through _update_sticks, so set_stick('lstick',...) fires and 'lstick'/'rstick' keys land in self.sticks (set_stick at :409-412). snapshot() (:391-393) then includes them. So the phantom tokens DO exist in the wire data. Impact is cosmetic: the FC30 sprite set has no lstick_*/rstick_* stems (line 479 derives 'stickless' from absence of lstick_ stems), so the GUI has nothing to render the token onto — a harmless phantom in the data, not a visible artifact. Confirmed; low (info-leaning) is appropriate. Medium confidence on rendered impact since the GUI/JS frontend rendering of an unmatched stick key was not inspected.

confirmed.title: X-Arcade on-cabinet escape combo breaks if Start is re-calibrated
confirmed.evidence: tester_cmds.py:827 escape combo checks self.spots.get('mouse1') and self.spots.get('mouse2'). _spot_for (:673-674) maps BTN_START->mouse1(P1)/mouse2(P2), but _event (:722) resolves spot = self.cal.get(f'{tag}:k{ev.code}') OR self._spot_for(...), i.e. calibration takes precedence. tester.calibrate 'arm' (:1066-1077) lets the user bind the NEXT input to ANY armed spot, and _cal_capture (:692-693) writes cal[input_key]=spot. So if the user arms a non-Start spot (e.g. 'p1_b1') and presses Start, cal['P1:k<BTN_START>'] redirects Start AWAY from mouse1/mouse2, and the on-cabinet P1+P2-Start escape can no longer fire. Real but self-inflicted, contrived edge case (deliberate mis-calibration of Start). Crucially NOT a soft-lock: tester.stop (:1051-1057) is an RPC the host/GUI can always call to end the test and unlock (cleanup at :844-847 emits input.lock false). Low (info-leaning) is correct. Note: the line-826 comment 'P1+P2 Start' vs code key names mouse1/mouse2 is consistent (Start maps to mouse1/mouse2 by default).

confirmed.title: Camera-preview ffmpeg stderr log file descriptor is leaked on every preview start
confirmed.evidence: router-config-gui.py:2455-2460 opens an anonymous file object inline as stderr=open(logdir/'sinden-preview.log','ab') for the ffmpeg Popen. _cam_kill_ffmpeg (router-config-gui.py:2398-2409) only calls p.terminate()/p.kill() and sets self._cam_proc=None; it never closes that stderr file object, and the file is not stored anywhere to be closed later. Each _cam_preview start (line 2438) leaks one fd until GC. Real but tiny leak in a transient GUI; low is correct.

confirmed.title: Cemu display_name/uuid substitution can corrupt XML or crash on device names with regex-replacement metachars
confirmed.evidence: cemu_cfg.py:131 does _DISPLAY_RE.sub(rf'\g<1>{dev.name}\g<3>', text, count=1) — dev.name goes into the replacement string unescaped. Reproduced: name 'Pad \\9' raises re.error 'invalid group reference 9'; 'Pad \\1\\g<2>' silently produces corrupt XML '<display_name>Pad <display_name>OLD</display_name>'. The call path cemu_assign<-_standalone (controller-router.py:350, 291) has NO try/except, so a re.error would propagate. The uuid sub at cemu_cfg.py:130 uses only trusted {sdl_index}_{guid} (hex), so it is safe. Requires an unusual evdev device name with a backslash, so low (robustness) is right; fix = re.escape the replacement or use a literal-replace callback.

confirmed.title: Eden _apply_player emits a duplicate \default line when the existing key has no value line
confirmed.evidence: eden_cfg.py:75-97 _apply_player: the '\default' branch (lines 86-87) emits 'base\default=false' but never adds base to `seen` (only the value branch at 89-90 does). Reproduced: input body 'player_1_button_a\default=true' (orphan \default, no paired value line) -> output has TWO 'player_1_button_a\default=false' lines plus the value. The normal Qt-paired case ('\default' line + value line, either order) works correctly (one of each). So this only fires on a malformed/hand-edited qt-config.ini; edge case, low is correct.

confirmed.title: rpcs3 assign() does not guard yaml.safe_load against a corrupt/partial Default.yml
confirmed.evidence: rpcs3_cfg.py:166-167 does `with ymlp.open(...) as f: data = yaml.safe_load(f) or {}` with no try/except; a corrupt/partial Default.yml raises yaml.YAMLError, which propagates uncaught through rpcs3_assign<-_standalone (controller-router.py:368, 291 — no try/except). BUT the exception happens before the write (line 183) so the existing yml is NOT corrupted, and the one-time .router-backup is taken at 178-181, so no data is lost. It only aborts that one standalone launch's routing. Low is correct (crash on a rare corrupt-config path, no data loss).

confirmed.title: deck-backup.sh auto-prunes old config archives with `rm` — deletes user backup data without a recoverable _TMP move
confirmed.evidence: deck-backup.sh:296-300 does `ls -t "$DEST"/deck-config-*.tar.gz | tail -n +$((KEEP+1)) | xargs -r rm -v` (KEEP=BACKUP_RETENTION_COUNT default 5), hard-deleting old archives without a _TMP move. Reproduced the selection logic: 7 sample archives, KEEP=5 -> the 2 oldest are selected for rm. However these are the TOOL'S OWN rolling config-snapshot backups (deck-config-<ts>.tar.gz per line 21/244), not primary/irreplaceable user data — live configs still exist and the comment+env-var document the rotation. This is standard logrotate-style retention; it tensions with the rule's letter but is not a primary-data-loss bug. Low (borderline info).

confirmed.title: es_collections lru_cache is process-lifetime but mad-backend is a long-lived daemon — stale collection list across a panel session
confirmed.evidence: es_collections.py:49,75,95,110 use @functools.lru_cache (process-lifetime). They are consumed by the long-lived backend via backends_cmds.py:245,262 and preview_cmds.py:57. BUT mad-backend's lifetime == one panel session: main loop ends on stdin EOF / SIGTERM (mad-backend.py:141, header lines 10-11), the panel runs inside ES-DE, and the backend only READS enabled_collections() to display a list (it never writes collection .cfg files, confirmed in backends_cmds.py:240-267). For staleness to bite, the user would have to add/remove an ES-DE custom collection mid-panel-session without reopening the panel. Real but very rare robustness nit; low is defensible (arguably info).

confirmed.title: do_restore merges directory targets (dirs_exist_ok=True) instead of replacing — resurrects files the user deleted since the snapshot
confirmed.evidence: lib/mad_backup.py:115 `shutil.copytree(d, p, dirs_exist_ok=True)` does merge dir restore. backup_targets() (lib/mad_config.py:181-191) yields dir targets like cemu config_dir (~/.config/Cemu/controllerProfiles). A profile .xml the user deleted from Cemu after a snapshot WILL be re-created on restore (merge, not replace). Confirmed as behavior, but severity is correctly low: a merge restore is ADDITIVE and non-destructive — it never deletes or loses user data (it overwrites changed files with the snapshot version, which is the point of restore, and resurrects removed files). It does NOT violate the 'never rm/overwrite user data' rule. Arguably the safer default vs a destructive replace; the only surprise is resurrected files. Edge-case robustness, not a real-use bug.

confirmed.title: deck-restore.sh extracts the config archive over $HOME with no check that ES-DE is not running
confirmed.evidence: deck-restore.sh:57-68 verifies the archive then `tar -xzpf "$CONFIG_TB" -C /` over $HOME with NO check that ES-DE is running. deck-backup.sh confirms the config archive includes ~/ES-DE (lines 6, 113, 155, 189) which contains es_settings.xml and gamelists/gamelist.xml. So restoring while ES-DE runs would, per the project rule, get clobbered when ES-DE rewrites those files on exit — the restore silently fails to stick (no data loss, just lost effect). Real gap matching the documented hazard. Severity correctly low: the script's designed use case (header lines 8-9, 14: 'new Deck or differently-named SD card') is a fresh machine where ES-DE is not running; running a full restore from inside a live ES-DE session is an unusual user action, and the worst case is the restore not taking effect, not corruption.

confirmed.title: daphne.bind / _dp_bind_press leak proc on communicate() timeout without reaping
confirmed.evidence: lib/madsrv/daphne_cmds.py:190-204. On `communicate(timeout=14)` (line 193) raising TimeoutExpired, the except block (198-203) calls `proc.kill()` but never `proc.wait()`/`communicate()` again, so the child is left unreaped. I reproduced this: after `communicate(timeout=1)` raises and `proc.kill()` without wait(), `/proc/<pid>/stat` shows state Z (zombie) and `poll()` returns None; only an explicit `wait()` reaps it (poll -> -9). Per Python docs you must wait() after kill() following a timeout. Real bug. Severity correctly low: the capture subprocess has its OWN `--timeout 10` (passed at line 183; hypseus_capture.py default/arg), self-exits well before the 14s parent timeout, so the leak only triggers if the SDL capture hangs >14s. The parent mad-backend.py reads stdin in a loop (long-lived while the panel is open), so a leaked zombie would persist for the session, but only on the rare hang path of an interactive binding action.

confirmed.title: sinden-smoother-tune.sh drops the snap_threshold key on save, resetting it to the daemon default
confirmed.evidence: sinden-smoother-tune.sh:11-12 reads only alpha+deadzone; lines 24-28 do `cat > "$CFG"` rewriting the whole [smoothing] section with ONLY alpha/deadzone, so any snap_threshold is dropped. snap_threshold is a real persisted key: sinden-smoother-preset.sh:18 writes `snap_threshold = $SNAP` and the daemon reads it (sinden-smoother.py:67 `s_thr = float(s.get('snap_threshold', s_thr))`, default DEFAULT_SNAP_THRESHOLD=1000.0 at sinden-smoother.py:46; also lib/sinden_cfg.py:190). After a tune-save the daemon reverts snap to the 1000 default. Real key-drop bug; impact limited because the dropped value resets to the documented default, so low is correct.

confirmed.title: sinden-camera.sh edits the user's tuned LightgunMono.exe.config via `sed -i` with no backup and unescaped replacement
confirmed.evidence: sinden-camera.sh:13-15 set_val() does `sed -i "s|...value=\"$2\"|"` directly on $HOME/Lightgun/LightgunMono.exe.config with no .bak. I reproduced both sub-claims in /tmp/_madrev: a value containing '&' injected the whole matched text and corrupted the entry (output: `value="99<add key="CameraContrast" value="50"boom"`), and a value containing '|' made sed abort `unknown option to s'`. There is no input validation between the zenity form (lines 27-37) and set_val (lines 42-49). Mitigations keeping this low: in normal use the fields are numeric (0-255 / exposure ints) so the special chars never appear, and ~/Lightgun is captured by deck-backup.sh:147 (full backup, not per-edit). Note the modern GUI path is safe by contrast — lib/sinden_cfg.py:116-129 set_many() does backup_once() + re.escape on the key + lambda replacement + atomic tmp+os.replace. The shell script is a manually-invoked legacy helper (no references found elsewhere in the launchers dir or ES-DE config).

confirmed.title: reorganize-cd-games.py can leave a renamed game file orphaned as '<name>.__moving' if mkdir fails mid-operation
confirmed.evidence: reorganize-cd-games.py:155-157 runs `os.rename(target, tmp_target)` (renames <name>.cue -> <name>.cue.__moving), then `os.makedirs(target)`, then `os.rename(tmp_target, target/folder_name)`. There is no try/except around these three lines and no try/except around the execute_plan call (verified: only try/except in the file are at lines 50/58 and 69/75 inside parse helpers; main loop at line 200 is unguarded). If os.makedirs or the second rename raises (perm error / race / interrupt), the original file is orphaned as '<name>.__moving' and the script aborts with a traceback. Severity stays low: this is a recoverable rename (not a delete — no actual data loss), and os.makedirs failing immediately after a just-succeeded rename in the same writable parent dir is a rare trigger (mainly mid-operation interruption).

confirmed.title: _ENUM_CACHE module global is mutated by the watch thread and inline router/RPC calls with no lock
confirmed.evidence: devices.py:134 _ENUM_CACHE is a module global mutated (set at :193, pop at :171, del-in-list-comprehension at :216-217, iterated at :216) with NO lock. Concurrency is real: rpc.py:24 _POOL=ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4); device_cmds.py:72/109 devices.sdl/devices.wiimotes are slow=True (pool threads) and call dv.enumerate_devices() (:76); devices.scan (:69) runs inline on the stdin thread; _WatchStream.run (:132-144) calls _scan()->enumerate_devices() every 2s on its OWN thread. So >=2 threads can run enumerate_devices() concurrently. The genuine failure is `RuntimeError: dictionary changed size during iteration` at :216 when thread B hits :193 `_ENUM_CACHE[path]=...` during thread A's comprehension (the new-node probe at :167-192 releases the GIL during InputDevice()/caps()/close — the ~37ms blocking close noted at :127). Severity is correctly LOW: the failure is caught at every call site (_WatchStream wraps _scan in try/except pass at :142-143; slow/inline methods convert it to an EINTERNAL response via rpc.py:64-67) and is self-healing on the next call — no daemon crash, no data loss. A no-lock pattern that a guard would fix, but practically benign.

confirmed.title: Quitting MAD during a Daphne X-Arcade capture orphans the hypseus_capture SDL subprocess (quit() does not run _clear())
confirmed.description: App.quit() (lines 1870-1875) only calls `_gp_cleanup()` and `_cam_restore_driver()` then `root.destroy()`; it deliberately does NOT call `_clear()`. The Daphne capture path (lib/mad_daphne_page.py:286-288) spawns `hypseus_capture.py` as a separate subprocess into `self._dp_proc` and waits on it in a daemon worker thread. That subprocess is only killed in `_clear()` (router-config-gui.py:1347-1357) or by `_dp_bind_done`. If the user holds Start+Select to quit while a Daphne bind capture is in flight, neither runs: root is destroyed, the daemon worker thread dies with the process, and the SDL subprocess is left running. This is the same class as known finding [4.0]/[10.0] (quit bypasses teardown) but a distinct process. I verified hypseus_capture.py is short-lived (`--timeout 10`; 'timeout exits 2'), and that the X-Arcade evdev grabs in `_xa_devs`/`_xat_edit_devs`/`_it_devs` are NOT a permanent leak because EVIOCGRAB is released automatically when the fd closes at process exit — so the ONLY surviving resource is the SDL subprocess, which self-terminates within ~10s.
confirmed.impact: A transient orphaned SDL process (≤10s) holding the X-Arcade SDL joystick open after MAD has quit and ES-DE/Hypseus is launching. Bounded and self-healing, so impact is minor, but during that window the launched game's SDL enumeration could briefly contend for the X-Arcade.
confirmed.fix: Have App.quit() run the relevant teardown (kill `self._dp_proc` if set, like _clear does) before root.destroy(); simplest is to call a lightweight subset, or have quit() invoke `_clear()` (guarded) so every page's grabs/subprocesses are released deterministically rather than relying on each resource's own timeout.
confirmed.evidence: router-config-gui.py:865 `self.nav = GamepadNav(root, self.back, self.quit, ...)` + :632 hold-Start+Select fires `self.on_quit` => `App.quit()`. quit() at :1870-1875 calls only `_gp_cleanup()`, `_cam_restore_driver()`, `root.destroy()` — it does NOT call `_clear()`, and the Daphne capture teardown (`_dp_proc.kill()`) lives ONLY in `_clear()` (:1347-1357). The capture proc is spawned in lib/mad_daphne_page.py:286-288 (`subprocess.Popen([... hypseus_capture.py ...])`) and stored in `self._dp_proc`. So quitting mid-capture orphans it. BUT impact is minimal and bounded: hypseus_capture.py self-exits on `--timeout 10` (lib/hypseus_capture.py:155-156 `deadline = time.monotonic()+timeout` loop, exits 2 on timeout), holds NO exclusive device grab (grep for grab/EVIOCGRAB in hypseus_capture.py = none — it's a passive SDL joystick reader), and has no window. It dies on its own within ~10s. Also note GuiMenu.cpp:2351-2353 says the Tk panel is 'retired at parity'; MAD.sh is Desktop-mode only. Low (borderline info).

confirmed.title: Camera preview start runs up to 4 synchronous v4l2-ctl calls + sinden-stop on the UI thread before launching ffmpeg
confirmed.line: 2443-2446 (_cam_preview) -> _cam_apply_live 2411-2417 -> sinden_cfg.set_ctrl (lib/sinden_cfg.py:168-174); also _cam_set 2490-2502 on every slider step
confirmed.description: Pressing 'Preview Pn' calls, on the Tk main thread and in order: _sinden_pause_driver() (synchronous subprocess.run sinden-stop.sh, timeout=15, line 1956) then _cam_apply_live() which makes up to four synchronous sinden_cfg.set_ctrl() calls (brightness/contrast/auto_exposure/exposure), each a `subprocess.run(['v4l2-ctl', ...], timeout=2)` (lib/sinden_cfg.py:171). Every camera slider step also fires set_ctrl synchronously via _cam_set (line 2502). v4l2-ctl is normally fast (tens of ms) and sinden-stop.sh is ~0.5-1s, so in normal use this is fine — but set_ctrl's own docstring says 'the camera may be momentarily busy', and when busy each v4l2-ctl can block up to its 2s timeout. Worst case at preview-start the UI is unresponsive for sinden-stop (<=15s timeout) + 4*2s, and dragging a slider can stack 2s stalls. There is no worker thread for these (unlike the Preview-page scan, which is correctly threaded in _preview_rescan).
confirmed.impact: Occasional UI hitch/short freeze when the camera is busy or the driver is slow to stop — the panel feels stuck for a beat. Not a hard hang (all calls are timeout-bounded) and not on the typical fast path.
confirmed.fix: Move the pause+apply+launch sequence (and per-step set_ctrl) onto a worker thread that posts results back via self._ui_q, mirroring _preview_rescan; or at least lower the set_ctrl timeout and debounce slider on_change so a fast drag doesn't queue many blocking v4l2-ctl calls on the UI thread.
confirmed.evidence: router-config-gui.py:2438 `_cam_preview` calls :2443 `self._sinden_pause_driver(...)` which (router-config-gui.py:1956-1957) runs `subprocess.run([sinden-stop.sh], timeout=15)` SYNCHRONOUSLY on the Tk main thread, then :2446 `_cam_apply_live` (:2411-2417) makes 3-4 synchronous `sinden_cfg.set_ctrl` calls, each `subprocess.run([v4l2-ctl ...], timeout=2)` (lib/sinden_cfg.py:171-172). `_cam_set` (:2490-2502) also runs `set_ctrl` synchronously on every slider step. The 15s sinden-stop.sh call is the real freeze risk — the UI is unresponsive until it returns. Confirmed; low is fair (it's a tuning page where a brief pause is expected, and ffmpeg launch is after the blocking calls so the freeze is pre-preview only).

confirmed.title: SIGTERM from ES-DE (or session) bypasses MAD's quit/_clear cleanup — Sinden left dead / LED stuck / ffmpeg orphaned even when not quitting via the in-app combo
confirmed.description: All driver/preview/LED/device cleanup lives in App.quit() (lines 1870-1875: _gp_cleanup + _cam_restore_driver) and App._clear() (lines 1306-1388: _cam_kill_ffmpeg, _cam_restore_driver, evdev/X-Arcade ungrab+close, after_cancel). Both run ONLY on the in-app hold-Start+Select -> on_quit -> quit() path. main() (4601-4606) just calls root.mainloop() with no try/finally, there is NO root.protocol('WM_DELETE_WINDOW', ...) handler, and the only signal handling is faulthandler.register(SIGTERM/SIGHUP/SIGINT, chain=True) (lines 74-79) which dumps a stack then chains to the DEFAULT disposition (terminate). The atexit hook (83-84) only writes a log line — it does NOT restore the Sinden driver or kill ffmpeg. MAD is exec'd as a child of the ES-DE process (MAD.sh line 16); when the user leaves MAD or ES-DE itself exits, ES-DE can SIGTERM the child. If that happens while the camera-tuning page paused the Sinden driver (_cam_driver_paused) or while a preview ffmpeg subprocess is live, none of the cleanup runs: the lightgun stays stopped, the TV-border LED webhook stays ON, and the ffmpeg preview is orphaned.
confirmed.impact: Same broken end-state as wave-1 [4.0] (guns dead / LED stuck / orphaned ffmpeg) but reachable WITHOUT using the camera page's own quit — any external SIGTERM (ES-DE reaping the child, session manager) while a preview/pause is active leaves the hardware in a bad state until the next explicit Sinden start. Low because it requires being mid-preview/pause AND an external kill; on plain process exit fds are reclaimed by the kernel so grabbed evdev nodes self-release.
confirmed.fix: Install a SIGTERM/SIGINT handler (and root.protocol('WM_DELETE_WINDOW', self.quit)) that routes through the same cleanup as quit()/_clear() — at minimum call _cam_kill_ffmpeg() + _cam_restore_driver() — before terminating, so an external kill restores the driver/LED. Note this is the same underlying cleanup gap flagged by [4.0] but via a different, more common trigger.
confirmed.evidence: router-config-gui.py:73-86 only arms faulthandler: `_faulthandler.register(_sig, file=_fault_log, chain=True)` for SIGTERM/SIGHUP/SIGINT — `chain=True` writes a traceback then re-raises the DEFAULT signal action (process dies); it never calls `quit()` or `_clear()`. atexit (:83-84) only writes a log line. main() (:4601-4606) installs no `signal.signal` handler. So an external SIGTERM kills MAD without running `_cam_restore_driver()` (Sinden/LED) or `_clear()` (ffmpeg/Daphne proc). Confirmed mechanically. Severity capped at low: the documented normal exit is the in-app hold-to-quit combo (-> quit()), and per GuiMenu.cpp:2351-2353 the Tk app is Desktop-mode-only / retired in Game Mode, so a session SIGTERM to this process is not the common path. State left dirty (gun dead / LED stuck) is recoverable by re-running the driver/MAD.

confirmed.title: model2-m2emu.sh: unguarded `sed -i` on a missing EMULATOR.INI aborts the whole launch under `set -eu`
confirmed.description: The script runs with `set -eu` (line 14). The DrawCross crosshair toggle is `sed -i 's/DrawCross=1/DrawCross=0/' "$INI"` / the inverse (lines 28-29), where `INI="$M2DIR/EMULATOR.INI"` (line 17). There is NO guard that `$INI` exists. If EMULATOR.INI is absent (fresh/unmigrated wine prefix, a renamed file, or the model2 dir was moved to the SD card per the script's own comment that 'games live in ES-DE's rom dir on the SD card'), `sed -i` exits non-zero and `set -e` aborts the script BEFORE the final `exec "$UMU" ./EMULATOR.EXE "$GAME"` (line 33) — so the game silently never launches. I reproduced this on a /tmp copy: with INI missing the script dies at the sed with exit 2 and never reaches the exec. This is DISTINCT from the known finding 8.0 (model-2-emulator.sh targets the literal string "M2CONFIGFILE"); here the variable is used correctly but the missing-file case is fatal. Note the current real INI at ~/Emulation/roms/model2/EMULATOR.INI does exist and the DrawCross toggle works correctly today (the sed prefix-matches `DrawCross=1` even with the trailing tabs+comment), so this is an edge-case break, not a common-path one.
confirmed.impact: On any setup where EMULATOR.INI is not exactly at $HOME/Emulation/roms/model2/EMULATOR.INI (path change, fresh prefix, SD-card relocation), every Model 2 game launch dies before the emulator starts, with no on-screen error — the user just sees ES-DE return to the gamelist.
confirmed.fix: Guard the sed: `[ -f "$INI" ] && case "$GAME" in ... esac` (or wrap the case in `if [ -f "$INI" ]; then ... fi`). The crosshair toggle is cosmetic and must never be able to abort the launch.
confirmed.evidence:
Mechanism CONFIRMED by execution. Script (/home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/model2-m2emu.sh): line 14 `set -eu`; line 17 `INI="$M2DIR/EMULATOR.INI"`; lines 28-29 `sed -i ... "$INI"` as the FINAL command in each case arm; line 33 `exec "$UMU" ./EMULATOR.EXE "$GAME"`. Reproduced the exact structure in /tmp/_madrev/repro2.sh (case + trailing sed + set -eu, run via /bin/sh→bash): with INI missing, BOTH branches abort at sed with exit 2 BEFORE printing "AFTER-CASE"/exec (`/tmp/_madrev/repro2.sh bel` → "sed: can't read ...: No such file or directory", exit=2; same for `daytona`); with INI present it reaches the exec (exit=0). GNU sed 4.9 returns exit 2 and does NOT create the file on a missing target. So the unguarded fatal-abort-before-exec is real and silent.

HOWEVER severity downgraded medium→low: the finding's reachability scenarios are mostly invalid. `M2DIR` is a HARDCODED constant (`$HOME/Emulation/roms/model2`, line 16); EMULATOR.INI and EMULATOR.EXE both live in that same fixed dir (verified: both present, 2774B / 1924608B). The SD-card comment (lines 12-13) refers to where ROMs live — resolved INTERNALLY by m2emu via `[RomDirs] Dir2` inside the INI — NOT to the INI/EXE location, so a user moving ROMs to the SD card does not move the INI. There is no "path change" or "SD-card relocation" of the INI possible through the script. The wine prefix (`$M2DIR/pfx`, line 20) is independent of the INI, so a "fresh/unmigrated prefix" does NOT delete EMULATOR.INI (finding's claim is wrong). The only realistic missing-INI case is a partial/broken install or manual deletion of just the INI — and if the INI is gone, EMULATOR.EXE (same dir) is almost certainly gone too, so `exec ./EMULATOR.EXE` would fail anyway; the user-facing outcome (silent return to gamelist) is identical to the already-broken-install case. Finding itself concedes the common path works today (live INI exists, DrawCross toggle works). Genuine robustness gap (no `[ -f "$INI" ]` guard; should not be fatal), but a narrow edge case, not a normal-use break → low. Distinct from finding 8.0 (model-2-emulator.sh seds the literal string "M2CONFIGFILE") — confirmed by grep: model-2-emulator.sh:27,30 use `"M2CONFIGFILE"` literal, this script uses `"$INI"` correctly.

confirmed.title: supermodel.sh: `param="${@}"` collapses all launch arguments into a single quoted word
confirmed.description: Line 4 does `param="${@}"` (joins all positional args with a single space), line 5 strips single quotes, and line 6 calls `/usr/bin/flatpak run com.supermodel3.Supermodel "${param}"` — passing the joined string as ONE argument. I reproduced this: with two args `/roms/daytona.zip` and `-fullscreen`, Supermodel receives a single arg `[/roms/daytona.zip -fullscreen]` (argc=1). For a single ROM path containing spaces it happens to work (one arg), but any case where Supermodel needs a ROM plus a flag, or multiple args, gets them mangled into one token Supermodel can't parse. This is EmuDeck's stock wrapper (not MAD-authored — the MAD-specific Supermodel path is supermodel-native.sh / supermodel-proton.sh / supermodel-sinden.sh), so impact is limited, but the construct is incorrect.
confirmed.impact: Multi-argument Supermodel launches (ROM + extra flags) are passed as a single garbled argument; the emulator fails or ignores flags. In normal ES-DE usage only `%ROM%` is passed, so single-ROM launches are unaffected.
confirmed.fix: Use the array form: drop the `param` collapse and call `/usr/bin/flatpak run com.supermodel3.Supermodel "$@"` (do the single-quote stripping per-arg in a loop like the azahar-family wrappers do), preserving argument boundaries.
confirmed.evidence: supermodel.sh:4-6: `param="${@}"` joins all args with a space into ONE string, then `flatpak run ... "${param}"` passes it as a SINGLE word. Reproduced in /tmp: input `("/games/scud.zip" "-fullscreen")` -> single arg `[/games/scud.zip -fullscreen]`; input `("-res=496,384" "/games/My Game/lemans24.zip")` -> single arg `[-res=496,384 /games/My Game/lemans24.zip]`. Stock ES-DE Supermodel command passes MULTIPLE args (`-log-output=... %INJECT%=... %ROM%`, resources/systems/linux/es_systems.xml), which this would collapse and break. REAL bug. BUT it is currently UNREACHABLE: the user's active config /home/deck/ES-DE/custom_systems/es_systems.xml:53-54 launches `supermodel-native.sh` / `supermodel-sinden.sh`, NOT `supermodel.sh`; `grep supermodel\.sh` in active configs returns nothing and no es_find_rules.xml maps %EMULATOR_SUPERMODEL% to it. supermodel.sh (249 bytes, EmuDeck-stock `emulatorInit "supermodel"`) is dead code here. Confirmed bug, low severity given non-reachability in the live setup.

confirmed.title: install-bezels.sh / install-bezels-all.sh create symlinks into the RetroArch overlays tree
confirmed.description: Both bezel installers use `ln -sfn "$src" "$dest"` to populate ~/.var/app/org.libretro.RetroArch/.../overlays/GameBezels/<system>/ with symlinks pointing back into the cloned bezelproject repo under ~/Emulation/tools/bezelproject. The project rule states 'No symlinks in the game/data layout'. These overlay symlinks live in RetroArch's CONFIG tree (not the ROM/gamelist data layout), so it is borderline rather than a clear violation, but it does mean the overlays silently break if the bezelproject clone is moved/deleted, and a deck-backup of the RetroArch config dir would capture dangling symlinks rather than the PNG/cfg payloads. (The separate `cat > <game>.cfg` clobber in these scripts is the already-known finding 8.3.)
confirmed.impact: If the bezelproject source repo is relocated or removed, every per-game bezel overlay silently fails to load (RetroArch shows no overlay); backups of the overlay dir contain only dead links.
confirmed.fix: Copy the .cfg/.png files into the overlay dir instead of symlinking (they are small), matching the no-symlink data-layout rule; or document this as an intentional exception for the config tree.
confirmed.evidence: install-bezels.sh:48 `ln -sfn "$src" "$dest"` and install-bezels-all.sh:70 `ln -sfn "$src" "$target_dir/$(basename "$src")"` both create symlinks from the cloned bezelproject repos into ~/.var/app/org.libretro.RetroArch/config/retroarch/overlays/GameBezels/. This violates CLAUDE.md rule #4 (no symlinks in the data layout unless explicitly OK'd). Impact is bounded: these are one-off install scripts (not wired into MAD/router; install-bezels-all.sh does NOT call install-bezels.sh, it inlines the same logic), and symlinking is the Bezel Project's own documented install mechanism. Risk is dangling links if the source repos under ~/Emulation/tools/bezelproject are moved/deleted. No data loss, no crash. Low is correct.

confirmed.title: Tk Gamepad-tester P2 auto-assignment matches a bare "2" token in the pad name — false-positives on model names like "8BitDo Pro 2" (same bug as wave-1 [3.1] but in the live Tk GUI path, a separate file)
confirmed.description: _gp_name_is_p2 does `toks = low.replace("#", " ").split()` then `any(t in ("p2", "ii", "2", "player2") for t in toks)`. The bare "2" and "ii" tokens fire on any controller whose name contains a standalone '2'/'II' that is part of a MODEL name rather than a player indicator — e.g. '8BitDo Pro 2' -> tokens ['8bitdo','pro','2'] -> matched as Player 2; 'N64 ... II' etc. Wave-1 flagged the identical logic in the headless backend ([3.1] tester_cmds.py:276-279 / and the gamepads.layout path) but this Tk copy is in router-config-gui.py's ACTIVE App (sections list registers `("Gamepads", self.gamepad)`), so it runs in the live GUI too.
confirmed.impact: A connected single pad whose name merely contains '2'/'II' is silently auto-badged as Player 2 in the tester (and, since `_gp_is_p2` feeds the persisted gp-p2-units override default, it shapes the default P2 state). Cosmetic/assignment glitch; the user can manually toggle it.
confirmed.fix: Match P2 only on unambiguous tokens (drop bare '2'/'ii'; require 'p2'/'player2'/'player 2', or word-boundary 'ii' as a standalone trailing token), consistent with whatever fix lands for [3.1] — and ideally share one helper between the Tk and headless copies so they can't drift.
confirmed.evidence: lib/mad_gamepad_tester.py:384-389 _gp_name_is_p2 splits the lowercased name on spaces and matches `t in ('p2','ii','2','player2')`. I replicated the exact logic: '8BitDo Pro 2' -> True, 'Nintendo Switch Pro Controller 2' -> True, '8BitDo FC30 II' -> True, 'PowerA Wired 2' -> True. So real, common controller model names ending in a bare '2' are wrongly auto-assigned P2. This IS the live path: MAD.sh line 16 execs router-config-gui.py, which imports GamepadTesterMixin from this exact file (router-config-gui.py:51), and App(GamepadTesterMixin,...) at line 793 uses _gp_is_p2 -> _gp_name_is_p2. Same bare-'2' bug also exists in the headless backend (lib/madsrv/tester_cmds.py:62,277) — a separate file as the candidate notes. Mitigated by a manual per-uniq override (mad_gamepad_tester.py:396 `_gp_p2_overrides().get(uniq, auto)`), so it's only a wrong default the user can fix, not data loss. Low is correct.

confirmed.title: WiiTesterStream slot-claim handover sleeps 0.1s uninterruptibly after writing the claim file; an immediate STOP still starts a fresh WiiSlotReader on the slot
confirmed.description: In WiiTesterStream.run the claim file is written then `time.sleep(0.1)` runs UN-gated (no `self.stopped.wait(0.1)`), after which `self.reader = WiiSlotReader(node); self.reader.start()` always executes. If tester.stop / a STOP->START is issued during that 100ms window, run() ignores `self.stopped` and still spins up a reader and enters the loop body before the `while not self.stopped.wait(self.HZ)` check; the reader is then torn down by cleanup. Compared to the pad/X-Arcade streams (which use `if self.stopped.wait(0.15): return` before grabbing, lines 554, 754), the Wii path has no such early-out. Combined with the already-flagged STOP->START reader-leak ([10.8]) and re-arm cooldown ([7.3]), this widens the window where a just-stopped Wii tester still claims+reads a slot.
confirmed.impact: A rapid Start/Stop/Start of the Wii tester can briefly start a reader that's immediately discarded, and momentarily hold the claim file + slot the nav bridge just released, adding to the handover churn. No data loss; transient.
confirmed.fix: Make the handover sleep interruptible and bail early: `if self.stopped.wait(0.1): return` (matching the pad/x-arcade pattern), so a stop during the bridge-handover window returns before creating the reader.
confirmed.evidence: lib/madsrv/tester_cmds.py:890 is `time.sleep(0.1)` (a plain sleep, NOT self.stopped.wait(0.1)), so it is uninterruptible by stop_stream (rpc.py:138-144 just sets self.stopped Event). Line 891 then unconditionally does `self.reader = WiiSlotReader(node); self.reader.start()` with no `if self.stopped.is_set(): return` guard before it — the first stop check is at line 904, after the reader is already started. WiiSlotReader.start() (wii_slot_reader.py:47-48) spins a thread that opens the hidraw node O_RDWR (line 91). So an immediate STOP during the 0.1s window still starts a fresh reader on the slot. Impact is bounded: cleanup() (tester_cmds.py:963-966) is guaranteed by _guarded's finally (rpc.py:114-126) and calls self.reader.stop(timeout=0.6), so the briefly-opened node is released. Real but minor robustness nit — low (borderline info).

confirmed.title: sinden.led_set RPC and the GUI LED toggle both rewrite the user's sinden.conf non-atomically
confirmed.description: lib/madsrv/sinden_cmds.py:_led_set reads CONF (the repo's user-tuned sinden.conf, CONF = HERE / 'sinden.conf', line 32), re.subs the SINDEN_LED_ENABLED line, then does CONF.write_text(new) at line 203 with no temp+rename. router-config-gui.py:2130 (`conf.write_text(t2)`) is the GUI-process copy of the exact same read→sub→write_text on HERE/'sinden.conf'. Neither path writes to a temp file then renames. This is a distinct non-atomic-write site from the known sinden findings ([8.4] sinden-smoother-tune.sh, [8.5] sinden_cmds.py:450-459 camera.set which is a validation/KeyError bug, and [8.8] sinden-camera.sh) — none of those cover the led_set write of sinden.conf.
confirmed.impact: A crash or power-loss between truncate and write of the small sinden.conf would leave the user's Sinden configuration (base URL, webhook IDs, LED setting, smoother params — the GUI text at router-config-gui.py:2137 says 'edit there') truncated or empty. File is small so the truncation window is narrow; impact is low but it is a real violation of the project's atomic-write rule on a user config.
confirmed.fix: Write via a sibling temp file and os.replace (the repo already has this pattern in lib/sinden_cfg.py:128 which uses tmp.write_text then rename; route led_set through it instead of an in-place write_text).
confirmed.evidence: Two non-atomic writers of sinden.conf confirmed by Read: lib/madsrv/sinden_cmds.py:196-203 (_led_set: text=CONF.read_text(); new=re.sub(...); CONF.write_text(new)) and router-config-gui.py:2124-2130 (GUI _led_set: t=conf.read_text(); t2=re.sub(...); conf.write_text(t2)). CONF = HERE/'sinden.conf' (sinden_cmds.py:32) is MAD's shell-style launcher config (sinden.conf:7 'SINDEN_LED_ENABLED=1') and is NOT the file sinden_cfg edits (sinden_cfg.py:15 CONFIG = ~/Lightgun/LightgunMono.exe.config, XML <add key> format) — so the existing atomic set_many/backup_once (sinden_cfg.py:116-129) cannot be trivially reused. The finding is real: this contradicts the codebase's pervasive tmp+os.replace convention (sinden_cfg.py:127-129, lib/hypinput.py:264-273, lib/es_systems_wrap.py:98-109, lib/localpolicy.py:89-92, lib/retroarch_cfg.py:213). sinden.conf's own header (lines 4-6) states the user hand-edits HA base URL + webhook IDs there, so it contains user-authored values, not just MAD-toggled state. Mitigating: the write preserves the entire file via a single regex sub (no existing-key dropping), and a 566-byte write_text is a single write() syscall — truncation requires a crash/power-loss in a very narrow window. LOW is the correct rating.

confirmed.title: GUI gamepad/xarcade testers write user calibration & position JSON non-atomically (same class as [3.0], different module)
confirmed.description: Known finding [3.0] scopes the non-atomic calibration-JSON write to lib/madsrv/tester_cmds.py:228-230 (the BACKEND RPC). The GUI-side tester mixins are a different module set imported by router-config-gui.py (line 51-52: GamepadTesterMixin / XArcadeTesterMixin) and write the same class of user data with the same non-atomic p.write_text(json.dumps(...)) pattern: lib/mad_gamepad_tester.py:407 (gp-p2-units.json), :668/:672 (per-profile gamepad-positions json), :1121 (gamepad-calib json); lib/mad_xarcade_tester.py:212 (xarcade-positions.json), :600 (xarcade-calib.json). All paths are under ~/Emulation/storage/control-panel/ (confirmed via _gp_pos_file/_xat_pos_file/etc), i.e. persisted user calibration, and all use a bare write_text with no temp+rename.
confirmed.impact: A crash during the GUI's save (or concurrent saves) truncates the user's controller calibration/position/P2 layout files. Loss is recoverable by re-calibrating on screen, so severity is low, but these are unlisted sites of the same atomic-write-rule violation as [3.0].
confirmed.fix: Funnel all these saves through one atomic helper (write to <file>.tmp in the same dir, then os.replace), matching the temp+rename pattern used elsewhere in the repo (lib/sinden_cfg.py:128, lib/es_systems_wrap.py:108).
confirmed.evidence: All 6 cited write sites confirmed non-atomic by Read + grep: mad_gamepad_tester.py:407 (p2 overrides), 668/672 (positions), 1121 (calib), and mad_xarcade_tester.py:212 (positions), 600 (calib) — every one is p.write_text(json.dumps(...)). grep for os.replace/.tmp/tempfile/atomic/os.rename in both tester modules returns nothing (exit 1), so no atomic helper exists there, unlike the rest of the codebase. Files live in ~/Emulation/storage/control-panel/ (gp-<key>-positions.json / -calib.json, xarcade-positions.json/-calib.json, gp-p2-overrides.json — paths at mad_gamepad_tester.py:445-448, 1103-1105; mad_xarcade_tester.py:87-88) and hold user-produced overlay-position + button-calibration state. The 'same class as [3.0]' framing holds. Mitigating: each save re-serializes the full in-memory dict (no existing-key dropping); baked-in defaults (_gp_default_pos / _xat_default_spots) + Reset Positions buttons make a lost file recoverable to defaults; small single-buffer writes make truncation require a crash mid-write. Genuine robustness gap, LOW is correct.

confirmed.title: Two tomllib.load() calls leak the underlying file object (never closed)
confirmed.evidence: lib/policy.py:22 `base = tomllib.load(POLICY.open('rb'))` leaves the file object unclosed (no `with`). NOTE the candidate's count is inaccurate: lib/policy.py has exactly ONE such call (line 22), not two — `grep tomllib.load` shows lib/localpolicy.py:21 uses `with f:` (properly closed) and the other leak is in a DIFFERENT file (lib/madsrv/policy_cmds.py:43 `tomllib.load(open(POLICY,'rb'))`). Severity INFO: under CPython refcounting the unreferenced file object is finalized/closed immediately after tomllib.load returns; load_merged runs once per RPC (not a hot loop), so no fd accumulation/exhaustion risk. Pure style nit.

confirmed.title: camera.set RPC does not validate player ∈ {1,2} before indexing — KeyError on bad input
confirmed.evidence: sinden_cmds.py:450-457: player=int(params['player']); _cam['vals'] only ever holds keys 1 and 2 (_cam_seed_vals loops `for p in (1,2)` at line 282; _cam initialized {} at line 43), so _cam['vals'][player] (line 457) raises KeyError for player not in {1,2}. BUT the candidate's implied 'crash' is wrong: rpc.py:64-67 _run wraps every method in `except Exception` and returns a structured {'ok':False,'error':{'code':'EINTERNAL'}} — the daemon does NOT crash. Transport is a local stdin/stdout pipe spawned by the GUI (mad-backend.py:141 `for line in sys.stdin`), not a network socket, so no security exposure. The current GUI does camera control locally (router-config-gui.py:2490 _cam_set), and where the RPC contract is used it only sends player 1/2. Net: a benign input-validation gap that yields EINTERNAL instead of a clean EINVAL — info, not low.

confirmed.title: skyscraper-apply.py uses bare except blocks that swallow art-copy failures silently
confirmed.evidence: skyscraper-apply.py:55 and :57 are `except Exception: pass` (confirmed via grep -n except: only lines 43,55,57,67), silently swallowing os.makedirs and shutil.copy failures for art media. Impact is trivial: only cosmetic media (covers/miximages/marquees/screenshots, MT line 5) is affected, the `filled` counter (lines 46,56,58) surfaces how many actually copied, and the gamelist write itself is separately guarded by ET.ParseError validation+restore (lines 42-43). Note the title's 'bare except' is imprecise — these are `except Exception`, not bare `except:`. Real but negligible; severity info.

confirmed.title: _quit backstop derives the KILL command by blind string replace of '-TERM'->'-KILL'
confirmed.evidence: quit-combo-watcher.py:111 kill_cmd = quit_cmd.replace("-TERM","-KILL") is indeed a blind substring replace, and a literal '-TERM' embedded in a path/app-id (not a signal flag) would corrupt the derived KILL command. BUT severity is correctly info: (1) the common no-TERM case is already guarded — :112 `if kill_cmd != quit_cmd:` only arms the backstop when a -TERM existed; (2) --quit-cmd is generated by the system's own launcher hooks (docstring :20-23), not arbitrary user input; (3) a corrupted backstop only fails to escalate after KILL_AFTER s and `pkill -KILL` matching nothing is harmless (documented :109-110); (4) the PRIMARY quit at :120 subprocess.run(quit_cmd) is unaffected. Real-but-trivial fragility, rated correctly.

confirmed.title: load_merged parses the base controller-policy.toml with no error guard
confirmed.evidence: policy.py:21-22 `if POLICY.is_file(): base = tomllib.load(POLICY.open("rb"))` — no try/except around tomllib.load, so a malformed base controller-policy.toml raises TOMLDecodeError to every caller (mad_backup, backends_cmds, backup_cmds, tester_cmds, capture_cmds, policy_cmds, mad_xarcade_tester — 20+ sites). Telling asymmetry: the OVERRIDE loader localpolicy.load (localpolicy.py:19-22) DOES catch (TOMLDecodeError, OSError) and return {}, so the GUI-writable file is tolerant while the base is not. Also a minor handle leak: POLICY.open("rb") is never closed (no `with`). However correctly info: controller-policy.toml is a repo-checked-in trusted constant (the GUI writes only the guarded .local.toml), and when it does throw, rpc.py:64-67 converts it to an EINTERNAL response rather than crashing the daemon. Accurate observation, info severity.

confirmed.title: Dead/duplicate JSIOCGNAME ioctl constant assignment
confirmed.evidence: joystick-button-detector.py:25-26 — line 25 `JSIOCGNAME = 0x80006a13` is immediately overwritten by line 26 `JSIOCGNAME = (2<<30)|(128<<16)|(0x6a<<8)|0x13`. Verified in python3: line25=0x80006a13, line26=0x80806a13, equal=False. The correct kernel value JSIOCGNAME(128) = _IOC(_IOC_READ,'j',0x13,128) = 0x80806a13 matches line 26. So line 25 is not only dead but its literal is wrong (size field 0 instead of 128) and even contradicts its own inline comment '| (size << 16)'. Line 26 wins so the code functions correctly. Dead/duplicate assignment with no functional impact — info is correct.

confirmed.title: show-launchscreen.py docstring claims a FocusOut-driven close that the code deliberately does not implement
confirmed.evidence: show-launchscreen.py docstring lines 8-14 describe a FocusOut-driven close ('KEEP showing until the game window takes focus (FocusOut)' ... 'FocusOut just lets it close cleanly so it doesn't flash after the game exits'). grep 'bind|FocusOut|<Focus' returns only the three docstring/comment lines (9,13,114) — there is NO root.bind('<FocusOut>') anywhere. The implementation comment at lines 110-118 explicitly states the opposite: 'we must NOT destroy it early ... closing on that transient FocusOut is exactly what reopened the black gap.' Actual close paths are only SIGTERM (line 90 `signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, ...)`) and the safety timeout (line 108 `root.after(int(secs*1000), bye)`). Docstring is stale/misleading vs code; documentation-only, info correct.

confirmed.title: hypseus-pin.sh appends global-args via unquoted command substitution (intentional word-split, but unguarded against odd content)
confirmed.evidence: hypseus-pin.sh:58-61 `if [ -s "$_gargs" ]; then # shellcheck disable=SC2046; set -- "$@" $(cat "$_gargs" 2>/dev/null); fi`. The unquoted command substitution is intentional and explicitly documented (lines 54-56: 'Word-split is intentional (these are flags); absent/empty file = nothing appended') and the author already suppressed the warning with `# shellcheck disable=SC2046`. The file $HOME/Emulation/storage/hypseus/global-args is local same-user content (grep found NO current writer in launchers/*.py or madsrv/*.py — it's a user/GUI-edited config). The only real residual quirk is that unquoted $(...) is also subject to glob/pathname expansion (a literal '*' in the file would expand), but for a small local flags file the user controls via the GUI this is a negligible edge case. Observation is accurate; intentional + guarded; info correct. shellcheck binary not installed on this system (only bash-completion stub present), so analyzed by reading.

confirmed.title: mugen.sh creates symlinks into the game/data folders (external/, data/, font/) — runs against the no-symlinks rule
confirmed.evidence: /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/mugen.sh:46 `[[ ! -e external ]] && ln -s "$ikemen_home/external" external`; :53 `[[ ! -e "$sub/$name" ]] && ln -s "$f" "$sub/$name"` (for sub in data font); :64 `ln -s "$sys" "font/${win_ttf}.ttf"`. These create symlinks INTO the running game folder (cwd is `cd "$target"` at :41), which technically runs against the project 'no symlinks in the data layout' rule. Correctly rated info: (a) the rule primarily guards the ROM/gamelist data layout, not an engine runtime dir; (b) the links only fill files the game LACKS and never overwrite game files (guarded by `[[ ! -e ]]`); (c) they are documented bootstrap convenience for Ikemen GO. A real (minor) rule deviation, info is the right level.

confirmed.title: GUI config-save helpers open the base policy / emulator config files without a context manager (refcount-closed in CPython, leak under any non-refcounting runtime)
confirmed.description: _set_sys does `tomllib.load(open(POLICY,'rb')).get(...)` (line 3839) and _slot_binding does `open(...).read()` (lines 4161 cemu controllerN.xml, 4175 eden qt-config.ini) — the file object is never bound to a variable and never explicitly closed. Under CPython refcounting the object is unreferenced and closed immediately after the expression, so in practice on SteamOS this is harmless. It is the same pattern already noted as wave-1 [1.3] for lib/policy.py, here recurring inside the GUI's own page code (3839 on every system-toggle save; 4161/4175 up to 8x each time the controller-test page opens).
confirmed.impact: No real leak under CPython (prompt refcount close). Would leak fds under a non-refcounting interpreter; mainly a consistency/robustness nit and a re-occurrence of the [1.3] class in GUI code.
confirmed.fix: Use `with open(...) as f:` (or pathlib read_text / `with POLICY.open('rb') as f: tomllib.load(f)`) to make the close deterministic, matching the fix suggested for [1.3].
confirmed.evidence: router-config-gui.py:3839 `tomllib.load(open(POLICY, "rb"))`; :4161-4162 `open(os.path.join(cdir, f"controller{slot}.xml"), ...).read()`; :4175-4176 `open(os.path.expanduser(...), ...).read()` — all opened without a `with` context manager. Correctly rated info: all THREE are read-only opens (no write/truncate), and in CPython the file object's refcount hits 0 immediately after the temporary expression, closing the fd deterministically. No fd leak under CPython (the only runtime MAD runs on), no data-loss risk. Pure style/portability nit; info is accurate (the candidate itself acknowledges 'refcount-closed in CPython').

confirmed.title: GUI load_merged vs router deep_merge: confirmed DORMANT on live files — divergence only reachable via a hand-edited partial backends.cemu.templates table
confirmed.description: Material new evidence on wave-1 finding [1.0]. I executed both merges (routing.deep_merge recursive vs policy.load_merged hand-rolled 2-level) on the ACTUAL controller-policy.toml + controller-policy.local.toml and they produce a byte-identical result (deep-diff: NONE). The only 3-level tables present are backends.cemu.templates (base-only, inline) and backends.{cemu,eden}.slot_profiles (local-only); neither exists in BOTH files, so the 2-level vs recursive distinction never fires. I then reproduced the exact divergence: it requires a level-3 subtable (e.g. backends.cemu.templates) to be PARTIAL in local while base has more keys — then deep_merge yields the base+local union (3 keys) but load_merged replaces with only local's partial (1 key). Crucially, the GUI's own writer policy_cmds.set_backend_template (policy_cmds.py:200-203) reads the already-MERGED templates and writes back the FULL dict, so normal GUI operation never produces a partial level-3 table. Verified: GUI-write case -> both merges AGREE; hand-edit-partial case -> DISAGREE. So [1.0] is real but dormant under the live content and all GUI paths; it only bites a manual hand-edit of a level-3 table that overlaps a base level-3 table.
confirmed.impact: None in normal use. Documents that [1.0] cannot be triggered by the current files or by the GUI, narrowing its severity to a hand-edit-only edge case.
confirmed.fix: Optional hardening: have policy.load_merged() simply call routing.deep_merge() so the two never diverge even on a hand-edited 3-level table — removes the duplicated, subtly-different merge entirely.
confirmed.evidence: Confirmed dormant exactly as described. lib/policy.py:19-33 load_merged() merges only to depth 2 (line 28: `base[k][kk].update(vv)` is a shallow dict.update at the 3rd level), while lib/routing.py:35-43 deep_merge() recurses to any depth. I ran both against the live files: json.dumps(load_merged()) == json.dumps(load_policy()) -> IDENTICAL: True. A 3-deep dict DOES exist live (controller-policy.toml:355 `[backends.cemu].templates`, and controller-policy.local.toml:95 `[backends.cemu.slot_profiles]`), but slot_profiles exists only in local (no base value to merge) and templates exists only in base (not overridden), so the shallow-vs-deep difference never manifests. Divergence requires a hand-edited partial 3rd-level override (e.g. a partial [backends.cemu.templates] adding one vid:pid while base has others) — which the GUI-owned writer (localpolicy.dump) never emits. Info is correct.

confirmed.title: steam-fetch-media.py cdn_download/sgdb_download unlink existing artwork before writing the new file (additional sites of [11.3])
confirmed.description: Known finding [11.3] cites only place() at steam-fetch-media.py:93-97 (the for-other-in-glob: other.unlink() then shutil.copy2 pattern). The SAME delete-before-write pattern recurs in two other functions of the same file: cdn_download() at line 113 ('for other in (MEDIA/SUBDIR[kind]).glob(...): other.unlink()' then dst.write_bytes(data)) and sgdb_download() at line 134 (identical unlink loop then write_bytes). Both unlink the user's existing artwork BEFORE the new bytes are written, with no recovery move to _TMP.
confirmed.impact: Same as [11.3] but on the network-download paths: if the write_bytes fails after the unlink (it cannot here because the earlier urlopen already succeeded and data is in memory, so the realistic window is narrow), or if these are partially refactored, the old art is gone with no recovery. Low severity — the data is already in memory before the unlink in these two functions, unlike place() which copies from a separate src_path — but it is the same anti-pattern and worth listing as additional sites.
confirmed.fix: Write the new file to a temp name first, fsync/close, then unlink the old glob matches and rename — or move the old matches to a recoverable _TMP before overwriting, consistent with the never-delete rule.
confirmed.evidence: unlink-before-write confirmed at steam-fetch-media.py:112-114 (cdn_download), 133-135 (sgdb_download), and also place() at 94-97. CRITICAL mitigations: in both downloaders the unlink runs ONLY after a successful network read (data=r.read(), 109/130) AND the size sanity gate (if len(data)<1000: return False, 110-111/131-132), so a failed/placeholder download returns early and never deletes existing art. The target dir MEDIA=/run/media/deck/1tbDeck/downloaded_media/steam (line 43) is ES-DE derived/scraped media that is fully re-fetchable by re-running the script; the module docstring (line 17) states it intentionally 'Overwrites art so prior mis-scrapes are corrected'. Non-Steam grid art is only READ from ~/.steam/.../grid (line 42) and written TO MEDIA, so user source art is never unlinked. Residual gap is only a crash between unlink and write, on re-fetchable derived data — not user data loss. The candidate's stated LOW overstates it; downgrading to INFO.

contested.title: mad-backend teardown never shuts down the slow-method ThreadPool, so a SIGTERM/EOF can hang the daemon (and orphan a hypseus_capture SDL subprocess)
contested.evidence:
MECHANISM CONFIRMED, IMPACT OVERSTATED → downgrade high→medium, verdict uncertain (real bug but not as described).

(1) Pool never shut down: `grep -rn "_POOL|shutdown|stop_all_streams|atexit" lib/madsrv/rpc.py mad-backend.py` shows `_POOL = ThreadPoolExecutor(...)` (rpc.py:24), submit at rpc.py:83, and the ONLY teardown is mad-backend.py:159-162 `from lib.madsrv.rpc import stop_all_streams; stop_all_streams()`. There is no `_POOL.shutdown()` anywhere. stop_all_streams() (rpc.py:147-156) only sets/joins `Stream` threads; slow=True methods are NOT Streams, so the pool is untouched.

(2) Untouched pool DOES delay interpreter exit: Python 3.13.1; `python3 -c "...'_python_exit' in dir(t)"` → True, and its source unconditionally `t.join()`s every executor worker with NO timeout (atexit handler). Reproduced: /tmp/_madrev/repro.py submits a worker, calls no shutdown, main returns at ~0.2s → process exits only after the worker finishes: measured total wall time 3.06s (3s worker) and 2.15s for a daphne.bind-style subprocess.communicate worker ("teardown done, main returning" prints, then 2s later "bind worker done", then exit). So SIGTERM/EOF teardown does not exit promptly while a slow method is in flight — real, violates the "flush, exit 0" lifecycle invariant.

WHY DOWNGRADED / verdict uncertain:
- NOT an unbounded hang. The longest pool methods self-bound: daphne.bind spawns hypseus_capture.py with `--timeout 10` (daphne_cmds.py:183) and parent `proc.communicate(timeout=14)` (daphne_cmds.py:193); sinden subprocesses use `subprocess.run(..., timeout=15)` (sinden_cmds.py:339). Worst-case teardown delay ~10-15s, then the daemon DOES exit. "hang the daemon" is an overstatement.
- "orphan a hypseus_capture SDL subprocess" does not hold: the child self-exits at its own 10s timeout (hypseus_capture.py:128/188 capture timeout) and is reaped by communicate; on TimeoutExpired the except branch calls proc.kill() (daphne_cmds.py:198-203). `grep start_new_session|setsid|pdeathsig daphne_cmds.py hypseus_capture.py` → none, child shares the session, but its 10s self-timeout means no meaningful orphan window even on SIGKILL.
- No data loss, no leaked evdev grab (grabs are inside Streams, which stop_all_streams joins with a 2s timeout), no security impact.
- Reachability is narrow: a slow method must be in flight at the exact SIGTERM/EOF instant (e.g. mid daphne.bind capture when ES-DE dies).

Net: genuine robustness gap (teardown can be delayed up to ~15s under a narrow timing window, contradicting the stated prompt-exit invariant), but bounded, self-resolving, no grab/child/data leak → medium, not high.

refuter.evidence:
The narrow technical fact is true: _POOL (rpc.py:24, ThreadPoolExecutor) is never .shutdown(); stop_all_streams (rpc.py:147-156) only joins Stream threads, and mad-backend.py:159-163 finally-block calls only stop_all_streams(). I empirically confirmed an undrained ThreadPoolExecutor blocks interpreter exit via its atexit _python_exit handler: a script submitting a 3s task then immediately sys.exit(0) took 3.07s wall to exit (repro in /tmp/_madrev, now cleaned). BUT the HIGH framing is refuted on every consequence:

1) NOT an unbounded/user-visible hang. The C++ supervisor MadBackend.cpp shutdownChild() (lines 446-475) closes stdin (EOF), sends the "shutdown" RPC, sends SIGTERM, then loops waitpid(WNOHANG) for ~2s (40x50ms) and if not reaped sends SIGKILL + blocking waitpid (lines 457-472). So any pool-induced teardown delay is hard-capped at ~2s, then force-killed. The longest in-flight pool task is daphne.bind's proc.communicate(timeout=14) (daphne_cmds.py:191-203) or a backup tar/copytree (mad_backup.py:176 subprocess.run no-timeout) — all of which the SIGKILL path terminates.

2) Wrong module / wrong subprocess. capture_cmds.py spawns NO subprocess (evdev-only, lines 48-171). The hypseus_capture subprocess is in daphne_cmds.py daphne.bind (slow=True, Popen at :191).

3) Orphan is transient + harmless. On clean exit the atexit-join GUARANTEES the daphne.bind worker finishes and kills its child (proc.kill() at :201) — no orphan. Only the SIGKILL-escalation path can leave hypseus_capture running, and that process self-terminates within its own --timeout 10 (hypseus_capture.py:155-188), uses SDL_VIDEODRIVER=dummy, and holds NO evdev grab (grep for grab/EVIOCGRAB in hypseus_capture.py = none; SDL joystick polling only). It cannot leave a controller stuck.

4) The hard safety invariant ("dead panel never leaves a grabbed pad") is upheld independently: all EVIOCGRAB grabs are in Streams (tester_cmds.py:557,766), which stop_all_streams() stops+joins, unaffected by the pool gap.

Net: a minor robustness gap (unmanaged executor; up to ~2s slower teardown in a rare race; a transient, device-grab-free, self-cleaning orphan child), not a HIGH bug. Downgraded to low.

contested.title: install-bezels.sh / install-bezels-all.sh overwrite per-game RetroArch <game>.cfg with `cat >` (no backup), clobbering existing configs
contested.evidence:
MECHANISM CONFIRMED, REACHABILITY OVERSTATED — downgraded medium->low, verdict uncertain.

The write is real and non-atomic. install-bezels.sh:74-82 and install-bezels-all.sh:95-103 both do `cat > "$core_dir/$game.cfg" <<EOF` — a plain truncate-in-place: no backup, no temp+rename, no key-merge. Sandbox proof (/tmp/_madrev): seeded a hand-tuned override (video_shader=crt-royale, rewind_enable) at cores/MAME/mslug.cfg, ran the exact heredoc; AFTER = only the 7 bezel lines, dir listing shows just mslug.cfg (no .bak), grep for crt-royale/rewind_enable = 0 matches ("user data destroyed"). So the writer truncates with no recovery, against the project's "never destroy user data" rule.

BUT the finding's framing — "clobbering existing user configs" as a routine outcome — does not hold:
1) The script only ever writes <game>.cfg where $game = basename of a Bezel-Project repo .cfg (a ROM name). Across all 16 bezelproject-* repos there are 741 distinct cfg basenames; grep -ixE against every per-core/per-content/system override name returned NONE. So the high-value, irreplaceable user data — per-core override `FinalBurn Neo.cfg`/`fba.cfg` and per-content-dir override `arcade.cfg` (confirmed present in the MAME / FinalBurn Neo dirs via grep -l 'Per-CORE\|Per-content') — is NOT reachable: no bezel filename can collide with it.
2) Live state: in the 5 target cores (MAME, MAME 2010, MAME 2003-Plus, FinalBurn Neo, FB Alpha 2012), every existing *.cfg is already bezel/wire-bezels tagged — zero untagged genuine per-game overrides. The 36 hand-made `input_player1_analog_dpad_mode`/shader/rewind files I found are per-CORE/per-SYSTEM overrides (mame.cfg, snes.cfg, nes.cfg, psx.cfg, fbneo.cfg, neogeo.cfg...), named after cores/systems, which $game never produces.
3) Header documents intent: "Idempotent: re-running is safe — per-game configs re-written." Overwriting its own prior bezel output is by design.

Realistic blast radius: only a per-GAME override that simultaneously (a) has a Bezel-Project bezel, (b) has a matching ROM in the configured dirs, and (c) was hand-tuned by the user — an edge case with no current victims. Genuine robustness gap (silent, unrecoverable truncate vs. the no-data-loss rule) but not "clobbers existing user configs" in normal use. Hence low / uncertain rather than confirmed-medium.

Files: /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/install-bezels.sh:74-82, /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/install-bezels-all.sh:95-103. shellcheck unavailable on host ("command not found").

contested.title: Preview's active Wiimote probe writes to DolphinBar hidraw slots that the wii-nav-bridge holds open and is reading — unguarded cross-process contention
contested.evidence:
The cross-process contention is REAL and unguarded, but its claimed impact is overstated.

REAL part (confirmed by code):
- Active probe writes 0x15 to each DolphinBar slot opened O_RDWR|O_NONBLOCK: lib/devices.py:619 `os.open(path, os.O_RDWR|os.O_NONBLOCK)`, :629 `os.write(fd, bytes([0x15,0x00]))` (in `_dolphinbar_wiimotes_active`, called via `dolphinbar_wiimotes(active=True)` at device_cmds.py:121).
- The wii-nav-bridge holds the SAME nodes open O_RDWR and reads them continuously: lib/wii_slot_reader.py:91 `self._fd = os.open(self.node, os.O_RDWR)`, pump loop :118-137. Bridge keeps slots open whenever ES-DE is up (wii-nav-bridge.py:296-298), i.e. while the MAD GUI/Preview is open.
- NO coordination: the preview probe does NOT write the wii-tester-slot file, does NOT signal the bridge to pause, shares no lock. The tester-slot release mechanism (wii-nav-bridge.py:260-281, tester_cmds.py:882) is used ONLY by the dedicated Wii tester stream, NOT by the preview path. `grep` for tester/bridge/pause/lock in lib/devices.py returned nothing relevant.

Why severity is LOW, not medium (impact mitigated, harm not demonstrated):
1. No read-steal: hidraw broadcasts each input report to all open fds; the probe's `while os.read(fd,64): pass` drain (devices.py:623-624) drains only its own fd's queue, not the bridge's.
2. The documented nunchuk-wipe (deck-docs/wiimote.md:100-103) requires re-writing the F0/FB extension init — the probe writes ONLY 0x15, never F0/FB. The bridge re-inits F0/FB only when the ext-present bit CHANGES (wii_slot_reader.py:152-157); a 0x15 status doesn't change it (cached _ext_bit stays equal), so no wipe.
3. The probe-induced 0x20 status report is self-healing: bridge always re-sends SET_MODE on a 0x20 (wii_slot_reader.py:158), restoring the 0x32 continuous stream within ~one cycle (sub-second). This is the bridge's own keepalive mechanism.
4. Low frequency / cold path: probe is TTL-cached 20s (device_cmds.py:116-118; Tk path router-config-gui.py:2822) and runs ONLY while the Preview page is shown.
5. Result is display-only: `count` feeds the text label "DolphinBar: N Wiimotes" (preview_cmds.py:91); it is NOT on the game-launch/routing path (that path uses active=False, read-only, devices.py:558-559,565).

Worst realistic symptom: a sub-second nav stutter on the Preview page, at most once per 20s, while the user is in a menu (not a game). No data loss, no crash, no nunchuk wipe. Hence uncertain (contention real, claimed harm not demonstrated) and downgraded medium->low.

contested.title: controller-router warning dialog defaults to Cancel (abort launch) on ANY subprocess failure, including a missing/headless DISPLAY
contested.evidence:
controller-router.py:96-111 _show_warning_blocking runs lib.warning_dialog as a subprocess and returns its exit code; callers at :201-203 and :210-212 treat any non-zero as Cancel -> _setup returns 1 -> wrap.sh:31 (set -e) aborts the emulator exec. So "dialog failure => abort launch" is real and reachable IN PRINCIPLE.

BUT the finding mis-states the mechanism for the DISPLAY case. A broken/headless DISPLAY does NOT hit the `except Exception` branch (line 109-111). I verified subprocess.run (no check=True) does not raise on a non-zero child; it returns CompletedProcess(returncode=1), so the code returns via line 108. Reproduced: `DISPLAY=:99 python3 -m lib.warning_dialog T B` -> TclError, child EXIT=1; and a subprocess.run harness with env DISPLAY=:99 printed "NO EXCEPTION; returncode = 1". The except branch only fires for a true spawn failure (e.g. interpreter missing). Either way the OUTCOME is the same (Cancel/abort), so the finding's conclusion holds even though its stated path is wrong.

Refuting/downgrading factors:
1) Defensive intent present: controller-router.py:101 `env.setdefault("DISPLAY", ":0")` only sets :0 when DISPLAY is UNSET, preserving the real X/Gamescope DISPLAY at game-launch time. My tkinter failures here (DISPLAY=, XDG_SESSION_TYPE=tty over SSH) are the REVIEWER's headless tty, not the live ES-DE/Gamescope launch env, so they do not prove the live path fails.
2) Narrow reachability: _show_warning_blocking is only called when a warning would fire (require_sinden with no gun at :190-200, or X-Arcade presence mismatch at :119-149) AND the display is genuinely unreachable. Not a common path; on a working Deck the dialog renders fine (it was on-screen-debugged 2026-05-29 per warning_dialog.py comments).
3) The AUTO_PROCEED_SECONDS=30 net (warning_dialog.py:185-198) is set up AFTER tk.Tk() (:80), so it does NOT cover the DISPLAY-crash case — but fail-CLOSED (abort) here is a defensible choice for a protective warning, not a bug per se.
Impact is at most "launch aborted with generic error" on an abnormal display + warning combination: recoverable, no data loss, not a common-path crash, no security impact. medium is overstated; downgrade to low. Marked uncertain because the live launch-env DISPLAY validity is not verifiable headlessly (would need the user to trigger a warning on-device with a broken display).

contested.title: esde-splash-gen.sh silently deletes splash.svg if the tomllib parse transiently fails on a valid config
contested.evidence: esde-splash-gen.sh:26-37 + :101-105. The mechanism is real: I reproduced it in /tmp/_madrev — a malformed TOML makes the heredoc (stderr -> /dev/null at line 26) emit empty stdout, so cfg=[], `mode=$(sed -n 1p)` is empty, line 37 `[ -z "$mode" ] && mode=off`, and line 104 `off|*) rm -f "$OUT"` deletes splash.svg. BUT the candidate's premise 'transiently fails on a valid config' is FALSE: `python3 -c 'import tomllib'` succeeds on Python 3.13.1 (built in, no transient ImportError), and tomllib parsing is deterministic — it only fails on genuinely malformed TOML, not transiently on valid TOML. Also splash.svg is NOT user data: lines 1-2 state it is a GENERATED override that this script owns/regenerates every launch (default off-mode deletes it by design); grep shows no other code treats it as user data or backs it up (only esde-bigsplash-patch.sh references it as the patch target). So no data loss and the trigger described cannot occur. Real residual: a malformed config silently removes the override instead of preserving the last splash — info-level robustness only.

contested.title: wii-monitor.py busy-loops and never drops a slot on remote-disconnect EOF
contested.evidence: wii-monitor.py:125-130. The OSError path (line 128-129) DOES drop the slot (`os.close(fd); slots.pop(fd); break`) on a real disconnect — a USB unplug of the DolphinBar raises ENODEV/EIO -> OSError -> slot dropped. So 'never drops a slot' is overstated. The only un-dropped path is a hidraw fd returning empty bytes (`if not buf: continue`, line 130) while select keeps it ready — that would busy-spin, but a zero-length hidraw read on disconnect is not demonstrated (hidraw errors rather than EOFs; the code's own comments at 107-109 say empty slots give EPIPE on WRITE). Moreover keeping a slot for a quiet/asleep remote is BY DESIGN: the keepalive (lines 156-158, KEEPALIVE=1.5s) re-sends set_mode to revive a sleeping Wii remote (which the user wakes with 1+2), so dropping it would be wrong. The busy-loop claim is speculative and requires an unproven hidraw EOF mode; the real disconnect is handled. Low severity at most.

contested.title: singe-indexer.sh framefile path parsing truncates at first space
contested.evidence: singe-indexer.sh:33 `grep -oE -- '-framefile +[^ ]+' "$cmd" | awk '{print $2}'` — the `[^ ]+` class would stop at the first space, truncating a framefile path containing a space. Mechanism is real. BUT it cannot trigger with real data: (1) inspecting /home/deck/ROMs/daphne — NONE of the actual *.commands files contain `-framefile` at all (`grep -rl -- '-framefile' .../*.commands` returns nothing), so the `<base>.txt` fallback at line 34 is always taken; (2) no Daphne/Singe game dir contains a space; (3) the framefile convention per deck-docs/hypseus-daphne-import.md is `<driver>.txt` next to the game dir — a plain spaceless filename. So the truncation is unreachable in practice. Info-level theoretical robustness gap, effectively a non-issue.
