contested.title: convert-pixel-systems.py overwrites per-system theme.xml with a fixed template, discarding any non-color customization
contested.evidence: convert-pixel-systems.py:101-113 does overwrite per-system theme.xml from a fixed TEMPLATE (lines 24-78) keeping only themeColor/selectColor. BUT this is the documented one-shot RetroPie->modern-ESDE conversion (docstring lines 2-13), and it writes a recoverable backup theme.xml.retropie-original (lines 106-110) guarded by `if not os.path.exists(backup)` so re-runs never clobber the saved original. Loss of non-color customization is the deliberate purpose and is recoverable. Accurate as an observation but not a defect; severity info, not low.

contested.title: Sysfs uevent reads in the DolphinBar slot-ranking loops leak the file object until GC (open() without close, `for line in open(...): ... break`)
contested.description: _db_slots (tester_cmds.py:140) and the Tk _gp_db_slots (lib/mad_gamepad_tester.py:104) do `for line in open(f"/sys/class/hidraw/{base}/device/uevent"): ... break` — the file object is never explicitly closed and the `break` leaves it referenced by the loop iterator until CPython refcount-GC reclaims it. wii-monitor.py:23 and devices.py:510-511,539,543 have the same `open(...).read()` pattern. On CPython these collect promptly so it's effectively harmless (at most a ResourceWarning), but it's a real fd-hygiene gap that bites on any non-refcounting interpreter and is trivially fixable.
contested.impact: Negligible on CPython; transient extra open fds during a gamepads.list probe sweep. No functional effect.
contested.fix: Wrap each read in `with open(...) as f:` (e.g. `txt = Path(...).read_text()` then iterate), closing deterministically.
contested.evidence: The cited code matches: lib/madsrv/tester_cmds.py:140 `for line in open(f"/sys/class/hidraw/{base}/device/uevent"): ... break` (and a mirror at lib/mad_gamepad_tester.py:104) opens without an explicit close. BUT the 'leak the file object until GC' framing is wrong: in CPython the file's refcount drops to 0 the moment the for-loop's iterator reference is dropped (loop end), so __del__ closes it immediately. I tested the exact pattern over 2000 iterations: `fd before: 4 after: 4 delta: 0` — zero FD accumulation. The only artifact is a ResourceWarning, suppressed by default. Also the candidate says 'loops' (plural) at the single cited site tester_cmds.py:140 — there is exactly ONE such loop in that file. So: real style nit (should use `with open(...) as f:`), correctly rated info, but the 'leak' / 'until GC' wording overstates a benign immediate-refcount-close. Confirmed-as-style / leak-claim-refuted.

refuted.title: model2.set lets FullScreenWidth/Height be set to an unvalidated raw string, corrupting the resolution
refuted.evidence:
Mechanism exists but is NOT reachable in normal use, so the medium rating is unjustified.

Reachability (the only caller): the single client of model2.set is the local C++ GUI GuiMadPageModel2.cpp over local stdin/stdout pipes — NOT a network service. mad-backend.py header (lines 3-7) "Spawned by the panel ... with pipes on stdin/stdout"; grep for socket/AF_INET/AF_UNIX/bind/listen/HTTP in mad-backend.py and lib/madsrv/rpc.py returned nothing (no listener). rpc.dispatch (rpc.py:70-85) routes params straight from the local stdin JSON request.

What the GUI actually sends: GuiMadPageModel2.cpp addEnumStepper (line 158) sends key=="Resolution" with one of the preset "WxH" strings; it NEVER constructs key="FullScreenWidth"/"FullScreenHeight". model2.get (model2_cmds.py:173-183) folds width/height into the synthetic "Resolution" row and never emits FullScreenWidth/Height as their own settings rows, so the GUI has no widget that produces those keys.

The Resolution write path IS validated: model2_cmds.py:210 re.fullmatch(r"\s*(\d{2,5})\s*[xX]\s*(\d{2,5})\s*", str(value)); only digit groups w/h reach _set_key, then it returns early at line 222 — never touching the line-224 gate. EDITABLE_KEYS |= {"FullScreenWidth","FullScreenHeight"} (line 83) is the only thing that would let a hypothetical direct key="FullScreenWidth" call pass line 224, but no reachable caller does that.

Sandbox confirmation (in /tmp/_madrev, since removed): real writer run — Resolution path "1920x1080" correctly wrote FullScreenWidth=1920/Height=1080 preserving the inline ";" comment (atomic). Only by forging a direct {"key":"FullScreenWidth","value":"oops ; not a number"} (not GUI-reachable) did it write garbage: line became "FullScreenWidth=oops ; not a number   ; horizontal res". And the write is atomic (_atomic_write temp+os.replace, lines 154-158) with a one-time .bak (_ensure_bak, 147-151), so even that hypothetical is recoverable, not silent data loss.

Net: requires an attacker with local code-exec as deck injecting raw RPC — who could already edit EMULATOR.INI directly. Defense-in-depth nit at most (validate numeric keys regardless of caller). Refuted as a medium "corruption" bug; severity info.

refuted.title: reorganize-cd-games.py moves user ROMs via os.rename/shutil.move with no recovery manifest and a no-confirm --all
refuted.evidence:
The factual claims are partly true but the MEDIUM rating and implied data-loss are not supported. File: /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/reorganize-cd-games.py.

CONFIRMED FACTS: no manifest is written (grep shows only read-only open(...,"r") at lines 51,70 — `grep -n "RECOVERY|manifest|input(|confirm"` returns nothing), and there is no interactive confirm before --apply / --apply --all (argparse mutually-exclusive group, moves at lines 155-157 os.rename and 170 shutil.move).

WHY DOWNGRADED / DATA-LOSS REFUTED:
1) No deletion/overwrite. Files are MOVED into a sub-folder of the SAME system dir (e.g. psx/Game.m3u/Game.m3u) — the project-preferred "move not delete" pattern; destination is itself recoverable. Sandbox run (copy of script with ROMS_ROOT repointed to /tmp/_madrev): `--apply psx` produced Game.m3u/{Game.m3u, Game (Disc 1).cue, .bin, Disc2...} with NO .__moving leftovers; reversible by moving back out.
2) Same filesystem: /home/deck/ROMs -> /run/media/deck/1tbDeck/ROMs (symlink, SD card) so per-file renames are instant/atomic.
3) Overwrite guard at lines 167-168 (`if os.path.exists(dst): SKIP`) works — tested a deliberate track.bin collision against pre-existing GameA.cue/track.bin: PRECIOUS pre-existing content preserved, new file dropped into GameB.cue/ without clobbering. The finding ignored this guard.
4) `used` set (line 133, `f not in used`) prevents a shared track being claimed/lost twice — tested shared.bin between GameA.cue/GameB.cue: file survived intact in GameA.cue/.
5) NOT on any common path: `grep -rn reorganize-cd-games` across launchers tree, mad-backend.py, router-config-gui.py, lib/, ~/.emulationstation, ~/ES-DE finds ZERO callers; file is non-executable (-rw-r--r--), hand-run only, documented with --dry-run plan mode; --apply is explicit/opt-in.

Residual genuine but minor weakness (keeps it at low, not refuted-to-info): moves live ROMs with no logged manifest and --apply --all has no final confirm, so undoing a mis-targeted run is tedious (locate files in per-game folders). No data-loss path exists, so the medium/critical framing is unwarranted.

refuted.title: set_system_flag computes the 'revert' default from the system's own base entry without resolving inherits
refuted.evidence: lib/madsrv/policy_cmds.py:43-48 does read the system's OWN base entry without resolving inherits (factually true). BUT this is correct-by-design, not a bug: (1) The revert is meant to 'mirror the Systems detail page' (docstring), and the detail page lib/madsrv/systems_cmds.py:208 `ent = merged.get('systems', {}).get(sysname, {})` ALSO does not resolve inherits for the displayed toggle value — so revert is consistent with what the page shows. (2) In the shipped controller-policy.toml the only inherits targets are arcade/nes/snes (`grep inherits | uniq -c` → arcade x10, nes x1, snes x22). Of these, nes/snes set only `warn_when_only_xarcade=true`, whose set_system_flag revert default (`flag.startswith('warn_')`=True) COINCIDENTALLY equals the inherited value; arcade sets no warn flag. The systems with non-default flags (wii `require_dolphinbar=true`, openbor/switch/wiiu `router_skip=true`, openbor `warn_when_only_xarcade=false`) are NOT inherited by any child. (3) The detail page only renders require_*/router_skip toggles for a child if the child has its OWN override (systems_cmds.py:224 `if flag in ent or sysname=='wii'`), so an inherited non-default flag never even surfaces a toggle on a child. No reachable user-facing inconsistency. Latent at most.

refuted.title: set_backend_list_member is_int branch sorts a mixed-type list instead of coercing to int
refuted.evidence: lib/madsrv/policy_cmds.py:187-188 `if params.get('is_int'): cur = sorted(set(cur))`. The 'mixed-type sort' never occurs: is_int=True is passed by exactly one caller (router-config-gui.py:3982) for `manage_ports` slot toggles where `member` is `slot` from range(8) (an int). The current list comes from merged backends manage_ports, which in controller-policy.toml:350/398 is `[1,2,3,4]` / `4` — all ints (TOML loads them as int). So cur is a homogeneous list of ints, `sorted(set(cur))` cannot TypeError. The RPC method is a verbatim port of the Tk App._set_list_member (router-config-gui.py:4074-4087) — identical logic, so it is not a regression. The `is_int` name is a misnomer (it dedupe-sorts, never coerces) but the behavior is correct for the actual data. No bug.

refuted.title: localpolicy.dump silently drops any top-level NON-dict value from the overrides file
refuted.evidence: lib/localpolicy.py:77-86 dump() iterates data.items() and only emits `if isinstance(tbl, dict)`, so a TOP-LEVEL non-dict value would be dropped (factually true). BUT no code path ever produces a top-level scalar: every dump() caller builds `data` from localpolicy.load(LOCAL) then sets only nested/dict top-level keys — `data.setdefault('systems'|'backends'|'quit_combo'|'hardware', {})` and the only two direct top-level assignments are `data['pins'] = tbl` (tbl is a dict, policy_cmds.py:110-121 / gui:3269) and nested `data['quit_combo'][...]`. The module docstring (lines 4-7) explicitly scopes the file to 'a flat top-level table plus dotted sub-tables', i.e. all top-level values ARE tables by design. So the drop is unreachable. Latent robustness gap only (would matter only if a future top-level scalar key were introduced); not a live bug.

refuted.title: Nav-sound playback leaks zombie child processes for the GUI's lifetime
refuted.evidence: gui_sound.py:104-108 does subprocess.Popen(cmd,...) fire-and-forget with no .wait()/.poll(). The title's claim 'leaks zombie child processes for the GUI's lifetime' is INACCURATE. Verified on the Deck's Python 3.13.1: subprocess._active is a list and subprocess._cleanup() exists; every new Popen() call reaps previously-finished children. Reproduced: after 5 unwaited Popen('true') there were 3 transient zombies, and creating one more Popen reaped them down to 1 (the just-created one's predecessors), with subprocess._active tracking references. Player processes (pw-play/paplay) are short-lived and Sound.play() is called on every nav tick, so any zombie is reaped on the very next play() (or any Popen anywhere in-process). The ONLY persistent case is play exactly one sound then never navigate again -> a single benign transient zombie, not an accumulating leak. Mechanism as described is wrong; downgrade to info.

refuted.title: supermodel-proton.sh restore() trap: `&&...||` precedence can skip the X-Arcade reattach
refuted.evidence: supermodel-proton.sh:63-67 uses the standard guarded idiom `[ -n A ] && [ -n B ] && cmd || true`. Bash left-associates && / || at equal precedence: when the guards are non-empty the reattach runs; `|| true` fires ONLY when a guard is empty (the intended skip — nothing to reattach) or when xinput itself fails (intentional swallow so the next restore line still runs). I reproduced this in /tmp: with both vars set the reattach executed; with XARCADE_PTR_SLAVE empty it correctly skipped; when the reattach command failed the subsequent P2 lines still ran. The X-Arcade reattach is never *wrongly* skipped. The only asymmetry (float at line 57 guards on XARCADE_PTR_SLAVE only, restore at 64 also requires CORE_PTR) needs the 'Virtual core pointer' to be absent, which never happens on a live X server. No bug.

refuted.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py rebuild_block silently drops gamelist tags that aren't simple <word>...</word> pairs
refuted.evidence: The regex at steam-fetch-metadata.py:179 `<(\w+)>(.*?)</\1>` only fails on self-closing or attribute-bearing tags. ES-DE never emits those: MetaData.cpp:127-149 appendToXML writes EVERY metadata field — including booleans favorite/kidgame/hidden — via `parent.append_child(name).text().set(value)` (line 146), which produces `<favorite>true</favorite>` text-content pairs, never `<favorite/>`. All ES-DE metadata keys are plain [A-Za-z0-9_] (MetaData.cpp key table: altemulator, collectionsortname, kidgame, playcount, etc.) so every one matches \w+. The live gamelist (/home/deck/ES-DE/gamelists/steam/gamelist.xml) contains only simple pairs (desc, developer, genre, lastplayed, name, path, playcount, players, playtime, publisher, rating, releasedate); grep for self-closing/attribute tags returned nothing. I ran the actual rebuild_block on a real block with all the stat tags: every tag (rating/playcount/lastplayed/players/playtime) was preserved via the `order + [t for t,_ in tags if t not in order]` loop (line 187). The drop only manifests for tags ES-DE does not write (a hand-edited <kidgame/> would be lost — I reproduced that), so it is a theoretical fragility, not a real-use defect. Also line 217 writes a timestamped .bak before any change. Refuted as a real bug; info at most.

refuted.title: devices.unwatch / devices.watch have an unsynchronized token swap that can leak a hotplug watcher thread or drop a live one
refuted.evidence: device_cmds.py:150 `@method("devices.watch")` and :159 `@method("devices.unwatch")` are declared WITHOUT slow=True. Per rpc.py dispatch (lines 81-85), non-slow methods run inline via _run() on the single stdin thread, serially; only slow=True methods go to the 4-worker ThreadPool (line 83). Two watch/unwatch calls therefore cannot interleave, so the unsynchronized _WATCH token swap (device_cmds.py:147-163) is unreachable as a race. No leaked/dropped watcher possible. Refuted on reachability.

refuted.title: quit-combo-watcher / wiimote-quit-watcher run a policy-derived quit_cmd via shell=True
refuted.evidence: quit-combo-watcher.py:120 and wiimote-quit-watcher.py:119 run the quit command via subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True). The command originates from the user's own trusted local config: es_systems.py:158-171 resolves it from `[backends.<backend>].quit_cmd` in controller-policy.toml(.local), passed through controller-router.py:467-474. The value intentionally contains shell syntax (e.g. 'flatpak kill app; pkill -TERM -f app', see usage example quit-combo-watcher.py:21), so shell=True is required by design and the `.replace('-TERM','-KILL')` backstop transform (line 111) needs it too. Not untrusted input, not a security hole. By-design; severity info, not a bug.

refuted.title: WiiTesterStream STOP->START restart can leak the previous tester's hidraw slot reader (cleanup runs after the new run() already claimed it)
refuted.evidence: tester_cmds.py: self.reader is per-instance (init :871, assigned :891, stopped only in own cleanup :964-966). A STOP->START's new WiiTesterStream gets its OWN reader, so the old cleanup's self.reader.stop() (line 965) stops the OLD reader, never the new one — no reader leak/double-stop. The slot-file hand-back is guarded by comparing the file to self._claimed_node (lines 971-973): if the new run() already rewrote it, the compare fails and the old cleanup does NOT unlink it. Additionally tester.start (:980, slow=True) stops the prior stream and sleeps 0.2s (lines 985-987) before constructing the new one. The exact race in the title is explicitly handled and commented (lines 967-969). Refuted.

refuted.title: RunFullStream releases _RUN_ACTIVE in run()'s finally even when start() failed — relies on start() exception path to compensate, fragile invariant
refuted.evidence: backup_cmds.py:46 _RUN_ACTIVE is a threading.Lock. acquire() is in _backup_run_full (line 181); the two release() sites are mutually exclusive: if RunFullStream(argv).start() (Stream.start, rpc.py:110-112 = just _thread.start()+return) SUCCEEDS, run()'s finally releases exactly once (line 154) and the except (line 190) is never reached; if start() raises (thread never started, so run() never executes), the except releases (line 190) and run()'s finally never runs. No path releases twice, so the RuntimeError on releasing an unlocked Lock cannot occur. The invariant is sound, not fragile. Refuted as a bug; at most an info-level style note.

refuted.title: X-Arcade tester opens nodes then grabs in a loop; a grab failure after opening earlier nodes is handled, but a partial-open with zero opened leaves no input.lock and silently no-ops
refuted.evidence: tester_cmds.py:778-781 — on zero opened nodes it does NOT silently no-op: it emits {"ended":"no_device","message":"No X-Arcade nodes found — is it connected and in Xbox mode?"} then returns. self.locked is initialized False at line 659; the early return at 781 happens BEFORE self.locked=True at 805, so cleanup() (lines 844-847, guarded by `if self.locked`) correctly does NOT emit a bogus input.lock unlock — there was no lock to leave because no panel was ever locked. cleanup() is GUARANTEED to run regardless: rpc.py:114-126 _guarded() wraps run() in try/finally that always calls cleanup() and ungrabs/closes. The partial-grab case (some open, some grab-fail) is also handled: line 768 increments `failed`, 807 reports `grab_failed:failed` to the GUI. The candidate's premise (silent no-op, missing/stuck lock) is wrong on every count.

refuted.title: steam-fetch-metadata.py MEDIA dir is hardcoded to /run/media/deck/1tbDeck — silently mis-targets on a differently-named card
refuted.evidence: `ls -d /run/media/deck/1tbDeck` => EXISTS (drwxr-xr-x deck deck, Jun 13). The card on THIS machine IS named 1tbDeck. /run/media/deck/1tbDeck is a documented project constant (CLAUDE.md: 'the SD card /run/media/deck/1tbDeck') hardcoded identically across the entire repo — openbor-*.py, steam-*.py, skyscraper-apply.py, clean-manual-cruft.py, deck-restore.sh, install-bezels*.sh all use the same literal. This is the user's fixed single-machine layout, not a bug. Worst case on a hypothetical renamed card is a loud mkdir failure at steam-fetch-metadata.py:200 (parents=True under a missing /run/media/deck/<name>), not silent corruption. Over-rated; info at most.

refuted.title: daphne.build_index passes an RPC-supplied arg as a ROM path segment (path traversal)
refuted.evidence: Not a security hole. mad-backend.py:5 — the JSON-RPC transport is stdin/stdout PIPES spawned by ES-DE's local MadBackend.cpp; there is NO network socket (no AF_INET/bind/listen). The only producer of `arg` is the local trusted MAD GUI: mad_daphne_page.py:96/99 supplies a real Daphne folder name (_dp_game[0].name) or the literal 'all'. daphne_cmds.py:282 passes it via sinden_cmds._detached which uses subprocess.Popen([str(a) for a in argv]) (sinden_cmds.py:56) — an argv LIST, NOT shell=True — so no command injection. Inside singe-indexer.sh it becomes `index_game "$ROMS/$arg"`; index_game (line 52) is read-only-ish: it requires `[ -d "$gd" ]`, requires the framefile to exist, and only runs hypseus + writes .dat files where hypseus seeks + a script to $WORK. It never deletes or overwrites arbitrary user paths. A '..' segment at worst points hypseus at a wrong/nonexistent dir (guarded, no-op). 'Path traversal' here has no exploitable consequence and the input is local-trusted; mis-rated.

refuted.title: art.resolve resolves attacker-controlled relative names with no path-traversal containment
refuted.evidence: systems_cmds.py:79-93 resolve_art does p=base/nm then p.is_file(); no '..' containment — literally true. But the 'attacker-controlled' security framing is unfounded: (1) the `names` are emitted by the local ES-DE C++ panel itself (es-app/src/guis/mad/GuiMadPanel.cpp:220-245 builds 'icons/'+artKey+'.png'), not a remote/untrusted source; (2) transport is same-user stdin/stdout pipes (mad-backend.py:5 'Spawned by the panel ... with pipes on stdin/stdout'; grep for socket/AF_UNIX/AF_INET/bind/listen in madsrv/*.py + mad-backend.py = none); (3) resolve_art only returns a PATH STRING (line 92 `return str(p)`) — it never reads or serves the file, GuiMadPanel just loads it as an icon. An 'attacker' able to speak this pipe already executes as user `deck` and can read any file directly, so traversal grants nothing. No security issue; at most a cosmetic 'could add containment' note. Severity info, framing refuted.

refuted.title: Eden/xemu blindly trust section_body parse; an existing [Controls]/[input.bindings] absence silently produces a fresh section
refuted.evidence:
eden_cfg.py:167 `body = inifile.section_body(text, 'Controls') or ''`. Reproduced in /tmp with inifile: text='[UI]
foo=bar
'; section_body(text,'Controls') -> None -> ''; after assign-style set_section the output is '[UI]
foo=bar
[Controls]
player_0_connected=false

'. So when [Controls] is absent the code correctly synthesizes a fresh, deterministic section AND preserves the rest of the file byte-for-byte (the [UI]/foo=bar block is retained). This is exactly the documented design (module docstring eden_cfg.py:15-18 'one device-agnostic template ... Unused players are marked disconnected ... we edit while it's closed'; inifile.py:14-29 set_section appends if missing). No dropped user keys, no data loss — a fresh section on a genuinely-absent section is the intended outcome, not a silent bug. Refuted.

refuted.title: wii-nav-bridge rescan re-arm cooldown only applies to slots not currently in self.readers
refuted.evidence: wii-nav-bridge.py rescan() lines 283-300: a slot only gets `self._disarmed[node] = monotonic()+REARM_COOLDOWN_SEC` inside `if node not in self.readers` (i.e. a newly (re)appearing slot). This is the CORRECT intended behavior, not a defect: a slot that stays continuously present is never popped from self.readers, so it must NOT be re-disarmed each 4s rescan (RESCAN_SEC=71, REARM_COOLDOWN_SEC=76 lines) — re-arming a live, already-armed slot would needlessly suppress real input. The comment at 299-300 ('Gate until live+neutral AND >= cooldown (held-button leak)') and the consume logic at 387-399 confirm the cooldown is specifically for fresh/reappearing slots to swallow the +-hold exit-gesture bounce. A removed slot is popped (288-293) and only re-added via the `want` loop, which always sets the cooldown. No gap. The candidate merely re-describes correct behavior as if it were a bug. Refuted.

refuted.title: Per-system [quit_combo.*] overrides for router_skip/HID systems (daphne, ps3, ps2) are dead config the watcher never consults for those systems
refuted.description: The live local file defines [quit_combo.ps2], [quit_combo.ps3], and [quit_combo.daphne], each = [314, 315] (identical to the global [quit_combo] default at lines 127-129). quit-combo-watcher._read_quit_combo (quit-combo-watcher.py:63-82) only applies a per-system override when the watcher is actually started with --system <name>. For daphne the base policy (controller-policy.toml:83-91) sets backend='hypseus' + router_skip=true and the docstring/es_systems.quit_cmd route Hypseus quit through hypseus-pin.sh's own pkill quit_cmd, NOT quit-combo-watcher, so [quit_combo.daphne] is never read. For ps2/ps3 these per-system tables merely restate the global default, so they are no-ops. I verified all three files parse with no duplicate keys and that the watcher's independent 2-level merge (base then local, local wins) is correct.
refuted.impact: No functional effect today (the values equal the default). It is dead/redundant config that can mislead a future editor into thinking daphne's quit is tunable here when it is actually owned by hypseus-pin.sh. If the user ever changes [quit_combo.daphne] expecting it to take effect, it will silently do nothing.
refuted.fix: Drop [quit_combo.daphne] (Hypseus quit is hypseus-pin.sh's quit_cmd, not the combo watcher) and the ps2/ps3 tables that only duplicate the global default; keep only genuinely-different per-system overrides. This is GUI-written state, so the cleanup belongs in the GUI's quit-combo save path, not a hand-edit.
refuted.evidence: Claim is factually wrong. router_skip only gates INPUT routing (controller-router.py:315, the 05-controller-router-standalone hook), NOT the quit-combo watcher. The watcher is started by /home/deck/ES-DE/scripts/game-start/quit-combo-watcher.sh, which runs iff `controller-router.py quit-cmd <SYSTEM>` is non-empty (line 15-16) and passes `--system $SYSTEM` (line 22-23). I executed es_systems.quit_cmd for all three: daphne -> "pkill -TERM -f '[h]ypseus.real'; sleep 1; ..."; ps3 -> "flatpak kill net.rpcs3.RPCS3 ..."; ps2 -> "pkill -TERM -f pcsx2"; and all three return True from quit_combo_systems() (the eligible list). The watcher's _read_quit_combo(system) (quit-combo-watcher.py:71-72) then reads `[quit_combo.<system>]` and applies it. So the per-system overrides for daphne/ps2/ps3 ARE consulted, not dead. (They happen to be redundant in value — same [314,315] as the global — but that is a separate, non-bug observation.)

refuted.title: wii-nav-bridge resumes DolphinBar writes immediately for runInBackground launches, contending with the game that owns the Wii Remotes
refuted.description: The deck-patches Wii-bridge gating wraps launchGameUnix with MadWiiBridge::pause() / resume() so the bridge stops writing to the DolphinBar hidraw slots while a game owns the remotes (FileData.cpp:2201-2202 states the bridge must NEVER write to those slots while a game owns them). But for a runInBackground launch, launchGameUnix(command, startDirectory, runInBackground=true) returns IMMEDIATELY while the game is still running (the surrounding code at FileData.cpp:2172,2230,2250 all branches on runInBackground precisely because the call doesn't block). So the sequence becomes pause() -> launch returns instantly -> resume() while the backgrounded game is still alive and using the remotes. The inline comment at line 2203 dismisses this as 'a harmless no-op there,' but it is not harmless: resume() re-arms the bridge's writes to the same DolphinBar slots the live background game is reading — exactly the cross-process hidraw contention the patch exists to prevent (the same class as wave-1 [10.1]/[7.3]). runInBackground is set when the es_systems.xml command carries %RUNINBACKGROUND% or the global RunInBackground setting is on (FileData.cpp:1058-1071). The DEINIT_ON_LAUNCH build forces runInBackground=false (line 2109) and would avoid this, but CMakeLists.txt:49 defaults DEINIT_ON_LAUNCH=OFF and the es-app build cache shows it undefined, so this deck takes the vulnerable non-deinit path.
refuted.impact: A game launched in the background that drives the Wii Remotes through the DolphinBar (e.g. a background-launched Dolphin, or a Steam shortcut using the remotes) will fight the wii-nav-bridge for the hidraw slots for its entire run: dropped/echoed Wiimote input in-game and/or stray ES-DE navigation from the bridge re-reading slots the game expects to own. Narrow trigger (needs a runInBackground game that actually uses the DolphinBar), so medium, not high.
refuted.fix: Skip the resume() until the background game actually exits, or do not pause/resume at all for runInBackground launches (keep the bridge paused for the background game's lifetime, resumed only when ES-DE regains focus / the game exits). At minimum, gate resume() on !runInBackground so the bridge stays paused while a background game still owns the remotes. The current 'harmless no-op' comment is wrong and should not be relied on.
refuted.evidence:
Code premise is partly real but the IMPACT is unreachable on this deck. FileData.cpp:2205-2207 wraps launchGameUnix(command,startDir,runInBackground) between pause()/resume(); for runInBackground=true launchGameUnix returns immediately, so resume() does fire while a backgrounded game is alive. resume() is NOT a no-op: MadWiiBridge.cpp:113-117 resume()->writeLine("resume\n"); wii-nav-bridge.py:342-345 handle_command("resume") calls self.rescan() which re-opens the DolphinBar slot readers (NavSlotReader). So the inline "harmless no-op" comment is technically inaccurate for the resume side.

BUT the contention impact requires a runInBackground game that drives the DolphinBar, which does not exist here:
- `grep -c RUNINBACKGROUND /home/deck/ES-DE/custom_systems/es_systems.xml` = 0 (no per-command flag anywhere in the ACTIVE config).
- Global flag off: es_settings.xml:49 `<bool name="RunInBackground" value="false" />`.
- The only DolphinBar/Wii-Remote path is the bundled-default `wii` system (appears because ~/ROMs/wii exists): es_systems linux default lines 2295-2304 -> Dolphin / Dolphin(Standalone) / PrimeHack commands, NONE carry %RUNINBACKGROUND%. The active `gc` system (es_systems.xml:236-247) uses controller-router-wrap.sh, no %RUNINBACKGROUND%.
- The bundled commands that DO use %RUNINBACKGROUND% are systems: desktop(554), emulators(642), epic(654), kodi(962), steam(2002), windows*(2315/2327/2342) — none drive the DolphinBar Wii Remotes.
- DEINIT_ON_LAUNCH claim verified true (CMakeCache.txt: DEINIT_ON_LAUNCH:BOOL=OFF; CMakeLists.txt:49 default OFF), so the non-deinit branch is compiled — but that only matters with a reachable trigger, which is absent.

Net: real code mechanism, but gated behind a configuration the user has not created (no background-launched DolphinBar game, global flag off). No data loss, no crash, no reachable contention on this deck. medium overrated; downgraded to low latent/code-quality (the misleading comment). Impactful framing refuted.

refuted.title: gamepads.list LIVE DolphinBar probe writes report-mode bytes into hidraw slots the wii-nav-bridge holds open and is reading — unguarded cross-process contention (distinct call site from the wave-1 device_cmds finding)
refuted.description: _gamepads_list (slow method) opens its OWN fd to each DolphinBar slot node and writes RPT_STATUS + the full EXT_F0/EXT_FB/EXT_ID/SET_MODE init sequence (via `_wii_probe_kind(fd, quiet=False)`, lines 194-195). When not in a game the wii-nav-bridge (wii-nav-bridge.py) holds those same slot nodes open via NavSlotReader threads and is actively reading/keepalive-writing them. The only guard here is `if _active["stream"] is not None: return` (line 186), which suppresses the probe while a *tester stream* runs — it does NOT account for the always-on nav bridge. The bridge only releases a slot named in the tester-slot CLAIM file (wii-nav-bridge.py:260-281), and gamepads.list never writes that file. So two fds on the same hidraw simultaneously write conflicting report-mode/extension-init bytes and both read. This is the same class as wave-1 [10.1] (device_cmds.py:121) but a separate, unflagged call site (the Gamepad picker refresh, which can run repeatedly).
refuted.impact: Each picker open/refresh that hits a live slot re-inits the remote's extension and resets its report mode under the nav bridge's feet, stalling/glitching Wii-Remote menu navigation until the bridge's next 0.5s keepalive re-asserts the mode (and momentarily corrupting the bridge's extension-kind detection). Navigation stutter, not data loss.
refuted.fix: Have gamepads.list honor the same claim mechanism the tester uses (write the tester-slot claim file for the slot being probed, or skip live probing of any slot the nav bridge currently owns), or move the probe behind a shared inter-process slot lock so only one reader touches a slot at a time.
refuted.evidence:
The concurrency the finding describes is real and reachable (no game + no tester claim => bridge holds slot, picker open => gamepads.list probes the same node, tester_cmds.py:188-202), and the "distinct call site from wave-1" claim is factually correct (device_cmds.py:121 calls dv.dolphinbar_wiimotes(active=True), a different probe path). BUT the finding's core framing — "unguarded cross-process contention" that "resets report mode under the bridge's feet" — is refuted by the actual code, which shows this is a deliberately-engineered, documented co-read design with purpose-built mitigations:

1) The probe is called with quiet=False (tester_cmds.py:195, 1040). _wii_probe_kind only writes the quiet/non-continuous mode 0x12 0x00 0x30 when quiet=True (lines 120-122); with quiet=False it leaves SET_MODE = 0x12 0x04 0x32 set — the IDENTICAL continuous mode the bridge itself uses (wii_slot_reader.py:30). Docstring lines 89-91: "quiet=False leaves CONTINUOUS reporting set: when the wii-nav-bridge co-reads the slot, dropping to quiet mode would stall its navigation." So the probe does NOT reset report mode under the bridge's feet — it leaves the bridge's preferred mode.

2) NavSlotReader (wii-nav-bridge.py:202-206) exists solely to halve the keepalive to 0.5s (vs base WiiSlotReader.KEEPALIVE=1.5): docstring "Snappier keepalive than the tester's: the MAD pages' periodic probes (gamepads.list / wii.probe_ext) re-write the slot's report mode and would otherwise stall navigation for up to the keepalive interval." This is an explicit mitigation naming gamepads.list by name.

3) The bridge's _handle is robust to interleaved writes: re-sets mode on EVERY 0x20 status report (wii_slot_reader.py:158) and re-inits the extension ONLY on a real ext-bit change (line 152), requiring the A4 20 signature (line 169).

4) The co-read architecture is documented as intentional: deck-docs/wiimote.md:77 ("DOLPHIN does the init first, then we co-read") and deck-docs/mad-backend-protocol.md:243 ("Leaves CONTINUOUS reporting set (the wii-nav-bridge co-reads)").

Residual risk: a brief (<=0.5s, self-correcting) navigation/accessory-detection blip for nunchuk/classic remotes only (per wiimote.md:100-103, mid-stream ext re-init momentarily wipes the accessory stick/buttons), which is exactly the "navigation stutter, not data loss" the finding itself states. That is the post-mitigation residual, not an unguarded defect. The medium "unguarded contention" rating is overstated; downgraded to low. No data loss, no crash, no security issue.

criticFindings.title: deck-post-update.sh runs samba-setup.sh as the user, but samba-setup.sh requires root and exits — Samba is never reinstalled after a SteamOS update
criticFindings.description: deck-post-update.sh is written to run AS THE USER and elevate individual commands with sudo (10 internal `sudo` calls; no root assertion at top; header says 'Needs sudo for the root bits'). Step 1/9 calls `bash "$T/samba-setup.sh"` with NO sudo. But samba-setup.sh line 16 is `[[ $EUID -eq 0 ]] || { echo "Run with sudo..."; exit 1; }` and uses BARE privileged commands (steamos-readonly/pacman/install/systemctl/smbpasswd), so it immediately exits 1 when invoked as the user. The caller catches that with `|| log "  samba-setup.sh returned nonzero"` and continues to '=== done ===', reporting overall success. Contrast: the very next step (sinden-reinstall-deps.sh, line 133, also called without sudo) self-elevates via 11 internal `sudo` calls and works fine — proving the script's design is user-invocation + per-command sudo, which samba-setup.sh alone violates.
criticFindings.impact: The #1 advertised job of the post-SteamOS-update recovery (re-installing the update-wiped Samba file sharing) silently never happens. The user follows the documented 'after every SteamOS update, run deck-post-update.sh' flow, sees a 'done' banner, but loses SMB file sharing with only a buried 'returned nonzero' log line. esde-health-check.sh keeps nagging (check_missing() greps `command -v smbd` at line 49), and that nag NEVER clears because the fix the dialog points to can't repair it.
criticFindings.fix: In deck-post-update.sh line 129, call samba-setup.sh with elevation: `sudo bash "$T/samba-setup.sh"` (matching how the rest of the script elevates per-command). OR make samba-setup.sh self-elevate like sinden-reinstall-deps.sh (re-exec itself via `sudo` if EUID!=0, and prefix its privileged commands with sudo). Verify on-device: run deck-post-update.sh as `deck`, then `command -v smbd` / `systemctl status smb`.

criticFindings.title: samba-setup.sh disables read-only root and never re-enables it — leaves the immutable SteamOS root writable
criticFindings.line: 22 (steamos-readonly disable); no matching 'steamos-readonly enable' anywhere in the file
criticFindings.description: samba-setup.sh line 22 runs `steamos-readonly disable || true` to allow the pacman install, but the script has NO `steamos-readonly enable` at the end (confirmed: grep for 'enable' matches only the systemctl line). Both sibling scripts that touch the immutable root balance it correctly: install.sh disables at :146 and re-enables at :151; sinden-reinstall-deps.sh disables at :21 and re-enables at :80. samba-setup.sh is the outlier. (It is independently broken via deck-post-update — see the high finding — but is run directly per its own header `sudo bash ~/Emulation/tools/samba-setup.sh` and by deck-backup.sh:145.)
criticFindings.impact: After running samba-setup.sh directly (the documented invocation), the SteamOS A/B immutable root is left mounted read-write until the next reboot/update. That defeats the OS's tamper/corruption protection, lets stray writes land on the root that the next OS update will silently wipe, and can confuse later steamos-readonly state checks. Low blast radius but a real deviation from the project's 'system root is immutable' model.
criticFindings.fix: Append `steamos-readonly enable || true` at the end of samba-setup.sh (after step 6), mirroring sinden-reinstall-deps.sh:80 and install.sh:151. Ideally guard it so it only re-enables if the script disabled it.

criticFindings.title: sinden-mpx-setup.sh hardcodes /dev/input/event27 for the P2 keyboard node instead of resolving a stable symlink
criticFindings.description: The P2 mouse node is resolved correctly via the udev symlink `/dev/input/sinden-gun-p2-event` (line 24). But the P2 keyboard node falls back to a literal `/dev/input/event27` (line 69). The 99-sinden-lightgun.rules file (confirmed: it only creates SYMLINK for js* and ENV{ID_INPUT_MOUSE} interfaces, never the keyboard interface) provides no stable name for the gun's keyboard endpoint, and this script's own header plus sinden-start.sh:95-97 explicitly warn that `/dev/input/eventN` numbers shift when devices are added/removed/reordered. So `event27` is fragile: after any topology change it points at the wrong device (or a non-Sinden device) and find_slave_by_node won't match.
criticFindings.impact: Bounded: the code comment (66-68) notes the P2-keyboard reattach is 'not strictly required for Dolphin' and it fails gracefully (readlink -e returns empty if event27 isn't the keyboard → find_slave_by_node returns nothing → skip). So worst case the P2 gun's keyboard slave silently stays merged into the Virtual core keyboard rather than being separated, with no error. No crash, no data loss.
criticFindings.fix: Add a udev SYMLINK for the keyboard interface in 99-sinden-lightgun.rules (e.g. match ATTRS{idProduct}=="0f39" with ENV{ID_INPUT_KEYBOARD}=="1" -> SYMLINK+="input/sinden-gun-p2-kbd") and resolve that symlink here, mirroring the mouse path at line 24.

criticFindings.title: clean-manual-cruft.py --apply can silently clobber one recovered manual when two leftover-extension PDFs map to the same target
criticFindings.line: 60 (existence check at scan time) and 69-70 (shutil.move recover loop)
criticFindings.description: The recover list is built per-system before any move; each entry's target is guarded by `not (mandir / f"{inner.stem}.pdf").exists()` (line 60) — evaluated at SCAN time. If a manuals dir holds two leftover-extension PDFs for the same game (e.g. `Game.bin.pdf` and `Game.cue.pdf`, both with inner.stem=='Game', both valid, with no existing `Game.pdf`), BOTH get appended to `recover` with the same destination `Game.pdf`. On --apply (line 69-70) the first `shutil.move` creates `Game.pdf`; the second `shutil.move` OVERWRITES it with no recovery — violating the project's never-overwrite/move-to-_TMP rule. The MOVE branch correctly routes to _TMP, but the RECOVER branch is a direct destructive rename.
criticFindings.impact: Narrow (requires two same-stem multi-format manual files), and both inputs were 'wrong-named cruft' to begin with, so the practical loss is one redundant copy. Still a silent overwrite of a user file on the --apply path, against the house rule.
criticFindings.fix: Detect duplicate recover targets (group by dst) and route all-but-one to the _TMP move branch, or skip recover and move both to _TMP when a target collision is detected. Alternatively re-check `dst.exists()` immediately before each shutil.move and divert to _TMP if it now exists.

criticFindings.title: supermodel-proton.sh leaves the X-Arcade pointer floated if proton is SIGKILLed (trap bypassed)
criticFindings.line: 59 (xinput float), 63-68 (restore trap on EXIT INT TERM)
criticFindings.description: Before launching, the script `xinput float`s the X-Arcade pointer (line 59) to keep its trackball off the crosshair, and reattaches it to the core pointer via a `restore()` trap on EXIT/INT/TERM (63-68). SIGKILL (e.g. the policy's KILL-escalation quit path, an OOM kill, or `kill -9`) bypasses the trap, so the X-Arcade trackball is left detached from every master pointer with no automatic recovery until reboot or a manual `xinput reattach`. The Sinden P2 reattach has the same exposure but matters less (it returns to its MPX master, also floated effectively).
criticFindings.impact: After a hard-killed Supermodel session, the X-Arcade trackball stops moving any cursor system-wide (floated) until the user reboots or manually reattaches — surprising for a non-technical user. SIGKILL-bypassing-trap is inherent to shell, but a self-healing reattach at the next launch would close the gap.
criticFindings.fix: At the START of supermodel-proton.sh (and/or in a shared sinden/x-arcade setup step run on every game launch), reattach any floating X-Arcade/Sinden slaves back to their masters before floating again — so a previous crashed session self-heals. Optionally drop a sentinel file and reattach it on the next router setup hook.

criticFindings.title: deck-post-update.sh reinstalls python-evdev/tk with pacman but never disables the read-only SteamOS root — the documented MAD recovery step fails after every OS update
criticFindings.description: Step 7/9 runs `sudo pacman -S --needed --noconfirm python-evdev tk` to restore the MAD GUI's wiped deps, but deck-post-update.sh never calls `sudo steamos-readonly disable` first. On SteamOS the immutable root is read-only by default right after an update (exactly when this script runs), so pacman fails with a read-only-filesystem error and the deps are NOT reinstalled. The sibling scripts that do the same kind of install handle this correctly: sinden-reinstall-deps.sh line 21 (`sudo steamos-readonly disable` ... line 80 re-enable) and install.sh line 146 both wrap pacman in readonly-disable/enable. deck-post-update.sh does not. Note step 2 calls sinden-reinstall-deps.sh which disables readonly then RE-ENABLES it at its end, so by the time step 7 runs the root is read-only again.
criticFindings.impact: After a SteamOS update wipes python-evdev/tk (a pacman-on-root scenario the script's own header lists as the reason it exists), the documented recovery (`deck-post-update.sh`, per CLAUDE.md rule 6) silently fails to restore the MAD panel's Python deps. MAD.sh / router-config-gui.py then won't launch (no tkinter/evdev) and the user is told only 'reinstalled but still failing — check pacman keyring' (line 203), pointing at the wrong cause. Real bug on the central recovery path.
criticFindings.fix: Wrap the step-7 pacman in `sudo steamos-readonly disable` before and `sudo steamos-readonly enable` after (mirroring install.sh:146-151), or run the install via sinden-reinstall-deps.sh's pattern. Also re-init the keyring if empty before this pacman since step 2 only inits it when mono is absent.

criticFindings.title: deck-post-update.sh runs samba-setup.sh without sudo, but that script hard-exits unless EUID==0 — Samba is never actually restored after a SteamOS update
criticFindings.description: Step 1/9 of the post-update recovery invokes `bash "$T/samba-setup.sh"` with NO sudo. samba-setup.sh line 16 is `[[ $EUID -eq 0 ]] || { echo "Run with sudo: sudo bash $0"; exit 1; }`, so when run as the `deck` user it immediately prints 'Run with sudo' and exits 1. deck-post-update.sh just swallows that as `|| log "  samba-setup.sh returned nonzero"` and moves on. Unlike sinden-reinstall-deps.sh (step 2), which uses per-command internal `sudo` and works fine without an outer sudo, samba-setup.sh requires the WHOLE script to be root. So step 1 is a guaranteed no-op every time post-update is run by the (non-root) user that the rest of the script's individual `sudo` calls assume.
criticFindings.impact: Samba file sharing — listed by the script header as item 1 of what a SteamOS update wipes (root pacman) — is never reinstalled by the documented recovery tool. The user believes post-update restored sharing; it didn't. They must separately know to run `sudo bash ~/Emulation/tools/samba-setup.sh`.
criticFindings.fix: Call it with sudo from post-update (`sudo bash "$T/samba-setup.sh"`), or refactor samba-setup.sh to use per-command sudo like sinden-reinstall-deps.sh instead of gating the whole script on EUID==0.

criticFindings.title: MAD backend load_merged() crashes on a malformed base controller-policy.toml — taking down EVERY panel page (distinct file/surface from the router-path findings)
criticFindings.description: load_merged() does `base = tomllib.load(POLICY.open("rb"))` with NO try/except (the local-overrides load below it IS guarded; the base is not). A TOML syntax error in controller-policy.toml raises TOMLDecodeError straight out of load_merged(). Verified by sandbox test in /tmp: feeding a corrupt base file makes load_merged() raise TOMLDecodeError. This is the DAEMON-side analogue of the confirmed router-path findings ([0.1]/[12.4], which cite routing.py:48-50 and the game-launch path). It is a separate file and a separate failure surface: load_merged() is called by policy.merged, by device_cmds._scan() (line 63: `xport = xarcade_port(load_merged())`, which devices.scan invokes), and by virtually every page-render RPC. policy_cmds.py:43 (`tomllib.load(open(POLICY,'rb'))`) has the same exposure but is at least wrapped in try/except there.
criticFindings.impact: A single hand-edit typo in the base controller-policy.toml doesn't just break game launches (already filed) — it makes the entire MAD CONTROL PANEL non-functional: the first devices.scan errors, every Players/Priority/Systems/Backends page errors, and the user has no in-panel way to see or fix the cause. The panel is exactly where a non-technical user would go to recover.
criticFindings.fix: Guard the base load like routing.deep_merge's callers should: wrap `tomllib.load` in try/except (tomllib.TOMLDecodeError, OSError) and fall back to `{"systems": {}, "backends": {}}`, emitting a structured error event so the panel can surface 'controller-policy.toml has a syntax error' instead of failing every page.

criticFindings.title: Media-fetch scripts mkdir -p straight into a hardcoded SD-card path with no mount check — when the SD is unmounted they write media into the root/tmpfs and shadow the real card on remount
criticFindings.description: MEDIA/MED point at the literal `/run/media/deck/1tbDeck/downloaded_media/...` and the place/download paths do `(MEDIA/sub).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` then copy/write files, with no check that `/run/media/deck/1tbDeck` is actually a mountpoint. On the Steam Deck `~/ROMs` and `~/ES-DE/downloaded_media` are both symlinks to this SD card (verified live). SD cards on the Deck frequently fail to mount (after updates, reseating, FS errors). When unmounted, `/run/media/deck/1tbDeck` either doesn't exist or is an empty stub on the root/tmpfs; `mkdir -p` then happily creates the whole tree on the SMALL root filesystem and writes the (large, video-included) media there. ES-DE can't see it (it reads via the now-dangling symlink), the root partition silently fills, and when the SD is remounted the freshly-created stub directories become the mountpoint and shadow/orphan the on-card structure.
criticFindings.impact: Silent root-partition fill (videos are tens of MB each) plus user-invisible media loss/duplication when an SD card is temporarily unmounted during a scrape. The user (non-technical) sees 'media fetched OK' yet ES-DE shows nothing, and the root FS quietly runs out of space.
criticFindings.fix: Before any mkdir/write, assert the SD root is a real mountpoint (`os.path.ismount('/run/media/deck/1tbDeck')`) or that the parent `downloaded_media` already exists as a directory ON the mounted card; abort with a clear message otherwise. Better: derive the media root from ES-DE's MediaDirectory setting / the `~/ES-DE/downloaded_media` symlink target and verify it resolves to a mounted path, instead of hardcoding the volume name.

criticFindings.title: ES-DE wrapper re-extraction has no disk-space/partial-extract guard; on a full ~/Applications it falls back to FUSE-mount, silently re-introducing the documented Steam-game deadlock
criticFindings.description: The wrapper re-extracts the AppDir when the source AppImage's mtime:size stamp changes: `rm -rf "$APPDIR" "$TMP"` then extract into TMP then `mv "$SRC" "$APPDIR"`, writing the stamp only on a successful mv. If extraction fails (disk full on /home, interrupted), it does `[ -x "$APPDIR/AppRun" ] || exec "$IMG" "$@"` — i.e. it execs the AppImage directly, which FUSE-mounts /tmp/.mount_ES-DE*. The whole reason this wrapper exists (per its own comment) is that a native Steam game launched from ES-DE deadlocks on that squashfuse /tmp mount. So a low-disk or interrupted extract silently degrades back into the exact deadlock condition the wrapper was built to prevent — with no warning to the user. There's also no integrity check that the extracted AppDir is complete before trusting it next launch (only `-x AppRun` is checked); a partial AppDir that happens to contain AppRun would run broken.
criticFindings.impact: On a full /home (common when backups/cores accumulate) or a power-loss mid-extract, ES-DE silently reverts to the FUSE path and Steam games launched from it can hang forever (request_wait_answer deadlock). Hard for a non-technical user to diagnose since ES-DE itself still starts.
criticFindings.fix: Before extracting, check free space on ~/Applications against the AppImage size; if the fallback `exec "$IMG"` path is taken, log a visible warning (it's the deadlock-prone mode). Optionally validate the extracted AppDir (e.g. presence of usr/bin/es-de) before writing the stamp.

criticFindings.title: esde-health-check BUILD_ID gate cannot distinguish a fresh/never-configured Deck from an update-wiped one, and a successful manual restore that doesn't update the marker keeps nagging
criticFindings.description: The check skips when BUILD_ID equals the recorded .last-os-build, else runs deck-post-update.sh --check. The marker is only written (line 37) when NOTHING is missing. Two edge cases: (1) On a fresh install where .last-os-build is absent, `cat` returns empty, BUILD_ID != '' so the full check runs and (if anything is incomplete during first-run bootstrap) the user gets a 'SteamOS updated — run the restore' dialog even though no update happened — wrong framing for first-run. (2) If the user fixes things via the Desktop restore but that path does not write .last-os-build (deck-post-update.sh's full run does not write the marker — only esde-health-check.sh does, on a later all-present launch), the nag persists on every launch until a clean health-check run happens to record it. The marker/restore handshake is split across two scripts with no shared write, so a successful out-of-band fix isn't acknowledged until ES-DE is relaunched and re-checked.
criticFindings.impact: Mostly cosmetic but user-confusing: a first-run or a freshly-restored-but-not-yet-relaunched Deck shows a 'SteamOS update wiped your setup — run the restore' dialog that misdescribes the situation. Best-effort and never blocks launch, so low severity.
criticFindings.fix: Distinguish first-run (marker absent) from update (marker present but differs) and use a first-run-appropriate message; have deck-post-update.sh write .last-os-build on a fully-successful run so a completed restore clears the nag immediately.

criticGaps.notes:
WHAT I EXAMINED THIS WAVE (areas the prior two waves did not, or only shallowly):

SYSTEM PERSISTENCE / POST-UPDATE (focus area):
- deck-post-update.sh (full read): found the high-severity samba-setup invocation bug (line 129 calls it without sudo, but samba-setup.sh requires root and uses bare privileged commands -> silently never reinstalls Samba). The check_missing() AppImage logic (34-36) and the in-line `--check`/`--wrapper` modes are CORRECT (the wrapper at ~/Applications/ES-DE.AppImage is a bash script that references ES-DE-MAD, so the grep works). The rewrite_wrapper heredoc, mem_sleep tmpfiles, udev reinstall, input-group, and python-dep reinstall paths all look correct.
- samba-setup.sh: found the unbalanced steamos-readonly disable (no re-enable) — medium. Confirmed both siblings (install.sh, sinden-reinstall-deps.sh) DO balance it.
- install.sh (full read): idempotency looks sound — it never clobbers controller-policy.local.toml (127-136), backs up existing hooks (87), backs up a non-repo launchers dir to _TMP (64-67), guards with --dry-run consistently, re-enables readonly (151). No new finding.
- deck-fetch-esde.sh (full read): the `[ A ] || [ B ] && FORCE=1` at line 35 looked suspect but is correct for both arg/no-arg cases. Never-delete (backs up to _TMP+RECOVERY at 120-131), sha256 verify, size/ELF sanity, atomic mv — all solid. No finding.
- esde-health-check.sh + esde-splash-gen.sh (full read): BUILD_ID-gated nudge logic is correct; splash gen uses a fixed /tmp/_spgen.png (race only under concurrent ES-DE launches, which don't happen). Note: the health-check correctly detects missing Samba but the fix it points to (deck-post-update) can't repair it — see the high finding; this makes the nag never clear.

UDEV / SAMBA CONFIG:
- 99-sinden-lightgun.rules: reviewed. No keyboard-interface symlink exists (root cause of the sinden-mpx-setup event27 hardcode). `wide links = yes`/`allow insecure wide links = yes` in smb.conf is a known Samba security relaxation but is the user's deliberate choice to follow the ROMs symlink to the SD card on a LAN-only Deck — not flagging as it's intentional and documented in-file.
- smb.conf: shares /home/deck and /run/media/deck to `valid users = deck`, security=user, map to guest=never. Reasonable.

GITHUB BUILD WORKFLOW:
- .github/workflows/build-appimage.yml (full read): well-engineered — pins ubuntu-22.04 for glibc backcompat, concurrency cancel-in-progress:false to protect the publish, asserts the artifact exists (recipe uses bare `exit`), generates sha256 + latest_release.json, rolling tag. No bug found. One observation (NOT a finding): the job ALWAYS checks out deck-patches regardless of trigger branch, and `paths-ignore: '**.md'` only applies to push (not workflow_dispatch) — both intentional and documented in-file.

DATA FILES:
- data/gp-defaults/*.json (all 10 read): valid JSON, loaded by lib/madsrv/tester_cmds.py:234 and lib/mad_gamepad_tester.py:466 as baked sprite-position defaults merged under saved user positions. The near-identical dpadup/down/left/right coordinates in several files (classic, xbox360, etc.) look like a copy-paste artifact but are almost certainly a deliberate single 'dpad cluster' spot; cannot confirm a bug without the on-screen sprite render — NOT flagged. The auto-P2 token match including bare \"2\" (tester_cmds.py:277) is the already-confirmed [3.1]/[N6.2] bug; I verified it also fires in the gamepads.layout RPC path but that's the same code location already covered.
- data/sounds/*.wav: static assets, not reviewed for content.

UNDER-REVIEWED .py / .sh I READ AND BELIEVE CLEAN (no new finding):
- wii-nav-bridge.py (full): solid; RAW-fd stdin reassembly avoids the readline buffer trap, disarm/cooldown logic is careful, tester_claim() guards stale files via live pid. Minor: os.write(lock_fd) at 415 doesn't O_TRUNC, but the only consumer reads splitlines()[0] so leftover trailing bytes are harmless (verified).
- wii-monitor.py, wiimote-quit-watcher.py (full): co-read hidraw, read-only, graceful device add/remove. Clean.
- mad-backend.py (full): flock single-instance, PR_SET_PDEATHSIG, SIGTERM->KeyboardInterrupt->teardown invariant, stop_all_streams() in finally. The signal-in-finally edge is theoretical; worker-pool concurrency is already covered by confirmed [0.2]/[2.1].
- sinden-serial-preflight.py (full): non-atomic write_text at 116 BUT makes a one-time .pre-preflight backup (112-113), so recoverable — weaker than the confirmed no-backup writers; noting here rather than as a finding.
- sinden-install.sh, singe-indexer.sh, scrape-manuals.sh, dolphin-wii-mode.sh, sinden-mpx-setup.sh (except the event27 bug), sinden-start.sh, MAD.sh, switch-to-desktop.sh, supermodel-proton.sh (except the SIGKILL float gap): all read; backup-before-replace and Dolphin/emulator-closed guards present where needed.
- shellcheck (warning level) on samba-setup.sh, sinden-mpx-setup.sh, dolphin-wii-mode.sh, esde-splash-gen.sh, switch-to-desktop.sh, scrape-manuals.sh, deck-post-update.sh: NO warnings/errors.

NOT EXAMINED / A HUMAN SHOULD CHECK ON-DEVICE:
- The largest files (router-config-gui.py ~245KB, controller-router.py, lib/madsrv/* internals, lib/sinden_cfg.py, lib/*_cfg.py beyond what waves 1-2 covered) were only spot-checked here; waves 1-2 covered them more deeply.
- proton-launch.sh (parent dir, ~9.5KB, stock-EmuDeck-derived) and the per-emulator stock wrappers (cemu.sh has `rm -rf \"$savesPath/.gaming\"` at line 227 — STOCK EmuDeck code, out of MAD scope but worth a human eye if MAD ever forks it).
- deck-backup.sh / deck-restore.sh internals beyond the confirmed findings (6.2/6.6) — not re-read this wave.
- ON-DEVICE VERIFICATION NEEDED for the high finding: run `~/Emulation/tools/launchers/deck-post-update.sh` as user `deck` after a (simulated) Samba removal and confirm whether smbd actually comes back — I am confident from the code it does NOT, but I could not execute the privileged path safely (read-only review, no sudo).
- The gp-defaults dpad coordinate question needs a screenshot of the MAD gamepad tester to confirm it's a deliberate cluster vs a layout bug.
- mad-backend-protocol.md and deck-docs/ were not cross-checked against the RPC implementations for spec drift."

SCOPE: whole-system / environmental failure modes — SteamOS-update persistence + recovery, SD-card-unmounted behavior, first-run/missing-config bootstrap, partial-write recovery, daemon/router behavior on deleted policy or vanished device. Read in full: deck-post-update.sh, deck-backup.sh, deck-restore.sh, deck-fetch-esde.sh, install.sh, esde-health-check.sh, MAD.sh, samba-setup.sh, sinden-start.sh, quit-combo-watcher.py, controller-router.py, mad-backend.py, lib/{policy,localpolicy,routing(top),retroarch_cfg,esde_paths,esde_settings,es_systems,es_collections,devices(top+helpers),mad_config}.py, lib/madsrv/{device_cmds,policy_cmds}.py, wii-nav-bridge.py(header). Grepped all .py for SD/mount path usage.

NEW findings reported (not in the confirmed list): (1) deck-post-update.sh pacman without steamos-readonly disable [HIGH — verified the script has zero readonly-disable calls and pacman needs it on SteamOS]; (2) post-update calls samba-setup.sh without sudo while that script hard-exits unless root [HIGH — verified samba-setup.sh:16 EUID gate and post-update:129 no-sudo invocation]; (3) lib/policy.py load_merged() unguarded base-policy parse crashes EVERY panel page [MEDIUM — sandbox-confirmed TOMLDecodeError; distinct file/surface from confirmed router-path [0.1]/[12.4]]; (4) media-fetch scripts mkdir into hardcoded SD path with no mount check -> root-FS fill + shadowing when SD unmounted [MEDIUM]; (5) wrapper re-extract has no disk/partial guard, falls back to deadlock-prone FUSE silently [LOW]; (6) health-check BUILD_ID gate confuses first-run vs update and the marker handshake is split [LOW].

VERIFIED-AND-FINE (no bug): es_collections._canon uses os.path.realpath which is lexical for non-existent components, so collection matching stays correct with the SD unmounted (confirmed by reading the code + its own comment). routing.load_policy / policy.load_merged with a DELETED (absent) base file degrade to an empty {systems:{}} base (the .is_file() guard) — router then console-defaults RA systems but standalone routing no-ops gracefully; only a CORRUPT (not absent) base is the crash, which is the finding above. quit-combo-watcher and the device watch stream both handle a device disappearing mid-session correctly (re-enumerate, close gone fds). deck-fetch-esde.sh handles network/GitHub-unreachable with clean exit 1 and never deletes (moves to _TMP+RECOVERY). Confirmed [6.0] (esde_paths prefers /tmp/.mount which the extracted-AppDir wrapper never creates) — not re-reported but it is real and interacts with everything reading bundled es_systems.

NOT EXAMINED / human should check ON-DEVICE: (a) Does a real `pacman -S` actually fail on this Deck's freshly-updated read-only root (I could not safely run pacman) — confirm finding #1 by attempting deck-post-update.sh step 7 after an update, or just add the readonly wrap defensively. (b) Whether samba was ever actually restored by a past post-update run (check journalctl / smbd presence after the last update) — confirms finding #2's real-world impact. (c) Behavior of ES-DE + MAD when the 1tbDeck SD genuinely fails to mount at boot (dangling ~/ROMs and ~/ES-DE/downloaded_media): does the panel still open, do the media scripts get run in that state. (d) wii-nav-bridge.py / wii_slot_reader.py full bodies, wiimote-quit-watcher.py, wii-monitor.py — long-lived hidraw daemons I only skimmed for the device-disappearance angle; their pause/resume + tester-slot-claim file handshake under a backend crash deserves a dedicated read. (e) sinden-mpx-setup.sh / sinden-serial-preflight.py system-state effects on game-launch. deepenNotes.notes:

Threading: confirmed the design is sound — worker threads only enqueue onto self._ui_q (drained by main-thread _ui_pump at 60ms; line 906-924), and _scan/_poll/_on_devices_changed all run on the Tk main thread. GamepadNav._poll re-arms in a try/except TclError finally so a destroy() mid-tick stops it cleanly. report_callback_exception and sys.excepthook both route to _log_exc which also surfaces a footer flash (good).

Teardown verdict: the _clear() vs quit() divergence is real and matches the wave-1 theme [4.0]/[10.0]. Beyond the known ffmpeg orphan I checked every resource _clear releases that quit() skips: _it_devs (evdev fds), _xa_devs/_xat_edit_devs (EVIOCGRAB grabs), and _dp_proc (SDL subprocess). The evdev fds + grabs are NOT a leak past process exit (kernel auto-releases EVIOCGRAB on fd close at exit), so the only surviving resource on quit-during-a-tester-page is the Daphne SDL subprocess (reported, low — it self-terminates in ≤10s via --timeout 10). Minor unguarded-raise note (not elevated to a finding): quit() wraps _gp_cleanup in try/except but NOT _cam_restore_driver() (line 1874); if that raised, root.destroy() (1875) would be skipped and MAD would hang post-quit-timer — but its internals route through the guarded _run, so the practical risk is very low.

State init: _clear() and _on_devices_changed defensively use getattr(...,None) for every page-scoped attr, so the show_section(0) call inside __init__ (line 902) is safe before those attrs exist. _sec_prebuild correctly saves/restores _cv/_inner/_cv_win/_page_parent and focus and runs synchronously (no interleaving). The bind_class('Button','<space>',...) intentionally replaces tk.Button's invoke-on-space default and _global_key returns 'break' universally (keyboard never navigates) — consistent with the documented gamepad+mouse-only design. No new bug there. The module-load file handles (_fault_log, nav-trace) are intentionally process-lifetime.

NOT re-reported (already on wave-1 list): [4.0] quit orphans ffmpeg/dead Sinden from camera page; [4.1] gui_sound zombie children; [4.2] camera ffmpeg stderr fd leak.

Scope reviewed: router-config-gui.py lines 1550-3050 (lightgun/Sinden page, button-map + live-press feed, action picker, recoil/behavior page, smoother/LED, camera tuning page incl. ffmpeg preview, Preview routing page + threaded rescan/Wii poll), plus the cross-referenced _clear() teardown (1306-1389), _run() (1962-1981), and the Daphne capture mixin lib/mad_daphne_page.py (_dp_bind_press/_dp_bind_done) since 'Daphne capture' was in scope.

NEW findings: (1) the camera _cam_tick loop never polls ffmpeg, so a self-exited ffmpeg yields a frozen-but-'live' UI + a retained zombie until page-leave (medium; verified empirically with /tmp tests that a dropped-vs-retained Popen differs in auto-reap behavior, and that the truthiness guard stays True after child exit). (2) preview-start and slider steps run blocking v4l2-ctl/sinden-stop on the UI thread (low).

Things I checked and concluded are NOT new bugs / are mitigated:
- _run() (line 1962) spawns detached children and never waits on them, but I empirically confirmed (/tmp test) that because _run drops the Popen reference, CPython's subprocess._cleanup() reaps these on the next Popen() call — MAD makes Popens regularly, so _run children are transient zombies that self-clean. Not a meaningful leak. Its log fd is in a `with` block (closed in parent). start_new_session=True is correct for detached daemons.
- _sinden_pause_driver (1956) blocks the UI on sinden-stop.sh, but that script is fast (pkill + sleep 0.5 + backgrounded curl); the timeout=15 is just a worst-case guard. Folded into the low-severity finding rather than separately.
- The Daphne GUI-side capture (_dp_bind_press worker, mad_daphne_page.py:294-300) has the same kill-without-reap pattern as wave-1 [7.1] (which is in the DIFFERENT file lib/madsrv/daphne_cmds.py), BUT: the subprocess self-times-out at --timeout 10 while communicate() uses timeout=14, so the kill path rarely fires; and after _dp_bind_done sets self._dp_proc=None the old Popen is GC'd and reaped by _cleanup() on the next Popen. So this code path's leak is well-mitigated — I did not file it as a duplicate/peer of [7.1].
- The _dp_proc assignment race (Popen at 286 before self._dp_proc=proc at 288, vs _clear reading _dp_proc at 1350) can orphan a just-started capture if _clear runs in that ~microsecond window, but the subprocess self-terminates via --timeout 10 and _dp_gen invalidation handles result staleness. Negligible.
- _cam_tick cannot fire after _clear: _clear cancels _cam_after (1367-1373) before nulling _cam_lbl (1377), and _cam_preview runs entirely on the main thread (no worker race for _cam_proc). The camera single-opener pause/restore flag plumbing (_cam_driver_paused/_cam_driver_was_running) is consistently restored on every exit route (_clear 1374, quit 1874, save 2518). Wave-1 [4.0] already covers the MAD-quit-orphans-ffmpeg case.

SCOPE COVERED: Read all three policy files in full (controller-policy.toml 491 lines, controller-policy.local.toml 145 lines, controller-policy.example.toml 50 lines) and cross-checked against lib/policy.py (load_merged), lib/routing.py (deep_merge, resolve_system, resolve_policy, pin_items, resolve_pins, resolve_ports, xarcade_port, is_xarcade), lib/localpolicy.py (load/dump/_esc/_key/_val/_table), plus the independent merges in quit-combo-watcher.py:_read_quit_combo and lib/es_systems.py, and the GUI writers in lib/madsrv/policy_cmds.py, lib/madsrv/backends_cmds.py, lib/mad_backup.py (apply_slot_profile). KEY CONCLUSIONS (mostly NO new bug — confirming dormancy of latent findings):
1. [1.0]/[1.1] merge divergence is DORMANT on the live files (executed both merges -> identical) and under all GUI write paths (set_backend_template re-reads merged and writes the FULL level-3 dict). It only fires on a hand-edited PARTIAL level-3 table; reproduced both AGREE (GUI) and DISAGREE (hand-edit partial) cases. Reported as info/material-evidence, not a re-report.

Schema integrity is sound: slot_profiles use string keys throughout (TOML bare-int key 0 parses as "0"; writer mad_backup.py:83 writes str(slot); reader backends_cmds.py:178 indexes str(s)) — no str/int mismatch. The base [systems."3do"] (quoted) and local [systems.3do] (bare) are the SAME TOML key — merge/override works. A system/collection key containing a dot or control chars is correctly quoted+escaped by localpolicy._key/_esc and round-trips (stress-tested with the live splash value, vid:pid colon keys, and adversarial names). All three files parse with NO duplicate keys.

Value-type assumptions hold: hardware.xarcade_port='1.1' is a valid port_of() chain; gui.font_scale='xlarge' is in SCALE_PRESETS and resolve_scale also degrades safely on unknowns; gui flags are bool()/str()-coerced in mad_config.py. All inherits targets resolve (no undefined/cyclic). gbc/gb being console-with-no-ports is intentional (base comment lines 130-134).
4. localpolicy.dump round-trips the entire live local file cleanly (empty [backends.eden] header + [backends.eden.slot_profiles] child re-emits and re-parses fine; atomic temp+os.replace). The escaper's defensive comments reflect already-fixed past bugs.
5. install.sh:131-133 seeds controller-policy.local.toml from the example on a fresh install, so the example IS live at install time — its example-as-local merge is clean and identical across both merge implementations (verified). The example's reversed pcsx2 pad_classes order and explicit router_skip=false markers are harmless/intentional.

COULD NOT VERIFY: the ES-DE game-start hook that launches quit-combo-watcher with --system <name> is not in this repo tree (likely under ~/.config/ES-DE/scripts), so I confirmed the daphne/ps2/ps3 quit_combo entries are dead-or-redundant by reading the base policy's routing intent (daphne->hypseus own quit_cmd, router_skip=true) and the watcher merge logic, not by observing the hook's actual --system argument live.

CONCURRENCY CONCLUSION (the assignment's core question): The GUI<->backend protocol DOES allow concurrent in-flight RPC requests — wave-1 findings that depend on concurrency (e.g. [2.1] systems.set_ra_option unlocked read-modify-write of .cfg, [0.2]/[10.11] _ENUM_CACHE mutation, the lost-update class) ARE reachable. Evidence: (1) the C++ client MadBackend::request() (MadBackend.cpp:387-412) is fully async/fire-and-forget — it assigns a monotonic id, writes one NDJSON line, and returns without waiting; it never blocks for a response. (2) The protocol spec deck-docs/mad-backend-protocol.md:36-37 states explicitly 'Responses may arrive OUT OF ORDER (slow methods run on a worker pool); correlate by id.' (3) The backend's main loop (mad-backend.py:141 'for line in sys.stdin') reads requests serially, but rpc.dispatch (lib/madsrv/rpc.py:70-85) submits any method declared slow=True to a ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) (rpc.py:24), so up to 4 slow methods run truly concurrently with each other and with new inline fast methods. (4) I confirmed systems.set_ra_option IS slow=True (systems_cmds.py:242) and does an unlocked set_system_option read-modify-write (systems_cmds.py:255) — so two rapid toggle taps from GuiMadPageSystems, or a systems.get + set_ra_option overlap, can land on two pool workers concurrently => the [2.1] lost-update is real. IMPORTANT NUANCE that LIMITS some races: every local.toml writer (all policy.* methods and profiles.apply_slot) is DELIBERATELY slow=False so it runs on the single stdin thread (single-writer invariant), documented at protocol spec line 161 and confirmed — none of policy_cmds.py's methods carry slow=True. So lost-update races are confined to the slow-pool writers that touch SHARED files OTHER than local.toml: systems.set_ra_option (RA .cfg), the standalone emulator cfg writers if any were slow (they are reached via policy.* fast path, so safe inline), camera/sinden file writes, daphne buffer, backup ops. The full slow=True set: gamepads.list, tester.start, wii.probe_ext, priority.list, devices.sdl, devices.wiimotes, systems.get, systems.set_ra_option, daphne.load, daphne.bind, backup.snapshot/restore_router/mad_code, preview.route/all, sinden.status/driver/apply/buttons, camera.preview/save.

C++-SIDE REVIEW (MadBackend.cpp/.h, GuiMadPanel.cpp/.h, MadJson.h, GuiMadCaptureModal.cpp, MadPage.cpp, MadWiiBridge.cpp, the GuiMenu.cpp:2361 launch site, main.cpp:1390/1402, FileData.cpp:2205): the supervisor is carefully written and I found NO new C++ data race or use-after-free. Specifically verified as NOT bugs: (a) every spawn() caller (constructor, restart()->terminate()->shutdownChild, handleChildDeath()->shutdownChild) joins the prior reader thread before creating a new one (shutdownChild joins unconditionally on mReaderThread.joinable(), independent of mChildPid), so no std::thread::operator= on a joinable thread / std::terminate. (b) Reader thread touches only mQueue (under mQueueMutex) + atomics mDead/mReaderDone; UI thread owns everything else; mStdoutFd is closed only AFTER the join (line 488) so no read-after-close. (c) mNextId is monotonic, never reset across restarts, and the queue is cleared on shutdown, so a late response from a dead daemon cannot alias a new daemon's pending id. (d) Lifetime: GuiMadPanel has no explicit dtor but member declaration order puts mPageStack last so pages destruct before mBackend's terminate() — pages clear their stream callbacks first. Capture modal uses a shared_ptr alive-token on every callback and pops-before-callback in finish(). (e) The poll() timeout sweep (MadBackend.cpp:236-246) erases the pending entry before invoking the callback, and no response/timeout callback ever calls restart()/terminate()/mPending.clear() (those are input-thread only: RETRY button + B-press delete this), so the theoretical iterator-invalidation re-entrancy is not reachable. (f) MadWiiBridge::spawn() sets SIGPIPE=SIG_IGN process-globally and never restores it (games inherit SIG_IGN), but MadWiiBridge.cpp:48-50 documents this as intentional/benign — left as-is.

NOT-A-BUG observations worth recording: long-running slow methods exposed as STREAMS (sinden.install, backup.run_full, backup.sizes) return their {stream} token quickly so the 4000ms default request timeout never fires on them; the backup page sets explicit 60000/120000ms timeouts for the blocking snapshot/restore/mad_code calls. backup.restore is deliberately FAST (runs on the stdin thread) and would block the whole backend if it ever took >60s, but it only copies the tiny local.toml so this is not realistic. enqueue()'s event cap (MAX_QUEUED_EVENTS 256, MadBackend.cpp:202-209) can in principle drop a queued terminal stream push if 256+ events pile up between two polls, but poll() runs every frame and the capture modal self-polls when topmost, so this is not reachable in practice. The one NEW finding I am reporting is the FileData.cpp:2205-2207 runInBackground pause/resume contention.
